rationale: The Sensory Profile (Dunn) is a caregiver questionnaire that assesses a child's sensory processing patterns across multiple environments.

stem: A client with peripheral neuropathy in the feet has impaired protective sensation. What safety education should the OTA provide?
options: Walk barefoot to improve sensory input
Inspect feet daily, wear protective footwear, and test bath water temperature before entering
Use heating pads directly on the feet
Ignore numbness as it will resolve
rationale: Impaired protective sensation requires daily foot inspection, wearing protective footwear, and checking water temperature to prevent burns and unnoticed injuries.

stem: An OTA is working with a client post-stroke who demonstrates shoulder-hand syndrome. What is the FIRST priority?
options: Edema management, gentle ROM, and pain management
rationale: Shoulder-hand syndrome requires early edema management, gentle ROM, and pain management. Aggressive exercise can worsen the condition.

stem: A client with a lower extremity prosthesis reports phantom limb pain. Which approach may be helpful?
options: Mirror therapy and desensitization of the residual limb
rationale: Mirror therapy creates visual feedback that can reduce phantom limb pain. Desensitization of the residual limb also helps manage phantom sensations.

stem: An OTA is using the Cognitive Orientation to daily Occupational Performance (CO-OP) approach. What strategy does it use?
rationale: CO-OP uses the global cognitive strategy Goal-Plan-Do-Check where the client identifies a goal, creates a plan, performs the task, and checks the outcome.

stem: A client with dementia wanders frequently at night. Which environmental modification is MOST appropriate?
options: Lock all doors and windows with complex locks
Install bed alarms, night lights, and provide a consistent bedtime routine
Give the client caffeine before bed
rationale: Bed alarms alert caregivers to wandering, night lights reduce confusion and fall risk, and consistent bedtime routines promote sleep.

stem: An OTA is measuring edema in a client's hand using volumetry. How is this done?
options: Using a tape measure around the wrist
Submerging the hand in a volumeter and measuring displaced water
Weighing the hand on a scale
rationale: Volumetric measurement uses a standardized volumeter where the hand is submerged and displaced water is collected and measured.

stem: A client with scleroderma has tight skin on the hands limiting finger flexion. Which intervention is appropriate?
options: Sustained gentle stretching, paraffin, and custom splinting to maintain ROM
rationale: Scleroderma-related skin tightness responds to sustained gentle stretching, heat modalities (paraffin), and custom splinting to maintain achieved ROM.

stem: An OTA is facilitating a social skills group for adolescents with autism. Which activity structure is MOST effective?
options: Structured role-playing with visual supports and explicit social rule teaching
rationale: Structured role-playing with visual supports and explicit teaching of social rules is most effective for adolescents with ASD.

stem: A client recovering from a stroke cannot recognize familiar faces despite intact vision. Which condition is this?
rationale: Prosopagnosia is the inability to recognize familiar faces despite intact visual acuity, caused by damage to the fusiform face area.

stem: An OTA is using constraint-induced movement therapy. What is the minimum amount of affected arm use required?
options: 2 hours of repetitive task practice per day
6 hours of structured practice per day
rationale: Traditional CIMT requires 6 hours of structured practice per day with the affected arm and restraint of the unaffected arm for 90% of waking hours for 2 weeks.

stem: A client in acute care has a PICC line in the right upper extremity. What precaution must the OTA follow?
options: No blood pressure measurements or restrictive activities on that arm
Exercise the right arm vigorously to promote circulation
Ignore the PICC line during therapy
Apply heat to the PICC line site
rationale: A PICC line requires avoiding BP measurements, blood draws, and restrictive activities on that extremity.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with cognitive deficits to manage their schedule. Which external aid is MOST helpful?
options: Relying solely on verbal reminders from staff
Smartphone with alarm reminders and a visual daily schedule board
rationale: Smartphone alarms and visual schedule boards provide consistent structure and reminders, compensating for memory and organizational deficits.

stem: A client with an acute burn to the anterior neck should be positioned in what way?
rationale: Anterior neck burns tend to contract into flexion. Positioning in neck extension with no pillow counteracts the expected contracture pattern.

stem: An OTA is working with a premature infant in the NICU. Which sensory input should be minimized?
rationale: Premature infants in the NICU are sensitive to environmental stimuli. Bright lights and loud noise should be minimized to reduce physiological stress.

stem: A client with hip osteoarthritis needs to get in and out of a car. Which strategy reduces joint stress?
options: Back up to the car seat, sit down, then swing legs in
Step directly onto the car floor with one leg
rationale: Backing up to the seat, sitting down, then swinging the legs in minimizes hip flexion and rotation, reducing stress on the arthritic joint.

stem: An OTA is evaluating a client's kitchen for accessibility. Which counter height is optimal for wheelchair users?
rationale: Counter height of 28-34 inches accommodates wheelchair users, allowing them to reach work surfaces comfortably.

stem: A client with obsessive-compulsive disorder spends 3 hours on grooming rituals. Which OT approach is appropriate?
options: Allow unlimited time for rituals during therapy
Collaborate with the treatment team to gradually reduce ritual time while introducing meaningful activities
Tell the client to stop all rituals immediately
Focus only on rituals during OT sessions
rationale: OT works with the treatment team to gradually reduce ritual time and redirect the client toward meaningful occupations.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a custom splint. What material is commonly used for low-temperature thermoplastics?
options: Low-temperature thermoplastic sheet such as Orfit or Aquaplast
rationale: Low-temperature thermoplastics become moldable in 140-170 degree F water, allowing custom fabrication directly on the client.

stem: A client with early-stage ALS is referred for OT. What is the PRIMARY focus of intervention?
options: Maximize current function, provide adaptive equipment, and plan for progressive decline
Aggressive strengthening to prevent muscle wasting
Discharge from OT as no intervention is helpful
rationale: ALS is progressive. OT focuses on maximizing current function, providing adaptive equipment, and advance planning for progressive functional decline.

stem: An OTA notices a client's hand edema is increasing despite elevation. What additional technique should be used?
options: Heat application to the swollen area
rationale: When elevation alone is insufficient, compression wrapping and retrograde massage provide additional mechanical fluid displacement.

stem: A client with bilateral below-knee amputations needs to transfer from bed to wheelchair. Which transfer is safest?
options: Sliding board transfer using upper extremity strength
rationale: Bilateral BKA eliminates the ability to stand-pivot. A sliding board transfer uses upper extremity strength to safely bridge the gap between surfaces.

stem: An OTA is providing caregiver training for bathing assistance. Which technique should be taught FIRST?
options: Proper body mechanics, transfer techniques, and safety precautions during bathing
How to clean the bathroom after bathing
rationale: Caregiver training should prioritize proper body mechanics, safe transfer techniques, and safety precautions to protect both caregiver and client.

stem: A child with sensory processing disorder seeks out intense proprioceptive input. Which activity is MOST appropriate?
options: Heavy work activities: pushing, pulling, carrying weighted objects, and jumping
rationale: Proprioceptive seekers benefit from heavy work activities that provide deep pressure and joint compression.

stem: An OTA is assessing a client's driving readiness after a stroke. Which cognitive skill is MOST critical for safe driving?
options: Visual attention, processing speed, and divided attention
rationale: Safe driving requires visual attention, processing speed, and divided attention for monitoring mirrors, speed, and traffic simultaneously.

stem: A client with a total knee replacement is 3 days post-surgery. What ROM goal is typical at this stage?
options: 0 degree extension and 70-90 degree knee flexion
rationale: At 3 days post TKR, the typical goal is full extension (0 degrees) and 70-90 degrees of flexion, progressing gradually.

stem: An OTA is using a cognitive rehabilitation approach for a client with attention deficits. Which task is appropriate?
options: Progressively longer attention tasks such as tabletop activities with timed intervals
Having the client rest with eyes closed
rationale: Sustained attention improves with progressively longer tasks that require continuous attention, starting with short durations and gradually increasing.

stem: A client with upper extremity lymphedema after mastectomy should avoid which activity?
options: Blood pressure measurement, IV insertion, or constrictive jewelry on the affected arm
rationale: Clients with lymphedema must avoid blood pressure measurements, venipuncture, constrictive clothing, and heavy lifting on the affected side.

stem: An OTA is planning intervention for a child with handwriting difficulties. Which grasp pattern is considered functional but immature?
rationale: Static tripod grasp is functional but immature. The writing instrument is held with three fingers but movement comes from the wrist rather than finger movements.

stem: A client with COPD is learning to perform morning ADLs. Which technique is MOST important?
options: Rush through all tasks before fatigue sets in
Use pursed-lip breathing during exertion and sit for as many tasks as possible
Hold breath during activities for more power
rationale: Pursed-lip breathing during exertion prevents air trapping. Sitting during tasks reduces energy expenditure and oxygen demand.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for a client with visual field cut. Which strategy is compensatory?
options: Eye patching of the affected eye
Teaching the client to turn their head toward the affected side to scan the environment
rationale: Compensatory strategies for visual field cuts include turning the head toward the blind field to bring objects into the intact visual field.

stem: A client is recovering from a tendon transfer surgery in the hand. When can active motion typically begin?
rationale: After tendon transfer surgery, the hand is typically immobilized for 3-4 weeks to allow tissue healing, then active motion begins gradually.

stem: An OTA notices a client with hemiplegia develops shoulder subluxation. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Pull on the affected arm during transfers
Use a shoulder sling or arm trough during upright activities and proper positioning
Apply ice to the shoulder joint
rationale: Shoulder subluxation in hemiplegia is managed with supportive devices, proper positioning, and avoiding traction on the affected arm.

stem: Which developmental reflex should be integrated by 6 months of age?
rationale: The ATNR should be integrated by 6 months. Persistence beyond this age can interfere with bilateral hand use and midline activities.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has tremor during reaching tasks. Which technique may reduce the tremor?
options: Have the client move as quickly as possible
Add light weights to the wrist to dampen the tremor during functional tasks
rationale: Light wrist weights can dampen intention tremor during reaching tasks, improving accuracy and function.

stem: A client in a burn unit has a 30% TBSA burn. During the acute phase, which positioning principle is MOST important?
options: Position joints in the comfortable flexion position
Position joints opposite to the anticipated contracture (anti-deformity positioning)
Keep the client flat supine at all times
Allow the client to choose any comfortable position
rationale: Anti-deformity positioning places joints opposite to the expected contracture pattern, preventing scar contractures during healing.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with mild cognitive impairment in a memory clinic. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Memory strategy training for daily routines including spaced retrieval and calendar use
rationale: Memory strategy training helps clients with MCI maintain function in daily routines and compensate for memory deficits.

stem: A client with a proximal humeral fracture is in a sling. Which exercise is safe during the immobilization period?
rationale: During immobilization, pendulum exercises provide gentle shoulder motion without muscular effort. Distal AROM prevents stiffness.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has visual agnosia. What is the nature of this deficit?
options: Inability to recognize objects visually despite intact visual acuity
rationale: Visual agnosia is the inability to recognize objects by sight despite intact visual acuity. Recognition through touch or auditory cues may be preserved.

stem: A client with elbow stiffness after fracture has 30-110 degree ROM. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Progressive static or dynamic splinting with gentle sustained stretching
Vigorous passive stretching beyond pain tolerance
rationale: Progressive static or dynamic splinting provides low-load prolonged stretch, the most effective method to increase ROM in a stiff elbow.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who exhibits tactile defensiveness. Which approach is MOST therapeutic?
options: Surprise the client with unexpected touch
Use deep pressure touch before introducing light touch activities
Use only light fingertip touch throughout therapy
rationale: Deep pressure touch is calming and organizing for the nervous system. Starting with deep pressure before lighter touch helps desensitize the client.

stem: A client with a below-elbow amputation is learning to use a myoelectric prosthesis. Which signal controls the hand?
options: EMG signals from residual forearm muscles
rationale: Myoelectric prostheses use electromyographic signals from residual muscles to control opening and closing of the prosthetic hand.

stem: An OTA is conducting a task analysis of handwashing. Which step comes FIRST?
options: Approach the sink and turn on the faucet
rationale: Task analysis breaks activities into sequential steps. Handwashing begins with approaching the sink and turning on the faucet.

stem: A client with Parkinson's disease demonstrates micrographia. What does this term mean?
rationale: Micrographia is progressively smaller handwriting, characteristic of Parkinson's disease, resulting from basal ganglia dysfunction.

stem: An OTA is making a home visit for a client with low vision. Which environmental modification improves safety?
options: Increase lighting, add contrasting colors at stair edges, and remove clutter
Use dark-colored tape on dark surfaces
rationale: Increasing lighting, adding high-contrast color at transitions, and removing clutter improve visibility and reduce fall risk for clients with low vision.

stem: A client with a brain injury demonstrates confabulation. What does this mean?
options: The client fills memory gaps with fabricated information without awareness of inaccuracy
The client repeats the same word
rationale: Confabulation is the unintentional production of false memories to fill gaps. The client genuinely believes the fabricated information is true.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has difficulty with in-hand manipulation. Which activity BEST addresses this?
options: Moving coins from palm to fingertips and rotating a pencil to erase
rationale: In-hand manipulation includes translation, shift, and rotation. Coin and pencil activities directly address these fine motor skills.

stem: A client on anticoagulant therapy is receiving OT. Which precaution is MOST important?
options: Avoid activities with high risk of cuts, bruises, or falls due to increased bleeding risk
Apply hot packs to all joints
rationale: Anticoagulant therapy increases bleeding risk. The OTA should avoid sharp tools, high fall-risk activities, and aggressive manual techniques.

stem: An OTA is using the COPM to establish treatment goals. What makes this tool unique?
options: It provides objective muscle strength measurements
It captures the client's self-perceived occupational performance and satisfaction
rationale: The COPM is entirely client-centered, capturing self-perceived performance and satisfaction across self-care, productivity, and leisure.

stem: A client is learning one-handed shoe tying technique. Which shoes might be recommended as an alternative?
options: Traditional lace-up shoes with double knots
Elastic shoelaces or slip-on shoes with Velcro closures
rationale: Elastic shoelaces convert lace-up shoes to slip-ons, and Velcro closures eliminate the need for tying, promoting independence.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with frozen shoulder in the frozen stage. Which intervention is appropriate?
options: Gentle ROM within pain tolerance, joint mobilization, and heat before exercise
No intervention until the thawing stage
rationale: In the frozen stage, gentle ROM within pain tolerance and prior heat application are appropriate. Aggressive manipulation risks fracture.

stem: A 4-year-old child cannot copy a circle. Is this a concern?
options: Yes, circle copying typically develops by age 3
No, children cannot copy circles until age 6
Yes, but only if the child can write their name
No, this is expected for a 2-year-old
rationale: Children typically copy a circle by age 3. A 4-year-old unable to copy a circle may have fine motor or visual-motor integration delays.

stem: An OTA is recommending bathroom equipment for a client after hip replacement. Which items are essential?
options: Raised toilet seat, grab bars, and long-handled bath sponge
Bathtub with high sides and no rails
rationale: After hip replacement, a raised toilet seat reduces hip flexion, grab bars provide safe support, and a long-handled sponge eliminates excessive bending.

stem: A client demonstrates the ability to use the affected hand as a stabilizer during bimanual tasks after stroke. Which Brunnstrom stage does this represent?
rationale: Stage 5 shows movement outside of synergy patterns with increasing control, allowing functional use of the affected hand as a stabilizer.

stem: An OTA is planning a meal preparation session for a client with cognitive deficits. Which recipe complexity is most appropriate initially?
options: A simple cold meal with 3-5 steps
A complex baking recipe with precise measurements
A recipe requiring multiple simultaneous cooking processes
rationale: Starting with simple cold meals reduces cognitive demands and eliminates stove/oven safety concerns, providing a baseline for grading complexity.

stem: A client with multiple sclerosis has an exacerbation with increased fatigue. How should the OTA modify treatment?
options: Push through fatigue to maintain strength
Shorten sessions, use energy conservation, and prioritize the most important tasks
rationale: During MS exacerbations, therapy should be modified with shorter sessions, energy conservation, and task prioritization.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with difficulty in topographical orientation. What does this mean?
options: The client cannot navigate familiar routes or find locations in familiar environments
The client has difficulty with reading
rationale: Topographical disorientation is the inability to navigate familiar routes, often resulting from right hemisphere or parietal lobe damage.

stem: A client with CRPS Type I of the hand has edema, pain, and vasomotor changes. Which stage is this?
rationale: Stage 1 (acute) of CRPS presents with pain, edema, warmth, and vasomotor instability, typically within 0-3 months of onset.

stem: An OTA is providing community mobility training for a client with visual impairment. Which strategy is MOST important?
options: Have the client stay home exclusively
Teach systematic scanning, use of landmarks, and safe street-crossing techniques
Only allow the client to go out with a sighted guide
Use a motorized wheelchair for all mobility
rationale: Community mobility training includes systematic scanning, landmark identification, safe street crossing, and orientation and mobility aids.

stem: A client is being discharged from inpatient rehabilitation. Which assessment helps determine supervision level at home?
options: Kohlman Evaluation of Living Skills (KELS) or functional independence assessment
rationale: The KELS or similar functional assessments evaluate a client's ability to perform daily living tasks independently.

stem: An OTA is leading a cooking group for clients with TBI. A client cannot follow recipe steps. Which compensation technique should be used?
options: Provide a visual step-by-step checklist with pictures and have the client check off each step
Have another client do the cooking
rationale: Visual step-by-step checklists with pictures provide external structure for sequencing, reducing cognitive demand.

stem: A client with total shoulder arthroplasty is 2 weeks post-surgery. Which motion should be avoided?
options: Active internal rotation and heavy lifting with the operated arm
Gentle passive forward flexion within protocol
rationale: After total shoulder arthroplasty, active internal rotation and heavy lifting are typically restricted for 6-12 weeks.

stem: An OTA is assessing a child's visual motor integration. Which standardized test is commonly used?
options: Beery-Buktenica Developmental Test of Visual-Motor Integration (VMI)
rationale: The Beery VMI is the gold standard for assessing visual-motor integration in children.

stem: A client with bilateral hand arthritis needs to open medication bottles. Which adaptive strategy is best?
options: Request non-child-proof caps and use a rubber jar opener for added grip
Ask someone else to open all bottles permanently
rationale: Non-child-proof caps are easier to open, and rubber jar openers provide additional grip without excessive joint stress.

stem: An OTA is working on UE weight bearing with a client post-stroke. Which position is appropriate?
options: Weight bearing on an open hand with fingers extended on a stable surface
Hanging the affected arm by the side
Placing weight on a closed fist
rationale: Weight bearing through an open hand promotes extensor tone, inhibits flexor spasticity, and provides proprioceptive input for shoulder stability.

stem: A client with a confirmed DVT in the lower extremity is hospitalized. Which OT intervention is SAFE?
options: Upper extremity exercises and seated ADL tasks
rationale: With active DVT, lower extremity exercise and massage are contraindicated. Upper extremity exercises and seated ADL tasks are safe.

stem: An OTA is treating a child with spina bifida at the L4 level. Which functional outcome is expected?
options: Community ambulation with AFOs and forearm crutches
rationale: L4 spina bifida preserves quadriceps function but lacks ankle strength. Community ambulation with AFOs and forearm crutches is realistic.

stem: A client post-stroke demonstrates constructional apraxia. How does this manifest?
options: Difficulty copying designs, constructing block patterns, or drawing figures
rationale: Constructional apraxia is the inability to copy designs or arrange blocks in patterns, often following right parietal lobe damage.

stem: An OTA is implementing a falls prevention program in a SNF. Which intervention has the STRONGEST evidence?
options: Multifactorial intervention including exercise, environmental modification, and medication review
rationale: Multifactorial falls prevention has the strongest evidence in SNF settings.

stem: A client with hemiplegia tends to lean to the affected side. Which intervention addresses this?
options: Allow the client to continue leaning
Provide trunk support and engage in weight-shifting activities toward midline
Only position the client in bed
rationale: Trunk support and active weight-shifting exercises help the client develop awareness of vertical alignment.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who uses an insulin pump. What should the OTA be aware of during therapy?
options: Remove the insulin pump during all activities
Ensure the pump site is not disrupted and be aware of hypoglycemia signs
Do not provide OT to clients with insulin pumps
rationale: The OTA should protect the insulin pump site and monitor for signs of hypoglycemia during therapy sessions.

stem: A 3-year-old child is unable to stack more than 3 blocks. What should the OTA document?
options: Below age expectation; a 3-year-old should stack 9-10 blocks
Block stacking is not a milestone
rationale: By age 3, children typically stack 9-10 blocks. Stacking only 3 blocks is below expectations and may indicate delays.

stem: An OTA is providing scapular stabilization exercises for a client with shoulder impingement. Which exercise is appropriate?
options: Scapular setting, wall push-ups, and prone scapular retraction exercises
rationale: Scapular stabilization begins with low-level exercises to improve scapulohumeral rhythm and reduce impingement.

stem: A client with a TBI at Rancho Level IV demonstrates what behavior?
options: Confused and agitated behavior with decreased ability to process information
rationale: Rancho Level IV is characterized by confused-agitated behavior with severely impaired attention and information processing.

stem: An OTA is selecting a chair for a client with back pain. Which chair features are MOST important?
options: Adjustable height, lumbar support, armrests, and seat depth adjustment
A hard wooden chair without cushioning
rationale: Ergonomic chair features include adjustable height, lumbar support, armrests, and adjustable seat depth.

stem: A client with expressive aphasia becomes frustrated during OT. Which communication strategy should the OTA use?
options: Use yes/no questions, picture boards, and gestures; allow extra response time
rationale: Expressive aphasia impairs verbal output but preserves comprehension. Yes/no questions, picture boards, and extra response time reduce frustration.

stem: An OTA is working on grip strengthening. Which principle of exercise applies?
options: Overload principle: gradually increase resistance as strength improves
All clients should use maximum resistance from the start
rationale: The overload principle states that muscles strengthen when progressively challenged beyond their current capacity.

stem: A client with osteoporosis should avoid which type of movement during activities?
options: Trunk flexion with rotation under load
rationale: Trunk flexion with rotation under load places maximum compressive force on anterior vertebral bodies, increasing fracture risk.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client to use a button hook. Which condition MOST warrants this device?
options: Limited fine motor dexterity or one-handed function
rationale: A button hook is indicated when clients have limited fine motor dexterity, weakness, or one-handed function.

stem: A client with a Colles' fracture has the wrist in which deformity?
rationale: Colles' fracture causes dorsal displacement of the distal radius fragment, creating a dinner fork deformity when viewed from the side.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with an intellectual disability on a new work task. Which teaching approach is BEST?
options: Task analysis with forward chaining, demonstration, and repetitive practice
rationale: Task analysis with forward chaining teaches each step in order with demonstration and repetitive practice.

stem: A client with a lower motor neuron injury demonstrates which clinical sign?
rationale: Lower motor neuron injuries produce flaccid paralysis, muscle atrophy, fasciculations, and decreased reflexes.

stem: A client has a stage 2 pressure ulcer on the ischial tuberosity. Which seating modification is MOST important?
options: Pressure-relieving cushion and regular weight shift schedule
rationale: A pressure-relieving cushion distributes weight, and regular weight shifts further prevent pressure injury progression.

stem: An OTA is using a weighted vest with a child who has sensory modulation difficulties. What is the therapeutic purpose?
options: To provide deep pressure proprioceptive input for calming and organizing the nervous system
rationale: Weighted vests provide deep pressure proprioceptive input that helps calm and organize the nervous system.

stem: A client with bipolar disorder in a manic phase is referred for OT. Which activity is MOST appropriate?
options: Complex group project requiring sustained concentration
Structured, calming individual activity with clear boundaries
Unstructured creative expression without time limits
rationale: During mania, clients benefit from structured, calming activities with clear boundaries. Stimulating activities can escalate manic symptoms.

stem: An OTA measures two-point discrimination at 15mm on the fingertips. What does this indicate?
options: Diminished protective sensation requiring safety precautions
rationale: Normal two-point discrimination at the fingertips is 2-5mm. A measurement of 15mm indicates significantly diminished sensation.

stem: A client with dysphagia is referred for OT. Which positioning promotes safe swallowing?
options: Reclined at 45 degrees with neck extended
Upright at 90 degrees with slight chin tuck
rationale: Upright positioning at 90 degrees with slight chin tuck closes the airway and opens the esophagus, reducing aspiration risk.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for attention deficits after TBI. Which environmental modification supports attention?
options: Reduce distractions: quiet room, minimal visual clutter, structured routine
rationale: Reducing noise, minimizing visual clutter, and establishing structured routines support focus for clients with attention deficits.

stem: A client with wrist extension contracture would benefit from which type of splint?
options: Dynamic or serial static wrist flexion splint
rationale: For wrist extension contracture, a splint that provides sustained wrist flexion force gradually increases flexion ROM.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has Erb's palsy (C5-C6). Which movement is affected?
options: Shoulder abduction, external rotation, elbow flexion, and forearm supination
rationale: Erb's palsy affects the upper trunk, resulting in loss of shoulder abduction, external rotation, elbow flexion, and forearm supination.

stem: A client in hospice care requests OT. What is the PRIMARY focus?
options: Comfort, quality of life, and meaningful occupation engagement
Discharge planning for return to work
rationale: OT in hospice focuses on comfort, quality of life, and engagement in meaningful occupations to maximize dignity and participation.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with tennis elbow. Which activity should be AVOIDED during the acute phase?
options: Ice application to the lateral epicondyle
rationale: Tennis elbow is aggravated by repetitive wrist extension against resistance. During the acute phase, this movement should be avoided.

stem: A client recovering from GBS is in the recovery phase. What is the key concern during exercise progression?
options: Avoid overwork weakness by progressing exercise gradually and monitoring fatigue
Do not begin exercise until full recovery
rationale: GBS recovery is vulnerable to overwork weakness. Exercise must be progressed gradually with careful monitoring.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a healed mallet finger. What exercise maintains DIP extension?
options: Active DIP extension exercises and nighttime extension splinting as prescribed
No exercises are needed after healing
rationale: After mallet finger healing, active DIP extension exercises maintain tendon integrity and ROM.

stem: A client with right homonymous hemianopsia needs compensatory strategies for community mobility. Which technique is essential?
options: Close the right eye during walking
Systematically turn the head to the right to scan the blind field
Walk only in a straight line
rationale: Systematic head turning to the right scans the blind field, increasing awareness of objects and obstacles.

stem: An OTA is training a client with cognitive deficits in money management. Which is the MOST basic skill to address first?
options: Identifying coin and bill denominations and making simple purchases
rationale: Money management training begins with the most basic skills: identifying denominations and making simple purchases.

stem: A client recovering from a stroke has difficulty with proprioception in the affected hand. Which assessment technique tests proprioception?
options: Moving the client's finger up or down with eyes closed and asking direction
rationale: Proprioception is tested by passively moving a joint with the client's eyes closed and asking them to identify the direction of movement.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a dynamic MCP extension splint for extensor tendon injury. What force does the splint apply?
options: Low-load sustained MCP extension force using rubber bands or springs
rationale: A dynamic MCP extension splint applies low-load sustained extension force using rubber bands or springs.

stem: A client with a hip fracture pinning is ordered partial weight bearing at 50%. How does the OTA help the client understand this?
options: Have the client guess the weight
Use a bathroom scale to practice placing approximately half body weight through the affected leg
Tell the client to avoid all weight
rationale: A bathroom scale provides objective feedback for practicing the prescribed weight-bearing percentage.

stem: An OTA is using the Allen Cognitive Level Screen. A client at Level 3 can do which type of activity?
options: Repetitive manual actions with familiar tangible cues
rationale: ACL 3 indicates the client can perform repetitive manual actions with tactile cues.

stem: A client with rotator cuff tendinopathy reports pain with reaching overhead. Which arc of motion typically causes pain?
rationale: The painful arc in rotator cuff tendinopathy occurs between 60-120 degrees of shoulder abduction.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who uses a hearing aid. Which accommodation is important during therapy?
options: Speak very loudly directly into the hearing aid
Face the client when speaking, reduce background noise, and ensure the hearing aid is functioning
Remove the hearing aid during therapy
Write all instructions in capital letters only
rationale: Facing the client allows lip reading, reducing background noise improves hearing aid effectiveness.

stem: A child with developmental delays is working on self-feeding. Which utensil adaptation facilitates independent scooping?
options: Curved-handled spoon with built-up grip and non-slip mat under the bowl
rationale: A curved-handled spoon compensates for limited wrist rotation, built-up grip accommodates weak grasp.

stem: An OTA is assessing muscle strength using manual muscle testing. A grade of 2/5 means what?
options: Full ROM against gravity and full resistance
Full ROM with gravity eliminated only
Full ROM against gravity but no resistance
rationale: MMT grade 2/5 (Poor) indicates movement through full ROM only with gravity eliminated.

stem: A client with a recent amputation is learning residual limb wrapping. Which wrapping pattern is correct?
options: Figure-eight pattern applying more pressure distally than proximally
rationale: Figure-eight wrapping with more pressure distally promotes proper limb shaping and edema control.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has ideomotor apraxia. What should the OTA expect?
options: The client cannot conceptualize the task at all
The client knows what to do but cannot execute the motor plan correctly on command
The client has weakness preventing movement
The client has no motor deficits
rationale: Ideomotor apraxia impairs the ability to execute a motor plan on command, though automatic performance may be intact.

stem: A client with a total knee replacement is using a CPM machine. What does CPM stand for?
rationale: CPM (Continuous Passive Motion) machine provides controlled repeating passive movement to the joint.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with conversion disorder. Which approach is appropriate?
options: Confronting the client about faking symptoms
Using a supportive non-judgmental approach with focus on functional rehabilitation
Discharging from OT as symptoms are not real
rationale: Conversion disorder produces real symptoms from psychological origins. A supportive functional approach is most effective.

stem: A client with carpal tunnel syndrome reports worse symptoms at night. Which intervention helps?
options: Sleep with wrist in maximum flexion
Wear a wrist splint in neutral position during sleep
Apply heat to the wrist before bed
rationale: Nighttime wrist splinting in neutral prevents wrist flexion during sleep which compresses the carpal tunnel.

stem: An OTA is working in early intervention with a 2-year-old with developmental delays. Which setting is MOST appropriate?
options: The child's natural environment such as home or daycare
rationale: IDEA Part C mandates early intervention services in the child's natural environment.

stem: A client with a wrist ganglion cyst reports pain during wrist extension. What should the OTA do?
options: Modify activities to reduce wrist extension stress and report to the supervising OT
Apply maximum pressure to the cyst
rationale: The OTA should modify activities and report symptoms to the supervising OT.

stem: An OTA is evaluating a client's home for universal design features. Which is an example of universal design?
options: Lever-style door handles that are usable by all people regardless of ability
rationale: Lever handles require less grip strength and dexterity than round knobs, embodying universal design.

stem: A client in acute psychiatric care demonstrates suicidal ideation. What is the OTA's FIRST responsibility?
options: Continue the therapy session as planned
Ensure patient safety and immediately report to the treatment team
Counsel the client about their feelings
Document at the end of the day
rationale: Suicidal ideation requires immediate safety action and reporting to the treatment team.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has essential tremor. Which adaptive strategy helps with self-feeding?
options: Use weighted utensils and a non-slip mat to stabilize dishes
rationale: Weighted utensils dampen tremor amplitude during self-feeding.

stem: A client with locked-in syndrome is referred for OT. Which communication method may be possible?
options: Eye blink or eye tracking communication systems
rationale: Locked-in syndrome preserves consciousness and vertical eye movement. Eye tracking technology enables communication.

stem: An OTA is providing bilateral coordination intervention after stroke. Which activity addresses this?
options: Bilateral activities such as folding towels, rolling dough, and opening containers
rationale: Bilateral coordination activities require both hands to work together in functional tasks.

stem: A client with cervical spine fusion at C5-C6 is 6 weeks post-surgery. Which precaution remains?
options: Avoid cervical flexion, extension, and rotation beyond prescribed limits
Full ROM exercises in all directions
rationale: Cervical fusion precautions limit flexion, extension, and rotation to protect the fusion site.

stem: An OTA is teaching proper body mechanics for lifting. Which technique is correct?
options: Bend at the waist with straight legs
Bend at the knees and hips, keep the load close to the body, and avoid twisting
Reach and lift with arms fully extended
rationale: Proper lifting includes bending at the knees and hips, keeping the load close, and maintaining a neutral spine.

stem: A child with Angelman syndrome is referred for OT. Which characteristic is typical?
options: Happy demeanor, movement and balance disorders, and limited speech
rationale: Angelman syndrome features a happy demeanor, ataxia, seizures, limited or no speech, and intellectual disability.

stem: An OTA is performing nerve gliding exercises after carpal tunnel release. What is the purpose?
options: To promote smooth gliding of the median nerve and prevent adhesions
To stretch the flexor tendons only
rationale: Nerve gliding exercises promote smooth excursion of the median nerve, preventing adhesion formation.

stem: A client with SCI at T1 should achieve which functional level?
options: Independence in all ADLs, manual wheelchair mobility, and potential for driving with hand controls
rationale: T1 SCI preserves full upper extremity function including hand intrinsics, enabling full independence.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has anxiety during therapy. Which technique is MOST appropriate?
options: Teach relaxation techniques and grade the activity to a manageable level
Increase the activity demands to distract from anxiety
rationale: Relaxation techniques help manage anxiety while grading activities maintains therapeutic engagement.

stem: A client needs a wheelchair cushion to prevent pressure ulcers. Which type provides the BEST pressure distribution?
options: Air-filled cushion or gel cushion for high-risk clients
rationale: Air-filled and gel cushions provide superior pressure distribution by conforming to body contours.

stem: An OTA notices an elbow flexion contracture after prolonged casting. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Low-load prolonged stretching with serial casting or dynamic splinting
rationale: Elbow flexion contractures respond best to low-load prolonged stretching methods.

stem: A client with MS asks about the OT role in their care. What should the OTA share?
options: OT helps maximize function, manage fatigue, adapt activities, and plan for potential changes
OT only provides splinting for MS
rationale: OT for MS focuses on maximizing function, energy conservation, adaptive equipment, and fatigue management.

stem: An OTA is using therapeutic activities to improve executive function after TBI. Which activity BEST addresses planning?
options: Planning and preparing a simple meal with multiple components
rationale: Meal planning requires executive functions: planning steps, organizing ingredients, sequencing tasks.

stem: A client with advanced Parkinson's demonstrates a mask-like facial expression. What is this called?
rationale: Hypomimia is the mask-like facial expression resulting from rigidity of facial muscles in Parkinson's disease.

stem: An OTA is fitting a resting hand splint. The MCP joints should be at approximately what degree of flexion?
rationale: In a resting hand splint, MCPs are positioned at 70-90 degrees flexion to maintain collateral ligament length.

stem: A client with diabetes has decreased hand sensation. Which safety precaution is MOST important in the kitchen?
options: Use oven mitts, check water temperature, and inspect hands after activities
rationale: Decreased sensation increases burn and injury risk. Oven mitts, temperature checks, and hand inspection are essential.

stem: An OTA is working on pencil control with a child. Which prewriting stroke is typically mastered FIRST?
rationale: Prewriting strokes develop in sequence: vertical line at 2 years, horizontal line at 2.5, circle at 3, cross at 4.

stem: A client with polyneuropathy has bilateral foot drop. Which device prevents foot dragging during gait?
rationale: An AFO maintains ankle dorsiflexion during swing phase, preventing foot drop and toe drag.

stem: An OTA is documenting a SOAP note. Which section contains the therapist's clinical judgment?
rationale: The Assessment section contains clinical interpretation including progress toward goals and treatment effectiveness.

stem: A client with a high-level cervical SCI uses a sip-and-puff wheelchair. At what level is this typically needed?
rationale: Sip-and-puff control is needed at C3-C4 or above where the client lacks sufficient upper extremity function.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who had a left cerebellectomy. Which deficit is MOST expected?
rationale: Cerebellar lesions cause ipsilateral coordination deficits. Left cerebellectomy produces left-sided ataxia.

stem: A client with a healing skin graft on the dorsum of the hand needs what type of splinting?
options: Static splint maintaining anti-deformity position to protect the graft
rationale: Skin grafts require static splinting in anti-deformity position to protect the graft while preventing contracture.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for chronic pain using a cognitive behavioral approach. Which strategy is included?
options: Activity pacing, cognitive restructuring of pain beliefs, and relaxation training
rationale: CBT for chronic pain includes activity pacing, cognitive restructuring, and relaxation training.

stem: A client with severe hand contractures cannot use standard utensils. Which adaptation allows independent eating?
options: Universal cuff with utensil attached to the palm and plate guard
rationale: A universal cuff straps the utensil to the palm, bypassing the need for grasp. A plate guard aids scooping.

stem: An OTA is working with a client recovering from encephalitis. Which cognitive domain is commonly affected?
rationale: Encephalitis commonly affects memory, attention, and processing speed.

stem: A client with a humeral shaft fracture is at risk for which nerve injury?
rationale: The radial nerve runs in the spiral groove of the humerus and is vulnerable to injury with humeral shaft fractures.

stem: An OTA is leading a stress management group. Which relaxation technique involves tensing and relaxing muscle groups?
rationale: PMR involves systematically tensing each muscle group then relaxing, reducing overall muscle tension.

stem: A client with total hip replacement must avoid crossing their legs. Which precaution does this address?
rationale: Crossing legs causes hip adduction past midline, which risks posterior hip dislocation after posterior approach THR.

stem: An OTA is using vibration as a facilitation technique for hypotonic muscles. Where should vibration be applied?
options: On skin away from the muscle
rationale: Vibration applied to the muscle belly stimulates muscle spindles, facilitating contraction.

stem: A client with late-stage Alzheimer's at Allen Cognitive Level 2 can be expected to do what?
options: Postural actions and gross motor movements in response to proprioceptive cues
rationale: ACL 2 indicates the client can perform postural actions in response to proprioceptive cues but requires maximum assistance.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with a flexor tendon laceration in Zone II. Why is this zone significant?
options: It is the easiest zone to repair
Both FDS and FDP tendons share a tight sheath making repair complex
Only the FDP tendon is present
No tendons pass through this zone
rationale: Zone II contains both FDS and FDP tendons within a tight fibrous sheath. Adhesion risk is high.

stem: A client with late-stage Parkinson's demonstrates freezing of gait at doorways. Which cue helps?
options: Stepping over a visual target line on the floor or using a laser cane
rationale: Visual cues such as floor lines or laser canes help override the basal ganglia deficit.

stem: An OTA is modifying a workstation for carpal tunnel prevention. Which modification is correct?
options: Wrist on a sharp desk edge during typing
Keyboard at elbow height with wrist in neutral and mouse close to body
rationale: Ergonomic setup: keyboard at elbow height maintains neutral wrist position, preventing repetitive strain.

stem: A client with a transfemoral amputation is learning prosthetic balance. Which exercise is appropriate?
options: Weight shifting and single-leg balance on the prosthesis
rationale: Balance training begins with weight shifting and progresses to single-leg stance before ambulation.

stem: An OTA is working with a scaphoid fracture client. Why is the scaphoid bone clinically significant?
options: It is a long bone of the forearm
It is a carpal bone prone to avascular necrosis due to retrograde blood supply
It is a bone in the finger
It is a bone in the elbow
rationale: The scaphoid has retrograde blood supply, making it prone to avascular necrosis after fracture.

stem: A client recovering from stroke has letters drifting upward across the page when writing. Which deficit might cause this?
options: Spatial relations deficit affecting line orientation
rationale: Letter drift suggests spatial relations deficits, commonly seen after right hemisphere stroke.

stem: An OTA is working with a painful hemiplegic shoulder. Which approach is CONTRAINDICATED?
rationale: Overhead pulleys are contraindicated for hemiplegic shoulders as they can cause impingement and subluxation.

stem: A client with generalized anxiety has difficulty sleeping. Which OT intervention addresses this?
options: Sleep hygiene education including consistent schedule, avoiding screens, and relaxation routine
rationale: Sleep hygiene includes consistent sleep/wake times, avoiding screens and caffeine, and relaxation routines.

stem: An OTA is treating a Bennett's fracture. Where is this fracture located?
options: Base of the first metacarpal at the thumb
Proximal phalanx of the index finger
rationale: Bennett's fracture is at the base of the first metacarpal (thumb CMC joint).

stem: A client with TBI at Rancho Level VI demonstrates what behavior?
options: Goal-directed behavior with cueing and recognizing familiar people
rationale: Rancho Level VI shows goal-directed behavior with external cueing and recognition of familiar people.

stem: An OTA is teaching wheelchair maintenance to a client. Which component should be checked regularly?
options: Tire pressure, wheel alignment, brake function, and seat upholstery condition
rationale: Regular maintenance includes checking tire pressure, wheel alignment, brake function, and upholstery.

stem: A client with a digital nerve laceration lost fingertip sensation. Which test monitors nerve recovery?
options: Semmes-Weinstein monofilament testing for light touch threshold
rationale: Semmes-Weinstein monofilaments measure light touch threshold, tracking nerve recovery.

stem: An OTA is providing feeding training for a client with dysphagia. Which food texture is typically recommended?
options: Pureed or mechanically altered food as determined by the speech therapist
rationale: Food texture modification reduces choking and aspiration risk. The speech therapist determines appropriate consistency.

stem: A client with hand osteoarthritis asks about joint protection for writing. Which adaptation is MOST appropriate?
options: Built-up pen grip or ergonomic pen to reduce finger joint stress
Writing for extended periods without breaks
rationale: Built-up pen grips reduce grip force needed for writing, decreasing stress on arthritic joints.

stem: An OTA observes a client using a walker on stairs. Going DOWN stairs, what is the correct sequence?
options: Walker goes down first, then affected leg, then unaffected leg
Walker cannot be used on stairs
rationale: Going down: walker first, then affected leg, then unaffected leg.

stem: A client with C6 SCI wants to write. Which adaptation allows this?
options: Pen inserted into universal cuff or tenodesis-action writing splint
rationale: C6 SCI preserves wrist extension but lacks hand grasp. A universal cuff or tenodesis splint enables writing.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with numbness in the ulnar two digits. Which nerve is likely affected?
rationale: The ulnar nerve provides sensation to the ring (ulnar half) and little fingers.

stem: A client with intellectual disability mastered sandwich making. How should the OTA progress the task?
options: Introduce a slightly more complex task such as using a microwave
rationale: Progressive task complexity moves from cold preparation to simple heating, then to stovetop cooking.

stem: An OTA is treating epicondylalgia using eccentric exercise. Which movement is performed?
options: Slow controlled wrist extension lowering a weight through the eccentric phase
rationale: Eccentric exercise involves slowly lowering a weight to promote tendon healing and remodeling.

stem: A client in an ICU is on a ventilator. Can OT be provided?
options: No, OT is never appropriate for ventilator patients
Yes, OT can address positioning, passive ROM, sensory stimulation, and communication
Only if the patient is conscious
OT should wait until ICU discharge
rationale: OT in the ICU includes positioning, ROM, sensory stimulation, and communication supports.

stem: An OTA is training a client to use voice-activated home technology. When is this MOST appropriate?
options: For clients with full physical function
For clients with significant physical limitations needing hands-free environmental control
rationale: Voice-activated technology enables clients with physical limitations to control their environment hands-free.

stem: A client receiving chemotherapy reports severe fatigue. Which OT approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Energy conservation, activity modification, and fatigue management education
rationale: Cancer-related fatigue is managed through energy conservation, activity modification, and fatigue education.

stem: An OTA notices a splint is too tight causing skin blanching. What should the OTA do FIRST?
options: Leave the splint on and document
Remove the splint immediately, assess the skin, and report for adjustment
Tell the client to wear it less often
rationale: Skin blanching indicates excessive pressure. Immediate removal prevents skin breakdown.

stem: A client with post-polio syndrome experiences renewed weakness. What should the OTA prioritize?
options: Energy conservation, pacing, and avoiding overuse of affected muscles
rationale: Post-polio syndrome requires energy conservation and pacing. Overuse accelerates decline.

stem: An OTA is making a thumb CMC stabilization splint for arthritis. What is the purpose?
options: To stabilize the thumb CMC joint during pinch and grasp, reducing pain
To stretch the thumb web space
rationale: A thumb CMC stabilization splint supports the arthritic joint during functional activities.

stem: A client with SCI asks about sexual function. What is the OTA's role?
options: Provide information about positioning, adaptive equipment, and energy conservation within OT scope
rationale: Sexual function is an ADL within OT scope. The OTA can address positioning and adaptive equipment.

stem: An OTA is working with a healing extensor tendon repair in zone III. Which position should the PIP be in?
options: Full extension to protect the central slip repair
rationale: Zone III repair involves the central slip. The PIP must be maintained in full extension.

stem: A client is using a long-handled bath sponge. Which limitation does this device address?
options: Limited reach due to hip precautions, back pain, or reduced shoulder ROM
rationale: Long-handled bath sponges extend reach compensating for limited bending or shoulder ROM.

stem: An OTA is evaluating functional reach in an older adult. Reach less than 6 inches indicates what?
options: Substantial fall risk and limited functional mobility
rationale: Functional Reach Test: less than 6 inches indicates substantial fall risk.

stem: A client with hemiplegia is learning to manage a zipper one-handed. Which adaptation helps?
options: Zipper pull ring attached to the tab with the bottom stabilized by a pin or hook
Eliminating zippers and using only buttons
rationale: A zipper pull ring enlarges the tab, and stabilizing the bottom allows one-handed operation.

stem: An OTA is using the OTPF-4. Which domain includes sleep and rest?
rationale: The OTPF-4 classifies rest and sleep as one of the nine occupation categories.

stem: A client with carpal tunnel has a positive Phalen's test. How is this test performed?
options: Sustained wrist flexion for 60 seconds producing symptoms
rationale: Phalen's test involves sustained wrist flexion for 60 seconds. Symptom reproduction is positive.

stem: An OTA is planning a leisure activity for psychiatric clients. Which activity builds social skills?
options: Cooperative board game requiring turn-taking and communication
rationale: Cooperative board games require turn-taking, communication, and social reciprocity.

stem: A client with cervical radiculopathy at C7 has weakness in which muscle group?
rationale: C7 radiculopathy affects the triceps and wrist flexors with diminished triceps reflex.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for a child with spastic quadriplegic CP. Which positioning device supports alignment?
options: Custom seating system with lateral trunk supports and headrest
rationale: Custom seating systems provide symmetrical alignment through lateral supports, headrest, and hip positioning.

stem: A client performing a stand-pivot transfer becomes dizzy and pale. What should the OTA do FIRST?
options: Lower the client safely to the nearest surface and monitor vital signs
rationale: Dizziness and pallor may indicate orthostatic hypotension. Safely lower the client.

stem: An OTA is working with cubital tunnel syndrome. Which nerve is affected?
rationale: Cubital tunnel syndrome involves compression of the ulnar nerve at the elbow.

stem: A child with autism has a restricted diet eating only 3 foods. Which OT approach addresses this?
options: Sequential oral sensory approach with systematic food exposure
rationale: The SOS approach uses systematic, non-threatening exposure to new foods through a hierarchy.

stem: An OTA is splinting a client with a PIP volar plate injury. Which position should the PIP be in?
options: Slight flexion of 30 degrees with a dorsal blocking splint
rationale: Volar plate injuries benefit from a dorsal blocking splint holding the PIP in 30 degrees flexion.

stem: A client in a nursing home repeatedly removes their splint. What is the MOST appropriate response?
options: Assess the reason and modify the splint or wearing schedule accordingly
rationale: Investigating the reason allows the OTA to modify the splint or adjust the wearing schedule.

stem: An OTA is using mirror therapy for phantom limb pain. How does this technique work?
options: The client watches their phantom limb
The mirror reflects the intact limb creating a visual illusion to reduce pain
Mirrors are used only for grooming
The therapist uses a mirror to observe
rationale: Mirror therapy creates an illusion of the phantom limb moving normally, reducing pain.

stem: A client with total hip replacement is 4 weeks post-surgery with posterior approach. Can they drive?
options: Typically not until 6-8 weeks post-surgery with surgeon clearance
rationale: Driving after posterior approach THR is typically restricted for 6-8 weeks.

stem: An OTA notices skin maceration under a splint. What is the cause and solution?
options: Moisture retention; ensure the skin is dry and use breathable materials
rationale: Maceration results from trapped moisture. Solutions include drying the skin and ensuring ventilation.

stem: A child with Down syndrome has ligamentous laxity. Which precaution is important?
options: Screen for atlantoaxial instability and avoid activities stressing the cervical spine
rationale: Children with Down syndrome risk atlantoaxial instability. Screening and avoiding cervical stress are essential.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with severe muscle atrophy. Which strengthening progression is correct?
options: Isometric to active assisted to active to resistive progression
rationale: Strengthening progresses from isometric to active-assisted to active to resistive, matching muscle capacity.

stem: A client with TBI has difficulty sequencing morning routines. Which compensation strategy is BEST?
options: Visual checklist posted in the bathroom with pictures and numbered steps
Reducing the routine to one task
rationale: Visual checklists with pictures and numbered steps provide external structure for sequencing.

stem: An OTA is applying a prefabricated wrist splint. How should fit be assessed?
options: The splint should be as tight as possible
Check alignment, adequate support, no pressure points, and comfortable fit
The client should not be able to remove it
Fit assessment is not necessary for prefabricated splints
rationale: Proper fit includes checking alignment, support, absence of pressure points, and allowance for prescribed movement.

stem: A client with generalized weakness after prolonged ICU stay is referred for OT. What is the initial focus?
options: Gentle active-assisted ROM, bed mobility, and basic ADL retraining
rationale: ICU-acquired weakness requires gentle, progressive rehabilitation with careful monitoring.

stem: An OTA is teaching ergonomic principles to an office worker. Which monitor position is optimal?
options: Top of screen at or slightly below eye level at arm's length distance
To the side requiring trunk rotation
rationale: The monitor top should be at or slightly below eye level, approximately arm's length away.

stem: A client with median nerve compression cannot perform which pinch type effectively?
rationale: Median nerve compression affects thenar muscles, impairing tip pinch and opposition.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with a new colostomy. What is the OT focus?
options: Training in colostomy care as an ADL including adapted techniques for bag changes
rationale: Colostomy care is an ADL. The OTA trains the client in adapted techniques for independent bag changes.

stem: A 7-year-old child consistently reverses letters when writing. Should the OTA intervene?
options: No, reversals are normal at any age
Yes, persistent reversals after age 7-8 warrant intervention for visual-motor integration
rationale: Persistence beyond age 7-8 suggests visual-motor integration difficulties warranting intervention.

stem: An OTA is using the biomechanical frame of reference. Which problem is this approach BEST suited for?
options: Loss of ROM, strength, or endurance in a client with intact CNS
rationale: The biomechanical frame addresses ROM, strength, and endurance deficits in clients with intact nervous systems.

stem: A client with shoulder impingement should avoid which arm position during daily activities?
rationale: Overhead reaching with internal rotation maximizes subacromial impingement.

stem: An OTA is introducing a client to a powered wheelchair. What is the appropriate training progression?
options: Full speed in open space immediately
Start in a large clear space at low speed and practice turning and stopping first
Only practice in hallways with people
rationale: Training begins in a large clear space at low speed, practicing basic maneuvers before obstacles.

stem: A client with a metacarpal fracture is in a splint. Which fingers should remain free for exercise?
options: The uninvolved fingers should remain free for active movement
Only the thumb should be free
rationale: Uninvolved fingers should remain free to prevent stiffness and maintain tendon gliding.

stem: An OTA observes a child cannot maintain prone extension for more than a few seconds. Which system should be assessed?
options: Vestibular and proprioceptive systems and core muscle strength
rationale: Prone extension requires vestibular processing, proprioceptive awareness, and core strength.

stem: A client at TBI Rancho Level V demonstrates what type of behavior?
options: Confused-inappropriate with ability to follow simple commands but confusion
rationale: Rancho Level V shows the client can follow simple commands but demonstrates non-purposeful behavior.

stem: An OTA is treating a healing skin graft on the volar forearm. Which motion should be carefully monitored?
options: Forearm supination and wrist extension which stretch the volar surface
rationale: Volar forearm skin grafts are stretched by forearm supination and wrist extension.

stem: A client with a hip fracture is using a raised toilet seat. What height increase is typically needed?
options: 4-6 inches to keep hip flexion below 90 degrees
rationale: A 4-6 inch raised toilet seat keeps hip flexion below 90 degrees, maintaining hip precautions.

stem: An OTA is using rhythmic stabilization technique. What is the purpose?
options: To improve isometric co-contraction and joint stability
rationale: Rhythmic stabilization uses alternating isometric contractions to improve co-contraction and joint stability.

stem: A client with Raynaud's phenomenon has color changes in fingers with cold exposure. What should the OTA recommend?
options: Expose hands to cold water to build tolerance
Keep hands warm with gloves, avoid cold exposure and vibrating tools
rationale: Raynaud's phenomenon causes vasospasm with cold. Prevention includes keeping hands warm.

stem: An OTA is improving trunk rotation for functional tasks. Which activity promotes this?
options: Reaching across the body to retrieve objects on the opposite side
UE exercises with trunk fully stabilized
rationale: Cross-body reaching requires trunk rotation, promoting flexibility and functional movement.

stem: A client with a new above-knee prosthesis has skin breakdown at the stump-socket interface. What should be checked?
options: Socket fit, skin hygiene, liner condition, and wearing schedule
rationale: Skin breakdown requires checking socket fit, hygiene, liner condition, and gradual wearing schedule.

stem: An OTA is modifying a morning routine for a client with MS fatigue. Which strategy is MOST effective?
options: Complete all care standing at the sink
Lay out clothes the night before, sit for grooming, and use adaptive equipment
rationale: Preparing ahead reduces decision-making. Sitting conserves energy. Adaptive equipment reduces physical demands.

stem: A client with thumb UCL injury needs what type of splint?
options: Thumb spica splint immobilizing the MCP joint
rationale: Thumb UCL injury requires a thumb spica splint immobilizing the MCP joint for ligament healing.

stem: An OTA is using the Ayres Sensory Integration approach. Which environment is required?
options: Enriched sensory environment with suspended equipment and varied textures
rationale: ASI requires a specially equipped environment with suspended equipment, crash mats, and varied sensory opportunities.

stem: A client with a rotator cuff tear has a positive drop arm test. What does this indicate?
options: Supraspinatus tear with inability to maintain arm in abduction
rationale: A positive drop arm test indicates supraspinatus dysfunction.

stem: An OTA is addressing handwriting legibility in a school-aged child. Which factor should be assessed FIRST?
options: Pencil grasp, hand strength, and visual-motor integration
rationale: Handwriting legibility depends on pencil grasp, hand strength, and visual-motor integration.

stem: A client with myasthenia gravis shows ptosis worsening throughout the day. Why does this occur?
options: Fatigable weakness of the levator palpebrae due to acetylcholine receptor dysfunction
rationale: MG causes fatigable weakness due to autoimmune destruction of acetylcholine receptors.

stem: An OTA is creating an obstacle course to improve motor planning in a child. Which elements should be included?
options: Only walking in a straight line
Varied activities including crawling through tunnels, climbing, stepping over obstacles, and balancing
rationale: Motor planning requires novel, varied challenges across different movement demands.

stem: A client with diabetes and peripheral neuropathy has a painless foot ulcer. Why is this dangerous?
options: Painless ulcers can progress to deep infection and amputation if undetected
rationale: Peripheral neuropathy prevents pain detection. Painless ulcers can progress to serious infection.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has tardive dyskinesia from antipsychotic medication. Which movement is characteristic?
options: Involuntary repetitive movements of the face, tongue, and jaw
rationale: Tardive dyskinesia presents with involuntary repetitive movements of the face, tongue, and jaw.

stem: A client needs to apply cream to their back but cannot reach. Which assistive device helps?
rationale: A long-handled lotion applicator extends reach to the back for independent cream application.

stem: An OTA is assessing grip strength. How many trials should be performed on each hand?
options: Three trials on each hand using the average or best of three
rationale: Standard protocol uses three trials on each hand with the average or best of three recorded.

stem: A client with chronic kidney disease on dialysis has an AV fistula in the left arm. Which precaution applies?
options: No blood pressure measurement, venipuncture, or compression on the fistula arm
Apply tight bandages to that arm
Use that arm for all heavy transfers
rationale: The AV fistula arm must be protected from BP measurements, venipuncture, and compression.

stem: An OTA is working on executive function after frontal lobe injury. Which activity targets planning skills?
options: Planning a shopping trip by making a list, budgeting, and organizing the route
rationale: Shopping trip planning requires goal setting, list making, budgeting, and problem-solving.

stem: A client with a Boxer's fracture is in a splint. Which position is the MCP maintained in?
options: 70-90 degrees flexion in intrinsic plus position
rationale: Boxer's fracture splinting positions the MCP at 70-90 degrees flexion to maintain collateral ligament length.

stem: An OTA is treating chronic low back pain with a cognitive-behavioral approach. Which technique is included?
options: Activity pacing, pain coping strategies, and gradual return to valued activities
rationale: CBT for chronic pain includes activity pacing, coping strategies, and gradually returning to meaningful activities.

stem: A client with a below-elbow prosthesis is learning to dress. What technique should the OTA teach?
options: Remove prosthesis for all dressing tasks
Don the prosthetic arm sleeve first then pull over the sound arm
Dress the sound arm first always
Have a caregiver always dress the client
rationale: When dressing with a prosthesis, the prosthetic arm sleeve is donned first (affected first on).

stem: An OTA notices MCP hyperextension with IP flexion in a hemiplegic hand. Which deformity is this?
rationale: Intrinsic minus hand presents with MCP hyperextension and IP flexion due to loss of intrinsic muscle function.

stem: A client reports difficulty putting on earrings due to reduced dexterity. Which adaptation might help?
options: Switch to magnetic or clip-on earrings requiring less fine motor precision
Have someone else always put in earrings
rationale: Magnetic or clip-on earrings eliminate the need for precise fine motor manipulation.

stem: An OTA is treating CRPS Type I of the hand in the early stage. Which intervention is critical?
options: Early active motion, edema management, and stress loading
rationale: Early CRPS intervention includes active motion, edema management, stress loading, and desensitization.

stem: A client with C5 quadriplegia wants to operate a computer. Which input method is appropriate?
options: Mouth stick, voice recognition, or head-pointing system
rationale: C5 quadriplegia limits upper extremity function. Mouth sticks, voice recognition, and head-pointing enable computer access.

stem: An OTA is assessing a client's ability to manage their home independently. Which IADL is typically the LAST to decline in dementia?
options: Simple housekeeping tasks like folding laundry
rationale: Simple, overlearned tasks like folding laundry are preserved longer due to procedural memory.

stem: A client with wrist flexion contracture is fitted with a serial static splint. How often should it be remolded?
options: Every 1-2 weeks as ROM improves at the new end range
Only when the client requests it
rationale: Serial static splints are remolded every 1-2 weeks as tissue elongates.

stem: An OTA is using a hand-over-hand technique with a child. What does this involve?
options: The child watches the OTA perform the task
The OTA places hands over the child's hands and guides the movement
The OTA completes the task alone
The child performs without any assistance
rationale: Hand-over-hand involves guiding the child's movement through the activity for motor learning.

stem: A client recovering from burns has hypertrophic scars. Which intervention addresses scar management?
options: Pressure garments, silicone sheets, and scar massage
rationale: Hypertrophic scar management includes pressure garments, silicone sheets, and scar massage.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has an eating disorder and refuses the meal prep group. What should the OTA do?
options: Respect autonomy, discuss concerns, and collaborate with the team on alternatives
rationale: Respecting autonomy while exploring the refusal allows for therapeutic rapport and alternative approaches.

stem: A client with lateral epicondylitis uses a counterforce brace. Where is it positioned?
options: 1-2 inches distal to the lateral epicondyle over the extensor muscle mass
rationale: The counterforce brace is placed over the common extensor muscle mass to disperse force.

stem: An OTA is evaluating UE function post-stroke using the Fugl-Meyer Assessment. What does this tool measure?
options: Motor function, sensation, balance, and ROM
rationale: The Fugl-Meyer is a stroke-specific measure of motor function, sensation, balance, and ROM.

stem: A client with SCI needs pressure mapping for wheelchair seating. What does this assess?
options: Distribution of seating pressure to identify high-pressure areas
rationale: Pressure mapping displays interface pressure distribution to guide cushion selection.

stem: An OTA is providing handwriting intervention using Handwriting Without Tears curriculum. Which method does it use?
options: Multisensory approach with wood pieces, chalkboard, and structured letter sequences
Copying from a printed book only
rationale: Handwriting Without Tears uses a multisensory approach with wood pieces and structured sequences.

stem: A client with a TBI demonstrates difficulty with depth perception. Which functional limitation would this cause?
options: Difficulty reaching accurately for objects, navigating stairs, and pouring liquids
rationale: Impaired depth perception affects distance judgment, causing inaccurate reaching and stair difficulty.

stem: An OTA notices a wheelchair is too narrow for a client. What problems can this cause?
options: Pressure on hips, skin breakdown, and difficulty with transfers
The wheelchair will be too slow
The wheelchair will tip over backward
rationale: A narrow wheelchair creates excessive pressure on hips and greater trochanters.

stem: A client with L1 SCI should achieve which mobility goal?
options: Community ambulation with KAFOs and forearm crutches or wheelchair independence
Unable to use a manual wheelchair
rationale: L1 SCI preserves hip flexor function. KAFOs with crutches or wheelchair mobility are realistic goals.

stem: An OTA is using thermal modalities with a client who has peripheral vascular disease. Which precaution applies?
options: Apply heat directly to the affected area
Avoid heat application to areas with compromised circulation due to burn risk
rationale: PVD impairs heat dissipation, increasing burn risk in affected areas.

stem: A child with spastic hemiplegia walks with equinovarus foot position. What does this look like?
options: Foot plantarflexed and inverted, walking on outer edge
rationale: Equinovarus is plantarflexion with inversion, causing the child to walk on the outer edge of the foot.

stem: An OTA is treating lateral elbow tendinopathy. Which test reproduces symptoms?
options: Cozen's test with resisted wrist extension and elbow extended
rationale: Cozen's test involves resisted wrist extension with the elbow extended. Pain at the lateral epicondyle is positive.

stem: A client in a memory care unit puts clothes on over pajamas. Which deficit contributes to this?
options: Sequencing deficit and impaired problem-solving from dementia
rationale: Dressing over pajamas reflects impaired sequencing and problem-solving in dementia.

stem: An OTA is providing neuropathic pain intervention. Which modality may provide relief?
rationale: TENS modulates neuropathic pain through the gate control mechanism.

stem: A client with quadriplegia wants to paint as a leisure activity. Which assistive technology solution enables this?
options: Mouth painting, head-controlled systems, or adaptive painting devices
Art is not possible with quadriplegia
rationale: Clients with quadriplegia can paint using mouth-held brushes, head-controlled systems, or adaptive devices.

stem: An OTA is evaluating dynamic balance in an older adult using the Four Square Step Test. What does this measure?
options: Dynamic balance and ability to step quickly in multiple directions
rationale: The Four Square Step Test evaluates dynamic balance by measuring multidirectional stepping ability.

stem: A client is using a rocker knife for one-handed cutting. How does this device work?
options: It requires two hands to operate
A curved blade rocks back and forth to cut food using one hand
rationale: A rocker knife has a curved blade that rocks to cut food without requiring fork stabilization.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with a new rheumatoid arthritis diagnosis. Which period is characterized by joint inflammation?
options: Exacerbation or flare phase with active inflammation
rationale: During RA exacerbations, joints become inflamed, swollen, warm, and painful.

stem: A client with developmental disabilities is transitioning to supported employment. What is the OTA's role?
options: Finding the client a job independently
Job task analysis, work skill training, and workplace accommodation recommendations
No OT role in supported employment
rationale: OT in supported employment includes job task analysis, training, and accommodation recommendations.

stem: An OTA is using the NDT or Bobath approach with a stroke client. Which principle is central?
options: Inhibiting abnormal tone and facilitating normal movement patterns through handling
rationale: NDT/Bobath focuses on inhibiting abnormal tone and facilitating normal movements through handling.

stem: A client with scleroderma has tightening facial skin causing reduced oral opening. Which intervention helps?
options: Oral stretching exercises, oral motor activities, and skin care
rationale: Oral stretching exercises and oral motor activities help maintain oral aperture.

stem: An OTA is training a client to use a mobile arm support. When is this device indicated?
options: For clients with full UE strength
For clients with proximal weakness who cannot lift arms against gravity but have distal function
rationale: Mobile arm supports support the arm against gravity when proximal muscles are weak.
