stem: A client with a digital nerve repair at 3 weeks post-surgery should avoid which activity?
options: Aggressive gripping and pinching that places tension on the repair
Light active ROM of uninvolved fingers
rationale: At 3 weeks, nerve repairs are fragile. Aggressive gripping creates damaging tension.

stem: An OTA is evaluating hand function using the Nine-Hole Peg Test. What does this measure?
options: Finger dexterity and hand coordination speed
rationale: The Nine-Hole Peg Test measures finger dexterity speed.

stem: A client with bilateral above-elbow amputations needs to feed independently. Which system is appropriate?
options: Adaptive feeding equipment with universal cuff on prosthetic terminal device or mouth-held utensils
rationale: Bilateral above-elbow amputees can use prosthetic terminal devices with adaptive utensils.

stem: An OTA is using the CMOP-E model in practice. What is the central construct?
options: Spirituality as the core of the person
rationale: The CMOP-E places spirituality (essence of self, meaning, purpose) at the core.

stem: A client with hand burns is in rehabilitation. Which exercise maintains the first web space?
options: Thumb abduction and opposition exercises with web space stretching
rationale: Thumb abduction, opposition, and web space stretching maintain the first web space for functional grasp.

stem: An OTA is teaching spaced retrieval technique for memory deficits. How does this work?
options: Study information for hours without breaks
Recall information at progressively longer intervals to strengthen retention
rationale: Spaced retrieval involves recalling information at progressively longer intervals.

stem: A client with a new transtibial amputation is wrapping the residual limb. Which wrapping error should be corrected?
options: Applying more pressure proximally than distally creating a tourniquet effect
rationale: More proximal pressure creates a tourniquet effect. Wrapping should apply more pressure distally.

stem: An OTA is providing parent education about a sensory-seeking child. Which home modification supports the child?
options: Create a sensory corner with crash pad, therapy ball, and resistance activities
Restrict the child to only seated activities
rationale: A sensory corner with appropriate equipment provides outlets for sensory-seeking behavior.

stem: A client with advanced COPD needs to shower independently. Which equipment combination is essential?
options: Shower bench, hand-held shower head, and organized supplies within reach
rationale: A shower bench, hand-held shower head, and organized supplies minimize energy expenditure during bathing.

stem: An OTA notices a client's TheraPutty exercises are too easy with yellow putty. What is the next step?
options: Progress to red medium putty for increased resistance
rationale: TheraPutty progression follows a color sequence for gradual resistance increase.

stem: A hemiplegic client demonstrates associated reactions during walking. What are associated reactions?
options: Voluntary movements of the affected arm
Involuntary increase in flexor tone in the affected arm during effortful activities
rationale: Associated reactions are involuntary increases in tone during effortful activities.

stem: An OTA is modifying a kitchen for one-handed cooking. Which tool combination is essential?
options: One-handed cutting board with spikes, jar opener, and non-slip matting
rationale: One-handed kitchen adaptations eliminate the need for bilateral hand use.

stem: A client with severe hand arthritis cannot turn doorknobs. Which home modification addresses this?
options: Remove all doors from the home
Replace round doorknobs with lever-style handles
rationale: Lever-style handles require push-down motion rather than grip and turn.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has Wernicke's aphasia. What communication challenge does this create?
options: The client cannot produce any speech
The client produces fluent but meaningless speech and has difficulty understanding
The client can only communicate through writing
rationale: Wernicke's aphasia produces fluent but meaningless speech with poor auditory comprehension.

stem: A client in a burn unit needs ROM exercises. When is the BEST time relative to pain medication?
options: 30-60 minutes after pain medication when analgesic effect peaks
Pain medication timing is not relevant
Only when no medication is given
rationale: Scheduling ROM exercises at peak analgesic effect enables better participation and ROM.

stem: An OTA is evaluating visual perception using the MVPT. What makes this test unique?
options: It measures visual perception without requiring motor responses
rationale: The MVPT assesses visual perception without motor demands, isolating visual processing.

stem: A client with a healing FPL tendon laceration is in a dorsal blocking splint. What motion is restricted?
options: Thumb extension beyond neutral to protect the repair
rationale: A dorsal blocking splint prevents thumb extension beyond neutral, protecting the FPL repair.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has nystagmus. Which functional limitation is expected?
options: Difficulty with visual fixation, reading, and smooth pursuit eye movements
Difficulty with fine motor tasks only
rationale: Nystagmus impairs visual fixation, reading, and smooth pursuit, affecting visual tasks.

stem: A client with CMC joint arthritis of the thumb needs which activity modification?
options: Reduce pinch force by using larger handles, adaptive openers, and joint protection
Use only the affected thumb for all tasks
rationale: CMC arthritis is aggravated by pinch force. Larger handles and joint protection reduce stress.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with ALS to use an eye-gaze communication device. At which stage is this introduced?
options: At diagnosis before any symptoms appear
When speech and UE function decline enough to limit communication
Only after the client cannot breathe independently
Eye-gaze devices are not appropriate for ALS
rationale: Eye-gaze devices are introduced when speech and hand function decline sufficiently.

stem: A client with chronic lateral ankle instability needs which exercise type?
options: Proprioceptive and ankle strengthening exercises including balance board activities
rationale: Chronic ankle instability benefits from proprioceptive training and ankle strengthening.

stem: An OTA is working with a child with oppositional defiant disorder. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Positive behavioral supports, clear expectations, and choices within structured activities
Eliminating all structure from the session
Physical restraint when the child is defiant
rationale: Children with ODD respond best to positive supports, clear expectations, and structured activities.

stem: A client with Dupuytren's contracture has a PIP flexion contracture of 40 degrees. Which intervention is appropriate?
options: Extension splinting, soft tissue stretching, and post-surgical hand therapy if indicated
rationale: Dupuytren's management includes extension splinting, stretching, and surgical intervention when conservative fails.

stem: An OTA is working with a cardiac rehab Phase II client. What MET level activities are appropriate?
rationale: Phase II cardiac rehab introduces 3-5 MET activities with continuous monitoring.

stem: A client needs a dressing stick for lower body dressing. What limitation does this tool address?
options: Limited reach and ROM preventing clients from pulling garments over feet and legs
rationale: A dressing stick extends reach for pulling garments when ROM or bending is limited.

stem: An OTA is assessing executive function after TBI. Which task tests cognitive flexibility?
options: Trail Making Test Part B requiring alternating between numbers and letters
rationale: Trail Making Part B tests cognitive flexibility and set-shifting.

stem: A client with a below-elbow amputation wants to cook independently. Which terminal device is BEST for this?
options: Voluntary-opening hook or myoelectric hand that can grasp cooking utensils
No prosthesis and only adapted tools
rationale: A voluntary-opening hook or myoelectric hand provides grasp and release for cooking utensils.

stem: An OTA notices a child has difficulty with visual closure. Which intervention helps?
options: Only showing complete objects to the child
Graded activities from near-complete images to more incomplete images and hidden pictures
rationale: Visual closure training uses graded tasks from nearly complete to increasingly incomplete images.

stem: A client with TBI demonstrates impulsive behavior during activities. Which strategy helps manage this?
options: Speed up the task to finish faster
Teach stop-and-think strategies and provide structured environmental supports
rationale: Stop-and-think strategies and structured environments help manage impulsivity.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a resting hand splint. What angle of wrist extension is standard?
rationale: Standard resting hand splint positions the wrist in 20-30 degrees extension.

stem: A client with a fractured clavicle has a figure-8 brace. Which exercise can the OTA safely perform?
options: Elbow, wrist, and hand active ROM exercises
rationale: During clavicle healing, elbow, wrist, and hand exercises maintain distal function.

stem: An OTA is providing group therapy for clients with substance use disorders. Which activity promotes relapse prevention?
options: Discussing past substance use in graphic detail
Identifying triggers, developing coping strategies, and practicing stress management
Avoiding all discussion of substance use
rationale: Relapse prevention groups identify triggers, develop coping strategies, and build social support.

stem: A client with PIP stiffness has 20-60 degree ROM. What is the functional arc needed for most ADLs?
options: Approximately 30-70 degrees for most functional grasp
rationale: Most functional grasp activities require a PIP arc of approximately 30-70 degrees.

stem: An OTA is providing caregiver education about safe transfers. What is the MOST important principle?
options: Using proper body mechanics with a wide base of support, bending at knees, and keeping the person close
Using only arm strength for lifting
The caregiver should lean backward to counterbalance
rationale: Safe transfers require proper body mechanics: wide base, bend at knees, keep person close.

stem: A client with Parkinson's has difficulty performing walking and talking simultaneously. What does this indicate?
options: Impaired automatic motor processing requiring conscious attention for movement
rationale: In Parkinson's, automatic motor processing is impaired, making dual-task performance difficult.

stem: An OTA is measuring forearm supination ROM. What is the normal range?
rationale: Normal forearm supination ROM is approximately 80-90 degrees.

stem: A client with bilateral above-knee amputations is learning wheelchair mobility. What modification is needed to prevent tipping?
options: Set rear axle back to prevent backward tipping due to altered center of gravity
rationale: Bilateral AKA shifts center of gravity posteriorly. The rear axle must be set back.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has hemispatial neglect. Which assessment is commonly used?
options: Line bisection test and cancellation tests
rationale: Line bisection and cancellation tests are standard assessments for hemispatial neglect.

stem: A client with scaphoid fracture nonunion needs which precaution during hand therapy?
options: Careful avoidance of excessive stress to the scaphoid due to avascular necrosis risk
rationale: Scaphoid nonunion carries avascular necrosis risk. Excessive stress should be avoided.

stem: An OTA is providing toileting training for a child with cerebral palsy. Which equipment supports independence?
options: Adapted toilet seat with supports, grab bars, and footrest
No equipment modifications are available for children
rationale: Adapted toilet seats with supports and grab bars provide postural stability for children with CP.

stem: A client with patellar tendinopathy is performing eccentric exercises on a decline board. What is the purpose?
options: To provide controlled eccentric loading of the patellar tendon to promote healing
rationale: Eccentric decline squats preferentially load the patellar tendon to stimulate healing.

stem: An OTA is working with a Grade III AC joint separation. What activity level is appropriate during healing?
options: Rest in sling with gentle pendulum exercises and gradual ROM progression
rationale: Grade III AC separation requires sling, gentle pendulums, and gradual ROM progression.

stem: A child cannot copy a diamond shape but can copy a square and triangle. At what age does diamond copying typically develop?
rationale: Diamond copying typically develops at 6-7 years, requiring integration of two diagonal lines.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a dynamic IP extension orthosis for finger contracture. Which type provides continuous low-load stretch?
options: Rigid static splint with no give
Spring-loaded dynamic splint providing constant gentle force
No orthosis is effective for contractures
rationale: Dynamic splints with spring-loaded outriggers provide constant low-load stretch for contractures.

stem: A client with a recent myocardial infarction is in Phase I cardiac rehab. What is the maximum MET level for activities?
rationale: Phase I cardiac rehab limits activities to 1-2 METs including self-care tasks with supervision.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has difficulty with bilateral hand use during cutting. Which foundational skill should be assessed?
options: Hand dominance, bilateral integration, and midline crossing ability
rationale: Bilateral hand use for cutting requires established hand dominance, bilateral integration, and midline crossing.

stem: A client with Huntington's disease demonstrates chorea. What type of movement is this?
rationale: Chorea consists of rapid, involuntary, irregular jerky movements characteristic of Huntington's disease.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for a client with thoracic outlet syndrome. Which position should be avoided?
options: Sustained overhead arm positions and heavy carrying with arms at sides
rationale: Thoracic outlet syndrome is aggravated by sustained overhead positions and heavy carrying that compress the neurovascular bundle.

stem: A client in inpatient psychiatry is at risk for elopement. What safety measure should the OTA follow?
options: Maintain awareness of the client's location and follow unit protocols for elopement risk
Allow the client to leave if they choose
rationale: Elopement precautions require constant awareness of client location and adherence to unit protocols.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with de Quervain's tenosynovitis. Which test is positive?
options: Finkelstein's test with pain on ulnar deviation with thumb tucked in fist
rationale: Finkelstein's test involves making a fist over the thumb and ulnar deviating the wrist, reproducing pain.

stem: A client needs to manage medication but has impaired fine motor skills. Which adaptation helps?
options: Large compartment weekly pill organizer with easy-open lids
rationale: Large compartment pill organizers with easy-open lids reduce fine motor demands.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a right hemisphere stroke. Which cognitive-perceptual deficit is MOST common?
options: Left neglect, spatial-perceptual deficits, and impaired safety awareness
rationale: Right hemisphere strokes commonly produce left neglect, spatial-perceptual deficits, and poor safety awareness.

stem: A client performing IADL training cannot sequence the steps to do laundry. Which cognitive skill is impaired?
options: Executive function including sequencing, planning, and organization
rationale: Laundry requires sequencing, planning, and organization, which are executive function skills.

stem: An OTA is providing vestibular rehabilitation for a client with BPPV. Which exercise is commonly prescribed?
options: Epley maneuver or Brandt-Daroff habituation exercises
rationale: The Epley maneuver repositions otoconia. Brandt-Daroff exercises promote central habituation.

stem: A client with osteoarthritis of the knee needs a home exercise program. Which exercise is MOST appropriate?
options: Low-impact exercises such as stationary cycling, pool exercises, and gentle ROM
rationale: Low-impact exercises reduce joint stress while maintaining mobility and strength.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a healing rotator cuff repair at 12 weeks. What exercise is now appropriate?
options: Progressive resistive strengthening and functional activities
rationale: At 12 weeks post rotator cuff repair, progressive resistance and functional activities are appropriate.

stem: A client with MS demonstrates intention tremor during reaching. What does this indicate?
options: Cerebellar involvement affecting coordination during voluntary movement
rationale: Intention tremor increases with voluntary movement and indicates cerebellar involvement.

stem: An OTA is providing pre-driving assessment for a client with a visual field cut. Which skill is critical?
options: Compensatory head turning and scanning to the affected side
rationale: Clients with visual field cuts must demonstrate consistent compensatory scanning before driving.

stem: A client with wrist arthrodesis (fusion) has the wrist permanently fused. Which compensatory movement becomes important?
options: Forearm pronation and supination to position the hand for tasks
rationale: After wrist fusion, forearm rotation becomes the primary compensatory movement for hand positioning.

stem: An OTA is using Rood's approach for sensory stimulation. Quick icing is used to what?
options: Facilitate muscle contraction by stimulating sensory receptors
rationale: Quick icing is a Rood technique that facilitates muscle contraction through sensory receptor stimulation.

stem: A client with hemiplegia is unable to don socks one-handed. Which device helps?
options: Sock aid device that holds the sock open while the client slides the foot in
rationale: A sock aid holds the sock open on a plastic form, allowing foot insertion without bending or bilateral hand use.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with a cerebral AVM rupture. Which initial OT intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Gentle positioning, sensory stimulation, and passive ROM within medical clearance
rationale: Post-AVM rupture, initial OT is conservative with positioning, sensory stimulation, and gentle ROM.

stem: A client needs to open and close window blinds but cannot reach. Which environmental control solution helps?
options: Move to a home without windows
Motorized blinds with remote control or voice activation
Have a caregiver open and close blinds permanently
rationale: Motorized blinds with remote or voice control enable independent environmental management.

stem: An OTA is treating a child with fetal alcohol spectrum disorder. Which characteristic is commonly seen?
options: Difficulty with abstract reasoning, poor impulse control, and social skills deficits
rationale: FASD commonly presents with difficulty in abstract reasoning, impulse control, and social skills.

stem: A client with a proximal phalanx fracture is immobilized. Which joints should the OTA exercise?
options: Uninvolved MCP, DIP, and wrist joints to prevent stiffness
All joints including the fracture site
rationale: Exercising uninvolved joints prevents stiffness while protecting the healing fracture.

stem: An OTA is teaching a transfer to a client with paraplegia. Which type of transfer is MOST commonly used?
options: Sliding board or depression transfer using UE strength
rationale: Paraplegia preserves UE function, enabling sliding board or depression transfers.

stem: A client with a healing extensor pollicis longus repair has the thumb in which position?
options: Thumb extension and radial abduction with wrist in slight extension
rationale: EPL repair requires thumb extension and radial abduction to protect the repair while healing.

stem: An OTA is addressing functional cognition in a client with mild dementia. Which activity targets procedural memory?
options: Practicing familiar overlearned routines such as folding laundry
rationale: Procedural memory preserves familiar overlearned motor tasks in mild dementia.

stem: A client with peripheral vascular disease has claudication during walking. What does the OTA recommend?
options: Push through the pain during ambulation
Take rest breaks when claudication occurs and resume when pain subsides
rationale: Claudication management includes walking to the onset of pain, resting until it subsides, then resuming.

stem: An OTA is providing instruction on joint protection for a client with RA. Which principle is MOST important?
options: Use small joints for heavy tasks
Use the largest joints available for the task and distribute force across multiple joints
Grip objects as tightly as possible
rationale: Joint protection uses larger joints to distribute force, reducing stress on inflamed smaller joints.

stem: A client with severe spasticity in the elbow flexors cannot extend the arm for dressing. Which technique may help?
options: Slow prolonged stretch of the elbow flexors and weight bearing through the arm
rationale: Slow prolonged stretch and weight bearing inhibit spasticity, allowing increased ROM.

stem: An OTA is training a client with low vision to use a magnifying glass for reading. Which type is MOST practical?
options: Stand magnifier or lighted magnifier that provides hands-free use
rationale: Stand or lighted magnifiers provide hands-free use, reducing fatigue during sustained reading.

stem: A client with a distal biceps tendon rupture is 6 weeks post-surgical repair. Which exercise is appropriate?
options: Gentle active-assisted elbow flexion within protocol guidelines
rationale: At 6 weeks post distal biceps repair, gentle active-assisted flexion within protocol is appropriate.

stem: An OTA is designing a sensory diet for a child with sensory modulation disorder. What is a sensory diet?
options: An individualized schedule of sensory activities throughout the day to maintain optimal arousal
rationale: A sensory diet is a scheduled plan of activities providing appropriate sensory input throughout the day.

stem: A client with severe RA has MCP ulnar drift. Which splint may be used?
options: Ulnar drift splint or MCP alignment splint to maintain proper joint alignment
rationale: Ulnar drift splints maintain MCP joint alignment in neutral, countering the ulnar deviation force.

stem: An OTA is providing driving rehabilitation for a client with left hemiplegia. Which adaptation is necessary?
options: Left-foot gas pedal and spinner knob on steering wheel
rationale: Left hemiplegia may require a spinner knob for one-handed steering and left-foot gas pedal.

stem: A client with a healing nerve graft demonstrates Tinel's sign distally. What does this indicate?
options: Nerve regeneration progressing distally along the graft
rationale: A distally advancing Tinel's sign indicates nerve regeneration progressing along the repaired nerve.

stem: An OTA is using proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF). Which diagonal pattern involves shoulder flexion, adduction, and external rotation?
rationale: D1 flexion involves shoulder flexion, adduction, and external rotation.

stem: A client with a spinal cord injury is at risk for autonomic dysreflexia. At which level does this occur?
rationale: Autonomic dysreflexia occurs in SCI at T6 and above in response to noxious stimuli below the injury.

stem: An OTA is working with a teenager with juvenile rheumatoid arthritis. Which activity modification is important during flares?
options: Rest the inflamed joints while maintaining gentle ROM and using splints as needed
rationale: During JRA flares, inflamed joints need rest with gentle ROM to prevent contractures.

stem: A client with an upper motor neuron lesion demonstrates which clinical sign?
rationale: Upper motor neuron lesions produce spasticity, hyperreflexia, and clonus.

stem: An OTA is making a static progressive elbow extension splint. What mechanism does it use?
options: Non-elastic components with turnbuckle or static adjustment for progressive stretching
rationale: Static progressive splints use non-elastic components with adjustable settings for sustained stretch.

stem: A client with bilateral hand burns needs splinting at night. What position are the hands placed in?
options: Intrinsic plus position: wrist extension, MCP flexion, IP extension, thumb abduction
Hands flat with all joints at zero
rationale: The intrinsic plus or anti-deformity position prevents the contractures expected with hand burns.

stem: An OTA is implementing a fall prevention program for a community-dwelling older adult. Which intervention is MOST evidence-based?
options: Tai chi or multicomponent exercise program targeting balance and strength
rationale: Tai chi and multicomponent exercise programs have strong evidence for fall prevention.

stem: A client with a metacarpal neck fracture is in buddy taping. What is the purpose of buddy taping?
options: To provide stability by taping the injured finger to an adjacent finger while allowing controlled movement
rationale: Buddy taping provides stability while allowing controlled movement of the injured digit.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for a client with post-traumatic stress disorder. Which OT approach is appropriate?
options: Establishing safety, building coping skills, and gradually reintroducing meaningful occupations
rationale: OT for PTSD includes establishing safety, building coping skills, and reintroducing meaningful activities.

stem: A client with a severe hand crush injury requires which immediate OT intervention?
options: Edema management, protective positioning, and gentle active motion within medical parameters
rationale: Crush injuries need edema management, protective positioning, and careful early motion within medical clearance.

stem: An OTA is assessing a child's grasp development. At what age does the dynamic tripod grasp typically develop?
rationale: The dynamic tripod grasp typically develops between 4.5-6 years of age.

stem: A client with severe cardiac disease must limit activity to 2 METs. Which activity falls within this limit?
options: Seated grooming and light desk work
rationale: Seated grooming and light desk work are within the 1-2 MET range.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with complex shoulder surgery. Which precaution is universal for all shoulder surgeries?
options: Follow weight-bearing and ROM restrictions specific to the surgical procedure
rationale: All shoulder surgeries require adherence to specific ROM and weight-bearing restrictions.

stem: A client with chronic fatigue syndrome reports post-exertional malaise. What should the OTA teach?
options: Push through fatigue to build stamina
Activity pacing with energy envelope management to avoid triggering post-exertional malaise
rationale: CFS requires strict activity pacing within the energy envelope to prevent post-exertional malaise.

stem: An OTA is providing visual-motor integration intervention. Which activity addresses this?
options: Copying geometric designs and completing mazes and dot-to-dot activities
rationale: Copying designs, mazes, and dot-to-dot activities integrate visual perception with motor output.

stem: A client with a healing distal humerus fracture is cleared for gentle ROM. Which motion is addressed first?
options: Gentle active elbow flexion and extension within pain-free range
rationale: After distal humerus fracture healing, gentle active elbow flexion and extension begin first.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has Korsakoff syndrome from chronic alcohol use. Which cognitive deficit is PRIMARY?
rationale: Korsakoff syndrome primarily causes severe anterograde amnesia with confabulation.

stem: A client with left hemiplegia uses a one-handed typing technique. Which keyboard adaptation improves efficiency?
options: One-handed keyboard layout or sticky keys function for key combinations
rationale: One-handed keyboard layouts or sticky keys enable efficient one-handed typing.

stem: An OTA is providing gait training cues for a client with Parkinson's. Which auditory cue is effective?
options: Rhythmic metronome beat matching desired step cadence
rationale: Rhythmic auditory cues from a metronome provide external pacing for gait initiation.

stem: A client with bilateral hand weakness needs to open jars. Which device is MOST helpful?
options: Rubber jar opener or under-cabinet jar opener requiring minimal grip
Asking a caregiver to always open jars
rationale: Rubber or under-cabinet jar openers reduce grip force demands for jar opening.

stem: An OTA is addressing hypertonicity in a child with CP. Which handling technique reduces tone?
options: Quick stretch to the spastic muscles
Slow rhythmic rocking and deep pressure over the muscle belly
rationale: Slow rhythmic rocking and deep pressure are inhibitory techniques that reduce hypertonicity.

stem: A client discharged from inpatient rehab needs outpatient OT. Which assessment should be completed before discharge?
options: Comprehensive discharge assessment including functional status, home setup needs, and follow-up plan
rationale: Discharge assessment includes functional status, equipment needs, home setup, and outpatient follow-up plan.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with intersection syndrome of the forearm. Where is this condition located?
options: At the intersection of the first and second extensor compartments on the dorsal forearm
rationale: Intersection syndrome occurs where the first extensor compartment crosses the second on the dorsal forearm.

stem: A client with a lower extremity prosthesis is learning to navigate curbs. Which technique is correct for stepping UP a curb?
options: Lead with the intact leg, then bring the prosthetic leg up
rationale: Going UP: lead with the intact (stronger) leg, then follow with the prosthetic leg.

stem: An OTA is providing cognitive rehabilitation for attention deficits. Which task progression is correct?
options: Progress from sustained attention to selective to alternating to divided attention
rationale: Attention rehabilitation follows a hierarchy: sustained, selective, alternating, then divided attention.

stem: A client with a healing wrist fracture has stiff fingers after cast removal. Which intervention is the priority?
options: Tendon gliding exercises and active finger ROM to restore differential tendon excursion
rationale: Tendon gliding exercises and active finger ROM restore differential tendon excursion after immobilization.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has hoarding disorder. Which OT approach is appropriate?
options: Removing all possessions without the client's consent
Gradual decluttering with cognitive behavioral strategies and organizing skills training
rationale: Hoarding disorder responds to gradual, client-directed decluttering with CBT and organizing skill development.

stem: A client with bilateral above-elbow amputations is learning to use body-powered prostheses. Which control motion opens the terminal devices?
options: Biscapular abduction or glenohumeral flexion through a cable system
rationale: Body-powered prostheses use biscapular abduction or shoulder flexion transmitted through cables.

stem: An OTA is using joint mobilization as part of hand therapy. When is this technique indicated?
options: For joint stiffness with capsular restriction to improve passive ROM
rationale: Joint mobilization addresses capsular restriction to improve passive ROM in stiff joints.

stem: A client with right hemiplegia is learning to write with the left hand. Which training approach is BEST?
options: Start with large arm movements progressing to letter formation, then words
Write in normal-sized letters from the start
rationale: Non-dominant handwriting training begins with large arm movements, progresses to letter formation.

stem: An OTA is educating a client about osteoporosis prevention. Which activity is MOST beneficial?
options: Weight-bearing exercise such as walking and resistance training
rationale: Weight-bearing and resistance exercises stimulate bone formation and slow bone loss.

stem: A client with a healing PIP joint fracture-dislocation is in a dorsal blocking splint. Why is this position used?
options: To prevent re-dislocation by blocking full extension while allowing protected flexion
rationale: The dorsal block prevents extension that could cause re-dislocation while allowing progressive flexion.

stem: An OTA is training a client to use a standing frame. What is the therapeutic benefit?
options: Promote weight bearing, prevent osteoporosis, improve bowel function, and reduce spasticity
rationale: Standing frames provide weight bearing, bone loading, improved bowel function, and spasticity reduction.

stem: A client with a pacemaker is receiving OT. Which precaution applies to modalities?
options: Avoid electrical modalities near the pacemaker including TENS and electrical stimulation
Apply TENS directly over the pacemaker
rationale: Electrical modalities can interfere with pacemaker function and are contraindicated near the device.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with Williams syndrome. Which characteristic is typical?
options: Overly friendly personality, intellectual disability, and cardiovascular problems
rationale: Williams syndrome features overly friendly personality, intellectual disability, elfin facial features.

stem: A client with a healed finger extensor tendon repair demonstrates an extensor lag. What does this mean?
options: The finger lacks full active extension despite having full passive extension
rationale: An extensor lag means full passive extension is possible but active extension falls short.

stem: An OTA is training a client with SCI to perform pressure relief in a wheelchair. How often should weight shifts be performed?
rationale: Pressure relief weight shifts should be performed every 15-30 minutes for 30-60 seconds.

stem: A client with a recent THR (posterior approach) wants to sit in a recliner. Which precaution applies?
options: Ensure the seat height allows hip flexion less than 90 degrees and use a firm elevated cushion
Sit in the lowest chair available
rationale: The recliner must allow hip flexion less than 90 degrees. A firm elevated cushion may be needed.

stem: An OTA is treating a child with torticollis. Which intervention stretches the affected SCM muscle?
options: Turning the head toward the affected side
Gently stretching by turning the head away from the affected side and lateral flexion toward opposite side
Keeping the head in midline always
No stretching is indicated for torticollis
rationale: Torticollis stretching involves turning the head away from the affected side to stretch the shortened SCM.

stem: A client with a healing wrist ORIF is ready to start scar management. When should scar massage begin?
options: After wound closure is complete and sutures are removed, typically 2-3 weeks post-surgery
Scar massage is not appropriate after ORIF
rationale: Scar massage begins after wound closure, typically 2-3 weeks post-surgery.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with severe depression who refuses to get out of bed. Which approach is MOST therapeutic?
options: Force the client out of bed
Start with bedside activities and gradually increase engagement and out-of-bed participation
rationale: Grading engagement from bedside activities to progressive mobilization respects the client's current state.

stem: A client with a trapeziectomy (CMC arthroplasty) is 8 weeks post-surgery. Which exercise is appropriate?
options: Light pinch and grip activities with progressive resistance
rationale: At 8 weeks post trapeziectomy, light pinch and grip with progressive resistance is appropriate.

stem: An OTA is adapting a computer for a client with low vision. Which modification is MOST helpful?
options: Screen magnification software, high contrast display, and large font settings
rationale: Screen magnification, high contrast, and large font improve computer accessibility for low vision.

stem: A client with SCI performs an independent transfer and scrapes their arm without feeling it. What does this highlight?
options: The importance of skin inspection after all transfers due to absent protective sensation
rationale: Absent protective sensation requires routine skin inspection after all activities to prevent injury.

stem: An OTA is working with a client with brachial plexus injury affecting C5-C6. Which muscles are weakened?
options: Deltoid, biceps, and rotator cuff muscles
rationale: C5-C6 brachial plexus injury weakens the deltoid, biceps, and rotator cuff.

stem: A client with chronic schizophrenia in a community mental health setting benefits from which OT program?
options: Life skills training including budgeting, cooking, community navigation, and social skills
rationale: Community-based OT for chronic schizophrenia focuses on life skills for independent living.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with a healing DIP joint arthrodesis (fusion). What should the client understand?
options: The DIP joint will be permanently fixed and the client must compensate with increased PIP and MCP motion
The surgery failed if the joint does not bend
A second surgery will restore motion
rationale: DIP arthrodesis permanently fuses the joint. Compensation occurs through increased use of PIP and MCP joints.

stem: A client with visual field neglect consistently misses items on the left during dressing. Which technique addresses this?
options: Remove all items from the left side permanently
Teach scanning techniques starting from left, use colored tape as anchoring cue on the left side
Place all items on the right permanently
rationale: Anchoring cues on the neglected side and systematic scanning training improve awareness.

stem: An OTA is providing ergonomic recommendations for a keyboard user with wrist pain. Which position is correct?
options: Wrists resting on the desk edge during typing
Wrists in neutral floating position with forearms supported and keyboard at elbow height
rationale: Neutral wrist position with forearm support prevents repetitive strain and carpal tunnel syndrome.

stem: A client with a brain tumor affecting the occipital lobe is MOST likely to have which deficit?
options: Visual processing deficits including cortical blindness
rationale: The occipital lobe processes visual information. Tumors here cause visual field deficits or cortical blindness.

stem: An OTA is working with a client recovering from a snake bite to the hand with resulting compartment syndrome. What is the OT priority?
options: Edema management, ROM preservation, and monitoring for Volkmann's contracture
rationale: Compartment syndrome risks Volkmann's contracture. OT focuses on edema, ROM, and contracture prevention.

stem: A client with autism spectrum disorder becomes overwhelmed in a noisy clinic. Which environmental modification helps?
options: Provide a quiet treatment space with reduced sensory stimulation and use noise-canceling headphones
rationale: Reducing environmental sensory input helps prevent sensory overload in clients with ASD.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with frozen shoulder in the thawing stage. Which intervention is appropriate?
options: Progressive stretching, joint mobilization, and functional activity retraining
rationale: The thawing stage allows progressive stretching, mobilization, and return to functional activities.

stem: A client with tetraplegia at C7 has what functional ability?
options: Wrist extension and some finger function allowing modified independence in most ADLs
rationale: C7 tetraplegia preserves triceps and wrist extensors with some finger control, enabling significant independence.

stem: An OTA is using contract-relax PNF technique. What happens during the relax phase?
options: The therapist passively moves the limb into the new range gained from the isometric contraction
The client contracts the same muscle again
rationale: After isometric contraction and relaxation, the therapist passively moves into the new available range.

stem: A client with age-related macular degeneration has which visual deficit?
options: Central vision loss affecting reading, facial recognition, and detailed tasks
rationale: AMD causes central vision loss, affecting reading, facial recognition, and detailed work.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who had a tendon transfer for radial nerve palsy. Which transfer is common?
options: Pronator teres to ECRB for wrist extension restoration
rationale: PT to ECRB transfer restores wrist extension in radial nerve palsy.

stem: A client with a total elbow replacement has which activity restriction?
options: Lifetime 5-10 pound lifting restriction with the affected arm
Can lift unlimited weight after 3 months
rationale: Total elbow replacements carry a lifetime lifting restriction, typically 5-10 pounds.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has difficulty with body awareness. Which activity improves proprioceptive awareness?
options: Heavy work activities such as wheelbarrow walking, wall push-ups, and carrying weighted objects
Listening to music with eyes closed
rationale: Heavy work activities provide intense proprioceptive input that improves body awareness.

stem: A client with osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures should avoid which exercise?
options: Trunk flexion exercises such as sit-ups and toe touches
rationale: Trunk flexion increases anterior vertebral body compression, risking further fracture.

stem: An OTA is addressing bilateral coordination in a preschool child. Which activity is MOST appropriate?
options: Catching and throwing a large ball with two hands
rationale: Catching and throwing a large ball requires bilateral upper extremity coordination.

stem: A client with a healing ulnar collateral ligament of the elbow is wearing a hinged elbow brace. What does this brace allow?
options: Controlled flexion and extension within safe ROM limits while blocking valgus stress
rationale: A hinged elbow brace allows controlled flexion-extension while protecting against valgus stress.

stem: An OTA is providing leisure exploration for a client with quadriplegia. Which activity is feasible?
options: Adaptive photography using mouth stick, voice control, or switch access
rationale: Adaptive photography uses assistive technology to enable creative leisure participation.

stem: A client with chronic pain reports fear of movement. What is this called?
rationale: Kinesiophobia is an excessive fear of movement often seen in chronic pain conditions.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with a healing proximal row carpectomy. What was removed?
options: The scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum bones from the proximal carpal row
rationale: Proximal row carpectomy removes the scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum to address wrist arthritis.

stem: A client in a group home needs to learn basic cooking safety. Which concept should be taught FIRST?
options: Fire safety, handling hot items with protection, and never leaving cooking unattended
rationale: Cooking safety begins with fire prevention, safe handling of hot items, and supervision requirements.

stem: An OTA is completing documentation for a client being discharged from OT. Which element is essential in the discharge summary?
options: Functional status at discharge compared to initial evaluation, goals met, and recommendations for continued care
Only the number of sessions attended
Only the therapist's opinion of the client
rationale: A discharge summary includes functional status changes, goal achievement, and recommendations for ongoing care.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with lateral epicondylitis. Which activity modification should be recommended?
options: Use tools with built-up handles and avoid repetitive wrist extension against resistance
Perform aggressive stretching of wrist extensors under heavy load
Apply heat before performing the aggravating activity without modifying technique
rationale: Lateral epicondylitis involves inflammation of wrist extensor tendons at the lateral epicondyle. Built-up handles reduce grip force, and avoiding repetitive wrist extension decreases stress on the affected tendons.

stem: A client with a T12 SCI wants to dress the lower body independently. Which technique should the OTA train?
options: Standing to pull pants up over hips
Supine or long-sitting lower body dressing using momentum and rolling techniques
Sitting at edge of bed with legs dangling unsupported
Kneeling position for lower body dressing
rationale: T12 SCI clients have intact upper body function but lack lower extremity control. Supine or long-sitting dressing with log rolling and momentum allows independent lower body dressing without standing.

stem: An OTA notices a client with diabetes has a small open wound on the dorsum of the hand that the client did not notice. What should the OTA do?
options: Ignore it since it is small
Alert the supervising OT and nursing staff immediately due to impaired sensation and healing risk
Apply essential oils to the wound
Tell the client to monitor it at home
rationale: Clients with diabetes often have impaired sensation and delayed wound healing. Even small wounds carry infection risk and must be reported to the supervising OT and nursing for proper wound care.

stem: A client with early-stage multiple sclerosis reports fatigue that limits daily activities. Which OT strategy is MOST appropriate?
options: Encourage the client to push through fatigue to build endurance
Teach energy conservation and work simplification techniques
Recommend the client stop all activities
rationale: MS-related fatigue is a primary symptom not relieved by pushing through. Energy conservation, work simplification, pacing, and activity modification are key OT strategies for managing MS fatigue.

stem: A 5-year-old child avoids playground equipment and becomes anxious when feet leave the ground. Which sensory system is likely involved?
rationale: Gravitational insecurity is a vestibular processing disorder where the child has an excessive fear response to movement and changes in head position, resulting in avoidance of playground equipment.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a flexor tendon repair at 6 weeks post-surgery. What exercise progression is appropriate?
options: Begin gentle active ROM as cleared by the surgeon
rationale: At 6 weeks post flexor tendon repair, the tendon has sufficient healing strength to begin gentle active ROM. Heavy resistance is typically not introduced until 8-12 weeks post-surgery.

stem: A client in an acute psychiatric unit refuses to participate in an OT group. What is the BEST initial response by the OTA?
options: Force the client to attend as it is prescribed
Respect the client's autonomy, offer alternatives, and document the refusal
Discharge the client from OT services immediately
Report the client's behavior as non-compliant to the psychiatrist
rationale: Clients have the right to refuse treatment. The OTA should respect autonomy, explore reasons for refusal, offer alternative activities, and document the interaction for the supervising OT.

stem: An OTA is training a client with C6 tetraplegia to use tenodesis grasp. What wrist movement enables this grasp pattern?
options: Wrist flexion opens the hand; wrist extension closes the hand
Wrist extension creates passive finger flexion for grasp
Pronation and supination control finger opening and closing
rationale: Tenodesis grasp uses wrist extension to create passive finger flexion through tension in the long finger flexor tendons, allowing a functional grasp pattern without active finger muscles.

stem: During a dressing evaluation, a client with left neglect consistently ignores the left sleeve. Which compensatory strategy should the OTA teach?
options: Always start dressing with the right side first
Anchor attention by placing a bright-colored marker on the left side and teach scanning left
Dress only the right side and have a caregiver complete the left
Use a mirror on the right side only
rationale: Anchoring techniques using visual cues (bright tape, colored markers) on the neglected side combined with systematic visual scanning training help clients attend to the left side during dressing.

stem: A client with COPD is learning upper body dressing. Which positioning reduces dyspnea during the task?
options: Seated with arms supported, using pursed-lip breathing and front-opening garments
Bending forward at the waist with arms behind the back
rationale: Seated positioning reduces oxygen demand, arm support decreases accessory muscle fatigue, pursed-lip breathing maintains airway patency, and front-opening garments reduce overhead reaching.

stem: An OTA is conducting a kitchen safety assessment. The client has poor standing balance. Which adaptation is the HIGHEST priority?
options: Installing grab bars near the stove and recommending a perching stool for meal preparation
Replacing all pots and pans with new ones
Changing the kitchen wall color for better contrast
rationale: Grab bars near the stove prevent falls during cooking, and a perching stool allows seated meal preparation, reducing fatigue and fall risk for clients with poor standing balance.

stem: Which developmental milestone is expected at approximately 6 months of age?
options: Sitting independently with hands free for play
rationale: Independent sitting with hands free typically develops around 6 months. Walking develops at 12-15 months, mature pincer grasp at 10-12 months, and scissor skills at 3-4 years.

stem: A client with a radial nerve injury at the spiral groove presents with wrist drop. Which splint should the OTA fabricate?
options: Resting hand splint in intrinsic plus position
Dynamic wrist extension splint to support the wrist and allow functional hand use
rationale: Radial nerve injury causes wrist drop due to paralyzed wrist extensors. A dynamic wrist extension splint supports the wrist in functional position while allowing tendon gliding and hand function.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has severe burns on both hands. What is the anti-deformity position for the burned hand?
options: Wrist flexion, MCP extension, IP flexion, thumb adduction
Wrist extension, MCP flexion 70-90°, IP extension, thumb abduction and extension
Fist position with all joints fully flexed
Wrist neutral with all fingers extended and adducted
rationale: The anti-deformity position for burned hands counteracts the typical contracture pattern: wrist extension, MCP flexion, IP extension, and thumb abduction to maintain web space and joint mobility.

stem: A client post-stroke is working on meal preparation. They can follow 2-step directions but get confused with 3 or more steps. How should the OTA grade the activity?
options: Provide all directions at once to challenge the client
Break the recipe into 2-step sequences with visual cue cards
Have the client memorize the entire recipe before starting
rationale: Grading the cognitive demands to match the client's current ability (2-step directions) with visual cue cards supports successful task completion and can be progressively upgraded.

stem: An OTA is documenting a client's progress using SOAP note format. Which statement belongs in the 'O' (Objective) section?
options: Client completed 10 repetitions of shoulder flexion to 120° with moderate assist
Client will improve UE ROM to within functional limits in 4 weeks
rationale: The Objective section contains measurable, observable data collected by the clinician — ROM measurements, repetition counts, and assistance levels are objective findings.

stem: A client recovering from Guillain-Barré syndrome is in the recovery phase. Which intervention approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Aggressive resistive exercise to maximum fatigue
Graded activity progressing from gentle AROM to light resistance, monitoring for overwork weakness
Passive ROM only for the duration of recovery
rationale: GBS recovery requires careful activity grading to avoid overwork weakness (paradoxical weakening from overexertion). Progressive activity from gentle AROM to light resistance with fatigue monitoring is appropriate.

stem: During a home visit, an OTA observes that a client's bathroom has no grab bars, a high tub wall, and a slippery floor. What is the priority recommendation?
options: Install decorative towel racks for visual appeal
Install grab bars near the tub and toilet, add a tub bench, and use non-slip mats
Suggest the client move to a facility
rationale: Bathroom falls are a leading cause of injury. Grab bars provide support during transfers, a tub bench eliminates stepping over the high tub wall, and non-slip mats reduce slip risk.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with de Quervain's tenosynovitis. Which tendon compartment is affected?
rationale: De Quervain's tenosynovitis affects the first dorsal compartment containing the abductor pollicis longus (APL) and extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) tendons at the radial styloid.

stem: A child with autism spectrum disorder becomes overwhelmed during a classroom art activity. Which sensory strategy should the OTA recommend to the teacher?
options: Insist the child complete the activity without modification
Provide a quiet corner with reduced stimulation where the child can self-regulate before returning
Remove art activities from the curriculum entirely
Have the child sit in the center of the classroom for more social exposure
rationale: A designated quiet space with reduced sensory input allows the child to self-regulate and return to the activity when ready. This supports participation without overwhelming the child's sensory system.

stem: A client with a recent hip fracture fixation is weight-bearing as tolerated. Which bathing adaptation is MOST important?
options: Use a standard bathtub without modifications
Use a shower chair and handheld shower head to avoid standing fatigue and fall risk
Take sponge baths only for 6 months
Bathe in cold water to reduce inflammation
rationale: A shower chair eliminates prolonged standing and fall risk, while a handheld shower head allows the client to bathe while seated, maintaining hip precautions and weight-bearing status.

stem: An OTA is using the Cognitive Disabilities Model (Allen) with a client at ACL 3. What type of task can this client perform?
options: Repetitive manual actions that are familiar and guided by tactile cues
Independent community mobility and money management
rationale: ACL 3 (manual actions) indicates the client can perform repetitive, familiar motor tasks guided by tactile cues but cannot attend to visual cues or problem-solve. Activities must be simple and hands-on.

stem: A client with an upper extremity amputation is learning to use a body-powered prosthesis. What motion operates the terminal device?
options: Glenohumeral flexion and scapular abduction via a cable-control system
rationale: Body-powered prostheses use a cable system activated by glenohumeral flexion and scapular abduction (biscapular abduction) to operate the terminal device for grasp and release.

stem: An OTA is leading a life skills group for adolescents with emotional disturbance. Which group format is MOST appropriate?
options: Parallel group — each member works independently
Project group — members share materials but work on separate tasks
Cooperative group — members work together with shared responsibility for outcomes
Evaluative group — members only observe and provide feedback
rationale: A cooperative group for adolescents with emotional disturbance promotes social skills, shared responsibility, peer interaction, and emotional regulation within a structured therapeutic context.

stem: A client with a median nerve injury at the wrist cannot oppose the thumb. Which functional grasp is MOST affected?
rationale: Median nerve injury at the wrist affects thenar muscles responsible for thumb opposition. Tip-to-tip pinch requires opposition and is most affected; lateral pinch uses ulnar-innervated adductor pollicis.

stem: An OTA is providing caregiver training for a family member who will assist a client with moderate dementia at home. What should be emphasized?
options: Complex verbal instructions for each ADL step
Consistent routines, simple one-step cues, and structured environment with visual supports
Allowing the client complete independence without supervision
Teaching the client new technology-based reminder systems
rationale: Clients with moderate dementia benefit from consistent routines, simple cues, and structured environments. Caregivers should maintain predictability and use visual supports to reduce confusion.

stem: During a functional assessment, an OTA notes a client requires verbal cues for 50% of the steps during grooming. What assistance level is this?
rationale: Moderate assistance is defined as the client requiring help with 25-50% or more of task steps. Verbal cueing for 50% of grooming steps indicates moderate assistance level.

stem: A client with carpal tunnel syndrome reports numbness in the thumb, index, and middle fingers. Which nerve is compressed?
rationale: Carpal tunnel syndrome involves compression of the median nerve at the carpal tunnel, causing numbness and tingling in the median nerve distribution: thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger.

stem: An OTA is using graded motor imagery with a client who has complex regional pain syndrome. What is the correct sequence?
options: Mirror therapy → laterality recognition → mental imagery
Laterality recognition → motor imagery → mirror therapy
Mirror therapy only without any preparatory steps
rationale: Graded motor imagery follows a specific sequence: laterality recognition (left/right discrimination), then explicit motor imagery (imagining movement), then mirror therapy, progressively activating cortical motor areas.

stem: A 7-year-old child has difficulty copying shapes from the board to paper. Which visual perceptual skill is challenged?
rationale: Copying shapes from a board to paper requires visual motor integration — the coordination of visual perception with fine motor output. Deficits result in poor copying, handwriting, and drawing.

stem: An OTA is positioning a client in a wheelchair to prevent pressure ulcers. How often should weight shifts be performed?
options: Every 15-30 minutes for at least 15-30 seconds
Only when the client feels discomfort
Once at the beginning and end of the day
rationale: Wheelchair-bound clients should perform weight shifts every 15-30 minutes to redistribute pressure over bony prominences and prevent pressure ulcer development. Methods include forward lean, lateral lean, and push-ups.

stem: A client with schizophrenia has flat affect and poor motivation for self-care. Which OT approach is BEST?
options: Structured routine with achievable goals, positive reinforcement, and graded task demands
Leaving the client alone to develop intrinsic motivation
Complex multi-step grooming routines to stimulate cognition
rationale: Negative symptoms of schizophrenia respond best to structured routines, achievable goals, positive reinforcement, and graded activities that build confidence and participation gradually.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a thumb spica splint for a client with gamekeeper's thumb (UCL injury). What position should the thumb be in?
options: Thumb in slight flexion and abduction to protect the UCL
rationale: Gamekeeper's thumb (UCL injury of the MCP joint) requires immobilization with the thumb in slight flexion and abduction to protect the healing ulnar collateral ligament.

stem: A client with myasthenia gravis reports increasing weakness during afternoon activities. How should the OTA schedule treatment sessions?
options: Schedule all sessions in the late afternoon to challenge endurance
Schedule sessions in the morning when the client has the most strength, and use energy conservation
Schedule sessions randomly throughout the day
Double session length to accomplish more per visit
rationale: Myasthenia gravis causes progressive muscle weakness with sustained activity. Scheduling sessions when the client is strongest (typically morning) and incorporating energy conservation maximizes function.

stem: Which type of grasp is used when holding a key to unlock a door?
rationale: Lateral pinch (key pinch) involves the pad of the thumb pressing against the lateral (radial) side of the index finger, used for holding keys, cards, and flat objects.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for a client with executive function deficits after traumatic brain injury. Which strategy is MOST effective?
options: Expecting the client to self-organize naturally over time
Using external aids (checklists, planners, alarms) and teaching metacognitive strategies for self-monitoring
Removing all structure so the client must adapt independently
Limiting all activities to passive participation
rationale: Executive function deficits after TBI benefit from external organizational aids and metacognitive strategy training to support planning, initiation, sequencing, and self-monitoring of daily activities.

stem: A client with rheumatoid arthritis has swan-neck deformity of the ring finger. What does this deformity look like?
rationale: Swan-neck deformity consists of PIP hyperextension and DIP flexion, caused by volar plate laxity and intrinsic muscle imbalance commonly seen in rheumatoid arthritis.

stem: An OTA is treating a child with developmental coordination disorder (DCD). Which activity is BEST to improve bilateral coordination?
options: Catching a large ball with both hands, progressing to smaller balls
rationale: Bilateral coordination activities like ball catching with progression from large to small targets provide graded motor challenges that directly address the bilateral coordination deficits in DCD.

stem: A client with a hip arthroplasty (posterior approach) needs to get dressed. Which dressing sequence should the OTA teach?
options: Dress the unaffected leg first, then the affected leg
Dress the affected leg first while maintaining hip precautions using a reacher and dressing stick
Stand to pull up pants without any assistive devices
Bend forward past 90° to reach the feet
rationale: After posterior hip arthroplasty, the affected leg is dressed first using assistive devices (reacher, dressing stick, long-handled shoe horn) to maintain precautions: no hip flexion >90°, no internal rotation, no adduction.

stem: An OTA observes that a client with Parkinson's disease has micrographia. What does this mean?
options: The client writes in abnormally large letters
The client's handwriting becomes progressively smaller and cramped
The client cannot hold a pen
The client writes only with the non-dominant hand
rationale: Micrographia is a common Parkinson's symptom where handwriting becomes progressively smaller, lighter, and more cramped due to bradykinesia and reduced motor amplitude.

stem: A client with low vision needs to manage medications at home. Which adaptation should the OTA recommend?
options: Use standard prescription labels without modification
Use large-print labels, a weekly pill organizer, and high-contrast markers on medication bottles
Have the client memorize all medication names by feel
Eliminate all medications that are difficult to identify
rationale: Large-print labels, pill organizers, and high-contrast markers compensate for low vision while supporting safe, independent medication management at home.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with trigger finger. Which conservative intervention is appropriate before considering surgery?
options: Aggressive passive stretching of the affected finger
Splinting the MCP joint in slight flexion (0-15°) to prevent triggering, combined with tendon gliding exercises
Immediate surgical release without conservative trial
rationale: Conservative treatment of trigger finger includes MCP blocking splints to prevent full flexion (which causes triggering), tendon gliding exercises, and activity modification to reduce repetitive gripping.

stem: A client with an anterior approach total hip replacement has different precautions than a posterior approach. What should be AVOIDED?
rationale: Anterior approach hip replacement precautions differ from posterior: avoid hip extension combined with external rotation, as this risks anterior dislocation. Hip flexion restrictions are less stringent.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has difficulty with handwriting legibility. Which foundational skill should be assessed FIRST?
options: In-hand manipulation, grasp pattern, and visual motor integration
rationale: Handwriting legibility depends on foundational skills: in-hand manipulation (pencil control), grasp pattern (efficient pencil hold), and visual motor integration (translating visual input to motor output).

stem: A client post-stroke demonstrates ideational apraxia. How does this present during ADL performance?
options: The client cannot initiate movement but knows the sequence
The client cannot conceptualize the correct sequence of steps needed to complete a task
The client has difficulty with fine motor dexterity only
The client cannot see objects on the affected side
rationale: Ideational apraxia is the inability to conceptualize and sequence the steps of a multi-step task. The client may use objects incorrectly (e.g., trying to brush hair with a toothbrush) or perform steps out of order.

stem: An OTA is using fluidotherapy for a client with hand stiffness. What is the treatment medium and primary therapeutic effect?
options: Water jets that massage the hand
Cellulose particles suspended in warm air providing dry heat and desensitization
Ultrasound gel applied to the hand
Cold gel packs wrapped around the hand
rationale: Fluidotherapy uses cellulose particles circulated in warm dry air (118-125°F) to provide superficial dry heat, desensitization, and active exercise opportunity simultaneously.

stem: A client with a colostomy bag wants to return to community activities but is embarrassed. Which OT approach addresses this barrier?
options: Address body image concerns, practice bag management, and plan community integration with strategies for confidence
Tell the client they should not be embarrassed
Recommend the client stay home until the colostomy is reversed
rationale: OT addresses psychosocial aspects of disability including body image. Teaching independent bag management, discussing concerns, and gradual community integration build confidence and participation.

stem: An OTA is assessing a client's home for wheelchair accessibility. What is the minimum recommended doorway width for standard wheelchair access?
rationale: The ADA recommends a minimum clear doorway width of 32 inches for standard wheelchair passage. Wider doorways (36 inches) are preferred for easier maneuvering and power wheelchair access.

stem: A client with bilateral upper extremity weakness due to ALS is having difficulty eating. Which assistive technology should the OTA consider?
options: Mobile arm supports (balanced forearm orthoses) and lightweight adapted utensils
rationale: Mobile arm supports (BFOs) compensate for proximal weakness by supporting the forearm against gravity, allowing the client to bring utensils to the mouth using minimal residual strength.

stem: An OTA observes a child using a whole-hand (palmar) grasp on crayons at age 5. What does this indicate?
options: Delayed grasp development — a dynamic tripod grasp is expected by this age
Normal grasp that requires no intervention
rationale: By age 5, children should demonstrate a dynamic tripod grasp. Persistent palmar grasp at this age indicates delayed fine motor development requiring assessment and intervention.

stem: A client with chronic pain demonstrates fear-avoidance behavior. Which OT approach is evidence-based?
options: Complete activity restriction to prevent pain
Graded activity exposure combined with pain neuroscience education and self-management strategies
Ignoring pain reports and pushing maximum activity
rationale: Fear-avoidance is addressed through graded exposure to feared activities, pain neuroscience education to reduce catastrophizing, and self-management strategies to build self-efficacy.

stem: During morning ADLs, a client with right hemiplegia has difficulty stabilizing a toothpaste tube while opening it. What is the BEST compensatory strategy?
options: Have the caregiver always open the toothpaste
Use a suction cup toothpaste holder or tube squeezer mounted to the counter for one-handed management
Use teeth to open the tube
rationale: Suction-based stabilizers and adapted tube squeezers enable one-handed toothpaste management for clients with hemiplegia, promoting independence in oral hygiene.

stem: An OTA is treating a premature infant in the NICU. Which intervention principle is MOST important?
options: Provide maximum sensory stimulation to promote development
Monitor physiological stress signs and provide gentle, graded sensory input based on infant state
Complete all therapy goals regardless of infant state
Use the same intervention protocol for all premature infants
rationale: NICU intervention requires monitoring physiological stress signs (desaturation, color changes, behavioral cues) and providing individualized, graded sensory input aligned with the infant's current state and tolerance.

stem: A client with cubital tunnel syndrome reports numbness in the ring and little fingers. Which nerve is compressed?
rationale: Cubital tunnel syndrome involves compression of the ulnar nerve at the elbow, causing numbness and tingling in the ulnar nerve distribution: little finger and ulnar half of the ring finger.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with cognitive impairments to use a smartphone for daily task reminders. What is this type of intervention called?
options: Assistive technology as a cognitive prosthetic
rationale: Using a smartphone as a reminder system is an example of assistive technology functioning as a cognitive prosthetic — an external device that compensates for cognitive limitations.

stem: A client with a stage II pressure ulcer on the sacrum is positioned in a wheelchair. What seat cushion should be recommended?
options: Pressure-relieving cushion (gel, air, or alternating pressure) with off-loading of the sacral area
Hard wooden seat for posture support
No cushion is needed if the client shifts weight regularly
rationale: Stage II pressure ulcers require pressure-relieving cushions that distribute weight and off-load the sacral area. Gel, air, or alternating pressure cushions reduce interface pressure below capillary closing pressure.

stem: An OTA is evaluating a client's pinch strength using a pinch gauge. Which pinch type typically produces the STRONGEST measurement?
options: All pinch types produce equal force
rationale: Lateral (key) pinch typically produces the strongest pinch force because it uses the powerful adductor pollicis muscle and provides a broader contact area between thumb and index finger.

stem: A client with bipolar disorder in a manic phase wants to participate in OT. Which activity is MOST therapeutically appropriate?
options: Competitive group sport with high stimulation
A calming, repetitive, structured individual activity such as simple beading or folding
An open-ended creative art project with no boundaries
A debate group to channel high energy
rationale: During manic episodes, clients benefit from calming, structured, repetitive activities that channel energy without increasing stimulation. Competitive or open-ended activities may escalate arousal.

stem: An OTA is performing scar management after a hand burn. Which techniques are evidence-based for scar remodeling?
options: Leave the scar untreated for natural resolution
Scar massage, silicone gel sheeting, compression garments, and custom pressure splints
Apply ice to the scar three times daily
Expose the scar to direct sunlight for vitamin D
rationale: Evidence-based scar management includes deep friction scar massage to mobilize collagen, silicone products to hydrate and flatten scars, and compression/pressure to reduce hypertrophic scarring.

stem: A child with Down syndrome has hypotonia and ligamentous laxity. Which precaution should the OTA take during treatment?
options: Apply maximum resistance to strengthen muscles
Avoid hyperextension of joints and be cautious with cervical spine due to risk of atlantoaxial instability
Encourage maximal stretching of all joints
Ignore joint laxity as it is not clinically significant
rationale: Children with Down syndrome have ligamentous laxity and are at risk for atlantoaxial instability. The OTA must avoid hyperextension of joints and take cervical spine precautions during all activities.

stem: An OTA is training a client to use one-handed techniques for shoe tying. Which method is appropriate?
options: One-handed shoe tying technique using loops and friction, or elastic laces as an alternative
rationale: One-handed shoe tying techniques (loop method) or adaptive alternatives (elastic laces, lock laces) enable independence. Multiple options should be presented based on client preference and dexterity.

stem: A client with dementia is in the late stage of the disease. What type of sensory-based intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Gentle multisensory stimulation (Snamezelen approach) with calming music, textures, and aromatherapy
rationale: Late-stage dementia clients benefit from multisensory stimulation that provides comfort and engagement without cognitive demands. Snoezelen environments offer controlled sensory experiences.

stem: An OTA is measuring grip strength using a Jamar dynamometer. Which handle position is standard for testing?
rationale: The second handle position of the Jamar dynamometer is the standard testing position and typically produces the highest grip force reading across populations.

stem: A client recovering from a traumatic brain injury demonstrates confabulation. What does this mean?
options: The client fabricates stories to fill memory gaps without awareness that they are false
The client is experiencing visual hallucinations
rationale: Confabulation is the unintentional production of false memories or stories to fill gaps in memory. The client genuinely believes the fabricated information is true — it is not deliberate deception.

stem: An OTA is adapting a computer workstation for a client with C5 tetraplegia. Which input method should be considered?
options: Voice recognition software, head/mouth pointer, or sip-and-puff systems
rationale: C5 tetraplegia limits hand function. Alternative computer input methods (voice recognition, head/mouth stick, sip-and-puff) bypass the need for fine motor control and enable computer access.

stem: A client has a Boutonniere deformity of the index finger. What is the characteristic position of this deformity?
options: MCP hyperextension with PIP and DIP flexion
rationale: Boutonniere deformity presents as PIP flexion and DIP hyperextension, caused by disruption of the central slip of the extensor tendon at the PIP joint.

stem: An OTA is conducting a group session for clients with depression. Which therapeutic factor of group therapy is MOST beneficial?
options: Universality — realizing others share similar experiences reduces isolation
Individual attention from the therapist only
rationale: Universality (Yalom's therapeutic factor) helps clients with depression realize they are not alone in their struggles. This shared experience reduces isolation and fosters hope through peer connection.

stem: A client recovering from a rotator cuff repair is 8 weeks post-surgery. The surgeon clears active ROM. Which progression is appropriate?
options: Start with gravity-eliminated active ROM and progress to against-gravity active ROM
Continue passive ROM only for another 6 weeks
rationale: At 8 weeks post-rotator cuff repair with AROM clearance, begin with gravity-eliminated positions and progress to against-gravity active ROM. Resistance is typically not introduced until 12+ weeks.

stem: An OTA notices a client using heavy joint compression during writing (pressing hard on paper). This behavior is characteristic of which sensory processing pattern?
rationale: Heavy joint compression and excessive pressure during tasks indicate sensory seeking behavior, specifically proprioceptive seeking. The client craves deep pressure and heavy work to meet their sensory needs.

stem: A client with a total knee replacement needs to get in and out of a car. What technique should the OTA teach?
options: Jump into the car seat quickly
Back up to the seat, sit down first, then pivot and lift the operated leg into the car using hands or a leg lifter
Step directly into the car with the operated leg first
rationale: After TKR, the client backs up to the car seat, sits down maintaining knee precautions, then pivots and lifts the operated leg into the car. A leg lifter strap may assist with leg clearance.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with dysphagia. What should the OTA do if the client coughs repeatedly during eating?
options: Encourage the client to drink water quickly to clear the food
Stop the meal, report to the supervising OT and speech-language pathologist, and ensure the client is positioned upright
Continue the meal at a slower pace
Lay the client flat to assist with swallowing
rationale: Repeated coughing during eating suggests aspiration risk. The OTA must stop the meal, position the client upright, and immediately report to the supervising OT and SLP for swallowing reassessment.

stem: A client with ulnar nerve palsy presents with clawing of the ring and little fingers. Which splint addresses this deformity?
options: MCP blocking splint (anti-claw splint) to prevent MCP hyperextension of the ring and little fingers
rationale: Ulnar claw hand involves MCP hyperextension and IP flexion of the ring and little fingers. An MCP blocking splint prevents hyperextension, allowing the intact extrinsic extensors to extend the IP joints.

stem: An OTA is helping an elderly client set up a medication management system. Which is the MOST effective approach for a client with mild memory impairment?
options: Weekly pill organizer with labeled compartments combined with an alarm-based reminder system
Writing medication names on a napkin
Trusting the client to remember without any aids
rationale: A weekly pill organizer provides visual structure, and alarm reminders compensate for memory impairment, creating a dual-system approach that maximizes medication adherence.

stem: A client with an incomplete SCI at C8-T1 has intrinsic minus (claw) hand posture. What functional limitation does this cause?
options: Difficulty with fine motor precision and inability to flatten the hand for grasping flat objects
rationale: Intrinsic minus hand (loss of lumbricals and interossei) causes MCP hyperextension and IP flexion, limiting fine motor precision, flat hand activities, and effective grasp of large or flat objects.

stem: An OTA is using the Model of Human Occupation (MOHO) to guide intervention for a retired client struggling with role loss. Which component should be primarily addressed?
options: Habituation — restructuring daily routines and developing new meaningful roles
