rationale: Habituation in MOHO addresses roles and habits. A retired client with role loss needs help restructuring routines, identifying new meaningful roles, and establishing productive habits.

stem: During a functional capacity evaluation, a client can lift 20 lbs from floor to waist. According to the DOT physical demand levels, this falls into which category?
rationale: The DOT classifies physical demand levels: Sedentary (up to 10 lbs), Light (up to 20 lbs), Medium (20-50 lbs), Heavy (50-100 lbs), Very Heavy (over 100 lbs). Lifting 20 lbs falls in the Light category.

stem: An OTA is treating a client who had a tendon transfer for radial nerve palsy. What is the MOST important post-operative intervention principle?
options: Protect the transfer, re-educate the transferred muscle through motor learning, and progress gradually
Apply maximum resistance to the transferred tendon
rationale: Post-tendon transfer requires protecting the healing transfer, re-educating the transferred muscle in its new function through motor learning principles, and gradual progressive strengthening.

stem: A client with hemiplegia is learning to open a jar one-handed. Which adapted method should the OTA teach?
options: Use a standard method with both hands
Place the jar in a Dycem-lined drawer, close the drawer to stabilize, and twist the lid with the functioning hand
Have the caregiver open all jars
rationale: The drawer method stabilizes the jar between the drawer walls (with Dycem for grip) while the functioning hand twists the lid, enabling one-handed jar opening for clients with hemiplegia.

stem: An OTA is assessing a client's visual field. The client can only see objects in the right visual field of both eyes. What type of visual field deficit is this?
rationale: Left homonymous hemianopia means loss of the left visual field in both eyes. The client can only see the right visual field, indicating damage to the right optic tract or visual cortex.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a fear of falling after a hip fracture. Which intervention strategy addresses this specific issue?
options: Graded exposure to mobility activities with progressively challenging balance tasks and fall prevention education
Tell the client that falling is unlikely and resume full activity
Only train transfers with maximum assist indefinitely
rationale: Fear of falling (post-fall anxiety) is addressed through graded exposure to mobility activities, progressive balance training, fall prevention education, and building confidence through successful experiences.

stem: A client undergoing chemotherapy reports peripheral neuropathy in the hands. Which safety precaution should the OTA emphasize?
options: Use the affected hands for all hot item handling
Test water temperature with unaffected body parts and use oven mitts, as impaired sensation increases burn risk
Ignore the neuropathy as it will resolve
Apply ice packs to improve sensation
rationale: Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy impairs protective sensation. The OTA must teach compensatory safety strategies: testing water temperature, using protective equipment, and visually monitoring hand position.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for a client with ideomotor apraxia. What characterizes this condition?
options: The client cannot understand verbal commands
The client knows what to do but cannot execute the correct motor plan on command despite intact motor function
rationale: Ideomotor apraxia is the inability to perform a motor act on command despite intact comprehension and motor ability. The client may perform the action automatically but fails when asked to demonstrate it.

stem: A client with a brachial plexus injury (Erb-Duchenne palsy) cannot raise the arm. Which nerve roots are affected?
rationale: Erb-Duchenne palsy affects the upper trunk (C5-C6) of the brachial plexus, causing loss of shoulder abduction, external rotation, elbow flexion, and forearm supination ('waiter's tip' position).

stem: An OTA is adapting a computer workstation for ergonomic use. What is the recommended monitor height?
options: Top of the screen at or slightly below eye level, approximately arm's length away
Above head height to promote neck extension
rationale: Ergonomic monitor placement: top of screen at or slightly below eye level to maintain neutral cervical spine position, approximately arm's length (20-26 inches) away to reduce eye strain.

stem: A client with an incomplete SCI demonstrates Brown-Séquard syndrome. What does this presentation include?
options: Complete loss of all motor and sensory function below the injury
Ipsilateral motor loss and proprioception loss with contralateral pain and temperature loss
Loss of only autonomic function below the injury
rationale: Brown-Séquard syndrome (hemisection of the cord) causes ipsilateral motor paralysis and proprioception loss (dorsal column damage) with contralateral pain and temperature loss (spinothalamic tract damage).

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with low back pain proper body mechanics for lifting objects from the floor. What is the correct technique?
options: Bend at the waist with straight legs
Bend at the knees and hips, keep the back straight, and hold the object close to the body
Twist while lifting to reach the object
Reach far away from the body with arms extended
rationale: Proper body mechanics: bend at knees and hips (not waist), maintain natural spinal curves, hold the object close to the body to reduce lever arm, and avoid twisting during the lift.

stem: A client who had a CVA 6 months ago is at Brunnstrom Stage 5. What does this mean for upper extremity function?
options: Isolated, voluntary movements are possible with decreasing synergy influence
rationale: Brunnstrom Stage 5 features increasingly isolated voluntary movements outside synergy patterns. The client can perform more complex and varied movements with diminishing obligatory synergy influence.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with bilateral hand contractures from scleroderma. Which intervention is MOST critical?
options: Sustained static progressive stretching combined with paraffin heat and active ROM to maintain tissue extensibility
Waiting for the contractures to resolve spontaneously
rationale: Scleroderma causes progressive skin and tissue tightening. Paraffin heat increases tissue extensibility, followed by sustained stretching and active ROM to maintain hand function and prevent further contracture.

stem: A client with traumatic brain injury is in Rancho Level IV. What behavior characterizes this level?
rationale: Rancho Level IV (Confused-Agitated) is characterized by heightened state of activity, confusion, bizarre and non-purposeful behavior, incoherent verbalization, and inability to cooperate with treatment.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with hemiplegia to put on a button-front shirt. What is the correct sequence?
options: Affected arm first for donning, unaffected arm first for doffing
Unaffected arm first for both donning and doffing
Affected arm first for both donning and doffing
rationale: The rule for hemiplegic dressing: affected arm enters the sleeve first when donning, and the unaffected arm comes out first when doffing. This minimizes required ROM in the affected limb.

stem: A client with upper extremity lymphedema following mastectomy asks about activity precautions. What should the OTA advise?
options: Avoid all upper extremity activity permanently
Avoid blood pressure measurements, venipuncture, and constrictive clothing on the affected arm; progress exercise gradually
Exercise aggressively to pump out the lymphatic fluid
Apply tight compression continuously without monitoring
rationale: Lymphedema precautions include avoiding blood pressure cuffs, needle sticks, and constrictive clothing on the affected arm. Exercise should be gradually progressed with compression garments as tolerated.

stem: An OTA is treating a child with spastic hemiplegia who has difficulty with bimanual activities. Which therapeutic approach is appropriate?
options: Only working on the unaffected hand to maximize independence
Hand-Arm Bimanual Intensive Therapy (HABIT) to train both hands working together during functional tasks
Focusing only on cognition without motor practice
rationale: HABIT provides intensive bimanual training with structured practice of both hands working together during functional tasks, improving coordination between the affected and unaffected upper extremities.

stem: A client with spinal cord injury reports a change in bowel and bladder function alongside new bilateral lower extremity weakness. What should the OTA do?
options: Stop treatment immediately and report to the supervising OT and medical team — these symptoms suggest a medical emergency
Document the findings and report at the next team meeting
Recommend the client rest and try again tomorrow
rationale: New onset bowel/bladder changes with bilateral LE weakness could indicate cauda equina syndrome or other medical emergency. The OTA must stop treatment and report immediately for urgent evaluation.

stem: An OTA is adapting a toothbrush for a client with limited grip. Which modification is MOST effective?
options: Build up the handle with foam tubing or use a universal cuff to strap the brush to the hand
Switch to a water-only oral rinse
Use a brush with a shorter handle
rationale: Foam tubing increases handle diameter for clients with limited grip strength, and a universal cuff straps the utensil to the hand for clients who cannot maintain a voluntary grasp.

stem: A client with Parkinson's disease has difficulty initiating movement to stand up from a chair. Which cueing strategy can help?
options: Wait silently until the client initiates movement
Provide rhythmic auditory cues (counting '1-2-3 stand') or visual targets to facilitate movement initiation
Pull the client to standing without warning
Tell the client to try harder
rationale: Parkinson's disease causes difficulty with motor initiation (akinesia). External cues — rhythmic auditory counting, visual targets, or verbal commands — help bypass basal ganglia dysfunction and facilitate movement.

stem: An OTA is setting up a child's classroom environment to support attention. Which modification is evidence-based?
options: Place the child's desk near the window for stimulation
Position the desk away from high-traffic areas, use a visual schedule, and provide a seat cushion for sensory input
Remove all visual supports from the classroom
Have the child sit on the floor without a desk
rationale: Environmental modifications for attention include reducing distractions (away from traffic/windows), visual schedules for predictability, and sensory supports (seat cushions) for alertness.

stem: A client is recovering from a Zone II flexor tendon injury (no man's land). Why is this zone particularly challenging for surgical repair?
options: The tendons are very thick in this area
The FDS and FDP tendons run within a tight fibro-osseous sheath, increasing adhesion risk
The tendons are not present in this zone
This zone has excellent blood supply that causes excessive swelling
rationale: Zone II ('no man's land') is challenging because both FDS and FDP tendons travel within a narrow fibro-osseous sheath, increasing the risk of adhesion formation between tendons and surrounding structures.

stem: An OTA is treating an elderly client with a fear of falling who avoids leaving the house. Which frame of reference guides this intervention?
options: Cognitive behavioral frame of reference — addressing maladaptive thoughts about falling and gradually increasing activity
rationale: Cognitive behavioral approaches address the maladaptive thought patterns (catastrophizing about falling) and avoidance behaviors through cognitive restructuring and graded behavioral activation.

stem: A client has a mallet finger injury. What is the characteristic deformity and splint position?
options: PIP flexion deformity; splint PIP in extension
DIP flexion deformity (drooping fingertip); splint DIP in full extension or slight hyperextension
MCP hyperextension; splint MCP in flexion
Wrist drop; splint wrist in extension
rationale: Mallet finger is caused by disruption of the terminal extensor tendon, resulting in DIP flexion (drooping fingertip). The DIP is splinted in full extension or slight hyperextension for 6-8 weeks.

stem: An OTA is evaluating a client's occupational performance using the Assessment of Motor and Process Skills (AMPS). What does this tool measure?
options: Isolated joint ROM and muscle strength
Quality of motor and process skills during observed performance of familiar daily tasks
rationale: The AMPS is a standardized observational assessment that evaluates the quality of motor and process (organizational/adaptive) skills during performance of chosen familiar daily living tasks in a natural context.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has de Quervain's tenosynovitis. Which splint is MOST appropriate?
options: Resting hand splint immobilizing all digits
Thumb spica splint immobilizing the wrist and thumb CMC/MCP joints
rationale: De Quervain's tenosynovitis involves the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons. A thumb spica splint immobilizes the wrist and thumb to reduce inflammation and pain.

stem: A client with schizophrenia is participating in a community reintegration group. The OTA notices the client is responding to internal stimuli. What should the OTA do?
options: Ignore the behavior and continue the group
Redirect the client to the task and provide structured, concrete instructions
Confront the client about hallucinations in front of the group
Discharge the client from the group immediately
rationale: Redirecting to structured tasks and providing concrete instructions helps ground a client experiencing hallucinations. Confrontation increases anxiety, while ignoring compromises participation.

stem: An OTA is treating a child with developmental coordination disorder (DCD). Which intervention approach is MOST evidence-based?
options: Passive range of motion exercises only
Task-specific training using Cognitive Orientation to Occupational Performance (CO-OP)
Isolated muscle strengthening without functional context
rationale: CO-OP is an evidence-based cognitive approach for DCD that uses guided discovery and problem-solving strategies to improve motor skill acquisition in meaningful, task-specific activities.

stem: A client with a median nerve injury at the wrist presents with which functional deficit?
options: Loss of thumb opposition and decreased sensation in the first three digits
Claw hand deformity affecting all fingers
Wrist drop with inability to extend fingers
rationale: Median nerve injury at the wrist affects the thenar muscles (loss of thumb opposition) and sensory distribution to the palmar thumb, index, middle, and radial half of the ring finger.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a flexor tendon repair at 6 weeks post-surgery. Which exercise progression is appropriate?
options: Continue passive ROM only; no active motion until 12 weeks
Begin gentle active ROM and light pinch activities per protocol
Discontinue all therapy as healing is complete
rationale: At 6 weeks post flexor tendon repair, the tendon has sufficient strength for gentle active ROM and light functional activities. Resistive exercises typically begin at 8-12 weeks.

stem: A client with bipolar disorder in a manic phase presents to OT. Which activity is MOST appropriate?
options: Complex multi-step group project requiring sustained attention
Brief, structured, gross motor activity with clear boundaries
Open-ended creative painting with no time limits
Competitive group game with unpredictable outcomes
rationale: During mania, clients have increased energy, distractibility, and impulsivity. Brief, structured gross motor activities channel excess energy while clear boundaries maintain safety and focus.

stem: An OTA is assessing a client's home for wheelchair accessibility. What is the minimum doorway width required for standard wheelchair passage?
rationale: The minimum clear doorway width for standard wheelchair passage is 32 inches, though 36 inches is preferred for easier maneuvering. ADA standards recommend 32-inch minimum clear width.

stem: A client with traumatic brain injury demonstrates perseveration during a dressing task. What does this mean?
options: The client refuses to attempt the task
The client repeats the same step or response inappropriately despite cues to move on
The client completes the task too quickly
The client cannot remember how to start the task
rationale: Perseveration is the repetition of a response or action when it is no longer appropriate, common in TBI and frontal lobe damage. It reflects difficulty with cognitive flexibility and response inhibition.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for a child with autism spectrum disorder who avoids playground activities due to gravitational insecurity. Which approach is BEST?
options: Force the child onto the swings to overcome fear
Gradually introduce vestibular input starting with low-to-ground, linear movement activities
Only provide proprioceptive activities with no vestibular input
rationale: Gravitational insecurity responds to gradual, controlled vestibular input. Start with low-to-ground linear movements (rocking horse) and progress gradually as the child's tolerance improves.

stem: A client with COPD is learning energy conservation for meal preparation. Which strategy is MOST effective?
options: Prepare all meals standing at the counter
Gather all supplies first, sit while preparing food, and use lightweight utensils
Cook all meals for the week in one day
rationale: Energy conservation for COPD includes organizing supplies before starting, sitting during tasks, using lightweight equipment, and pacing the activity to reduce oxygen demands.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a static progressive splint for a client with MCP joint flexion contractures. What is the purpose of this splint?
options: Immobilize the joints in their current position
Apply low-load prolonged stretch to gradually increase ROM
rationale: Static progressive splints apply low-load prolonged stress to contracted tissues, gradually increasing ROM through sustained end-range positioning that promotes tissue remodeling.

stem: A client with major depressive disorder reports difficulty getting out of bed and performing morning self-care. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Tell the client they need to try harder
Collaborate to establish a simplified morning routine with achievable steps and positive reinforcement
Remove all self-care expectations until depression resolves
rationale: Grading activities into manageable steps with client collaboration and positive reinforcement supports self-efficacy and gradually builds participation in daily routines despite depression.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has had a CVA affecting the middle cerebral artery (MCA). Which deficit pattern is expected?
options: Contralateral face and upper extremity weakness greater than lower extremity
rationale: MCA stroke causes contralateral hemiplegia with upper extremity and face affected more than lower extremity, plus potential language deficits (left MCA) or neglect (right MCA).

stem: A client post-TBI demonstrates difficulty sequencing the steps of making a sandwich. Which cognitive skill is impaired?
rationale: Sequencing is an executive function skill. Difficulty ordering the steps of a multi-step task like sandwich making indicates impaired executive functioning, common after frontal lobe TBI.

stem: An OTA is providing hand therapy for a client with Dupuytren's contracture post-fasciectomy. What is the primary OT goal?
options: Maintain finger extension and prevent recurrence of flexion contractures through splinting and ROM
rationale: Post-fasciectomy for Dupuytren's, the goal is maintaining gained finger extension through night extension splinting, active/passive ROM, scar management, and gradual return to function.

stem: A client with Guillain-Barré syndrome in the acute phase has ascending paralysis reaching the upper extremities. What is the OTA's PRIMARY concern?
options: Monitoring respiratory status and positioning to prevent complications
rationale: In acute GBS with ascending paralysis, respiratory compromise is the primary life-threatening concern. The OTA focuses on positioning, monitoring respiratory status, and preventing complications.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with low vision to organize medications. Which adaptation is MOST helpful?
options: Use high-contrast labels, tactile markers, and a pill organizer with large compartments
Rely entirely on family members for medication management
Arrange all medications in identical unmarked containers
rationale: High-contrast labels, tactile markers (rubber bands, raised dots), and large-compartment pill organizers support independent medication management for clients with low vision.

stem: A client with a rotator cuff repair is 3 weeks post-surgery. The OTA notices the client actively lifting their arm during dressing. What should the OTA do?
options: Encourage the client to continue as they are progressing well
Stop the activity and educate the client that active shoulder motion is restricted to protect the repair
Add resistance exercises to the dressing activity
Document the observation but take no immediate action
rationale: At 3 weeks post-rotator cuff repair, active shoulder motion is typically restricted. The OTA must stop the activity, educate about precautions, and reinforce passive ROM only.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has ideational apraxia. How does this present?
options: The client cannot conceptualize or sequence the steps of a task despite intact motor ability
The client has poor visual acuity
The client lacks motivation to participate
rationale: Ideational apraxia is the inability to conceptualize and plan the steps of a purposeful activity. The client may not know what to do with objects or how to sequence task steps.

stem: A 7-year-old child with handwriting difficulties is referred to OT. Assessment reveals poor in-hand manipulation skills. Which activity addresses this?
options: Picking up and rotating small pegs within the hand without using the other hand
Carrying heavy objects with two hands
rationale: In-hand manipulation involves moving objects within the hand (translation, shift, rotation). Picking up and rotating small pegs targets these skills directly to improve handwriting.

stem: A client with multiple sclerosis reports that symptoms worsen significantly with heat exposure. What is this phenomenon called?
rationale: Uhthoff's phenomenon is the temporary worsening of MS symptoms with heat exposure. The OTA should educate on avoiding hot environments and using cooling strategies.

stem: An OTA is conducting a group for clients with substance use disorders. Which type of group is MOST appropriate for early recovery?
options: Unstructured open discussion with no therapist guidance
Structured psychoeducational group focusing on coping skills and relapse prevention
Advanced community outing requiring independent problem-solving
rationale: In early recovery, structured psychoeducational groups provide education on coping skills, relapse prevention, and life skills in a predictable, supportive format.

stem: A client with a below-elbow amputation is being trained in prosthetic use. Which is the FIRST step in prosthetic training?
options: Donning and doffing the prosthesis independently
rationale: Prosthetic training begins with donning/doffing, then progresses to controls training, pre-positioning, functional use with simple objects, and finally complex bilateral tasks.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with lateral epicondylitis. Which activity modification should be recommended?
options: Increase gripping activities to strengthen the extensors
Use a counterforce brace and modify grip techniques to reduce wrist extensor strain
Apply heat before and after all activities
Avoid all upper extremity movement for 6 months
rationale: Lateral epicondylitis involves the wrist extensors at the lateral epicondyle. A counterforce brace reduces strain, and ergonomic modifications decrease repetitive stress on the tendons.

stem: A client with a C4 spinal cord injury requires which type of wheelchair?
options: Manual wheelchair with standard push rims
Power wheelchair with head or sip-and-puff controls
Standard transport wheelchair pushed by family
No wheelchair — the client can ambulate with a walker
rationale: C4 SCI results in tetraplegia with no upper extremity function below the shoulders. A power wheelchair with head, chin, or sip-and-puff controls enables independent mobility.

stem: An OTA is evaluating a client's kitchen for accessibility. The client uses a wheelchair. What is the optimal counter height?
rationale: Wheelchair-accessible counter height is 28-34 inches with knee clearance underneath, allowing the client to approach and work comfortably from a seated position.

stem: A client with hemiplegia has developed shoulder subluxation on the affected side. Which intervention helps manage this condition?
options: Aggressive passive ROM into shoulder flexion above 90°
Arm trough or lap tray for support and proper positioning when seated
Hanging weights from the affected arm
Ignoring the subluxation as it will self-resolve
rationale: Shoulder subluxation in hemiplegia requires proper support and positioning. An arm trough or lap tray supports the affected arm and reduces gravitational pull on the glenohumeral joint.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has hoarding disorder. Which approach is MOST therapeutic?
options: Discard all items without the client's knowledge
Collaboratively sort items using a decision-making framework while respecting client autonomy
Avoid addressing the hoarding as it is not within OT scope
Tell the client their behavior is irrational
rationale: Hoarding intervention requires collaborative sorting with the client, using cognitive-behavioral strategies and decision-making frameworks while respecting autonomy and building skills.

stem: A client with carpal tunnel syndrome reports nighttime numbness and tingling. Which splint position is recommended for nighttime wear?
options: No splint is indicated for carpal tunnel
rationale: A wrist splint in neutral position reduces pressure on the median nerve within the carpal tunnel. Nighttime wear prevents wrist flexion that increases carpal tunnel pressure during sleep.

stem: An OTA is providing fall prevention education to a client with osteoporosis. Which recommendation is MOST important?
options: Wear high-heeled shoes for ankle support
Install grab bars in the bathroom and use a night light for nighttime navigation
Avoid all physical activity to prevent fractures
Remove all furniture from the home
rationale: For osteoporosis, fall prevention is critical due to high fracture risk. Grab bars in the bathroom and night lights reduce fall risk in the most common locations for falls.

stem: A client with ALS is in the early stage of the disease. What should the OTA focus on?
options: Intensive resistive exercise to build strength reserves
Energy conservation, adaptive equipment training, and maintaining function as long as possible
Wheelchair training as the sole intervention
Discharge from therapy since early-stage clients don't need OT
rationale: Early ALS intervention focuses on energy conservation to prevent fatigue-induced weakness, adaptive equipment for emerging deficits, and maintaining current functional abilities.

stem: An OTA is assessing a child's visual-motor integration skills. Which standardized assessment is commonly used?
rationale: The Beery-Buktenica VMI assesses visual-motor integration by having the child copy geometric forms of increasing complexity. It is widely used in pediatric OT assessments.

stem: A client with complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) of the hand demonstrates allodynia. What does this mean?
options: The client cannot feel any sensation in the hand
Normally non-painful stimuli (such as light touch) cause significant pain
The client has excessive sweating in the hand
The client has full pain-free ROM
rationale: Allodynia is pain from stimuli that are not normally painful, such as light touch or gentle pressure. It is a hallmark feature of CRPS and guides gentle, graded intervention approaches.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has dysphagia following a stroke. Which is within the OTA's scope?
options: Training the client in safe feeding positions and modified eating techniques
Prescribing a specific diet consistency independently
rationale: OTAs can train clients in safe positioning, adaptive feeding techniques, and modified utensil use for dysphagia. Instrumental swallowing evaluations and diet prescription are outside OTA scope.

stem: A client with a traumatic brain injury is at Rancho Los Amigos Level IV. How does the client present?
options: Confused and agitated — may be combative and disoriented
Alert, oriented, and able to participate in complex problem-solving
Purposeful and appropriate in all activities
rationale: Rancho Level IV is the confused-agitated state. The client is disoriented, may be combative, has very short attention span, and cannot cooperate with structured tasks.

stem: An OTA is providing intervention for a client with trigger finger. What conservative intervention is appropriate?
options: Splinting the MCP joint in extension and activity modification to reduce repetitive gripping
Immobilizing all fingers and the wrist
Heat application as the sole treatment
rationale: Conservative treatment for trigger finger includes splinting the MCP in slight extension to prevent full flexion, activity modification, and anti-inflammatory measures to reduce A1 pulley inflammation.

stem: A client in an inpatient psychiatric unit has been placed on suicide precautions. What must the OTA ensure during therapy sessions?
options: Allow the client to use scissors and sharp tools for therapeutic activities
Maintain constant visual contact and remove all potentially harmful objects from the environment
Leave the client alone to encourage independence
rationale: On suicide precautions, the OTA must maintain line-of-sight supervision, remove all sharp objects and potential ligature risks, and follow facility safety protocols during all interactions.

stem: An OTA is working with a premature infant in the NICU. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Bright lights and loud music to stimulate development
Gentle positioning, containment, and graded sensory input based on infant cues
No intervention until the infant reaches full-term gestational age
rationale: NICU OT focuses on developmental positioning, containment to simulate the womb, and carefully graded sensory input based on the infant's behavioral cues to prevent overstimulation.

stem: A client with an ulnar nerve injury at the elbow presents with which hand deformity?
options: Claw hand deformity affecting the ring and small fingers
rationale: Ulnar nerve injury causes claw hand deformity in the ring and small fingers (MCP hyperextension and IP flexion) due to loss of the lumbricals and interossei to those fingers.

stem: An OTA is teaching a client with C6 SCI to dress independently. Which technique is essential for donning pants?
options: Standing to pull up pants with bilateral grasp
Using a supine or side-lying position with momentum and leg management techniques
Having a caregiver dress the lower body at all times
Using a standard sitting position and bending forward past 90°
rationale: C6 SCI clients dress lower body in supine or side-lying, using tenodesis, momentum, and leg management techniques. This compensates for absent hand grasp and trunk instability.

stem: A client with rheumatoid arthritis has ulnar drift of the MCP joints. Which splint design helps correct this?
options: MCP ulnar deviation splint providing radial pull at the MCP joints
Resting hand splint in full extension
rationale: Ulnar drift correction splints provide radial deviation force at the MCP joints to counteract the ulnar deforming forces. They are used during functional activities or at rest.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has difficulty with bilateral coordination. Which activity BEST addresses this deficit?
options: Single-hand coloring on a flat surface
Cutting with scissors while the other hand holds and turns the paper
Watching a demonstration of the task
rationale: Cutting requires the dominant hand to operate scissors while the assisting hand stabilizes and turns the paper, directly training bilateral coordination with a role-differentiated task.

stem: A client with myasthenia gravis reports increased weakness as the day progresses. What should the OTA recommend?
options: Schedule the most demanding activities for the end of the day
Schedule the most demanding activities in the morning when strength is greatest
Increase activity intensity throughout the day
Avoid all activity to conserve energy
rationale: Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable weakness that worsens with activity. Scheduling demanding tasks in the morning capitalizes on peak strength and conserves energy for essential activities.

stem: An OTA is providing scar management for a client 8 weeks post-burn injury. Which technique is appropriate?
options: Leaving the scar exposed without intervention
Scar massage, silicone gel sheeting, and compression garments to minimize hypertrophic scarring
Aggressive stretching that causes the scar to reopen
Applying ice to the scar continuously
rationale: Scar management post-burn includes massage to mobilize tissue, silicone gel to hydrate and flatten scars, and compression garments to prevent hypertrophic scarring during the remodeling phase.

stem: A client with Parkinson's disease has difficulty initiating movement to stand from a chair. Which strategy helps?
options: Have the client remain seated at all times
Use rhythmic auditory cueing and rocking technique to generate momentum for standing
Push the client to standing without warning
Provide a lower chair to increase difficulty
rationale: Rhythmic cueing addresses the initiation difficulty (akinesia) in Parkinson's disease. Rocking 1-2-3 with auditory cues helps generate momentum to overcome freezing during sit-to-stand.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a splint and notices the thermoplastic material is too close to the client's skin during heating. What is the PRIMARY risk?
options: The splint will be too thick
Thermal burn injury to the client's skin
The material will not conform properly
The splint will be too flexible
rationale: Heated thermoplastic material can cause thermal burns. The OTA must ensure proper temperature, use wet toweling as a barrier, and monitor skin throughout the molding process.

stem: A client with autism spectrum disorder demonstrates restricted interests and repetitive behaviors. How can the OTA use this therapeutically?
options: Eliminate all restricted interests from therapy
Incorporate the client's special interests as motivators within therapeutic activities
Only allow restricted interests outside of therapy
Redirect away from interests at every opportunity
rationale: Using a client's special interests as motivators increases engagement and participation in therapeutic activities, leveraging intrinsic motivation for skill development.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has had a below-knee amputation. What is the proper positioning for the residual limb when sitting?
options: Hanging the residual limb off the edge of the wheelchair
Supported on a wheelchair stump board in extension to prevent hip and knee flexion contractures
Flexed under the wheelchair at all times
Elevated above heart level at all times
rationale: The residual limb should be positioned in extension on a stump board to prevent hip and knee flexion contractures, which would impair future prosthetic use and ambulation.

stem: A client with a visual field cut on the left side repeatedly bumps into objects on the left. Which compensatory strategy should the OTA teach?
options: Cover the right eye to force left-side awareness
Teach systematic head turning and visual scanning to the left
Place all objects on the right side only
Wear dark glasses to reduce visual input
rationale: Compensatory strategies for left visual field cut include systematic head turning toward the affected side, anchoring techniques, and organized visual scanning patterns.

stem: An OTA is adapting a computer workstation for a client with C5-C6 tetraplegia. Which input device is MOST appropriate?
options: Mouth stick, voice recognition software, or trackball with universal cuff
rationale: C5-C6 tetraplegia preserves some shoulder and elbow function but limits hand function. Mouth sticks, voice recognition, and adapted pointing devices enable computer access.

stem: A client with fibromyalgia reports widespread pain and fatigue. Which OT approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Graded activity program with energy conservation, stress management, and gentle exercise
Complete bed rest until pain resolves
rationale: Fibromyalgia management includes graded activity to avoid boom-bust cycles, energy conservation, stress management techniques, sleep hygiene, and gentle low-impact exercise.

stem: An OTA is treating a child with sensory processing disorder who seeks excessive proprioceptive input. Which activity is appropriate?
options: Heavy work activities such as carrying weighted objects, wall push-ups, and pulling a wagon
Sitting quietly at a desk for extended periods
rationale: Proprioceptive seekers benefit from heavy work activities that provide deep pressure and joint compression, helping to organize the sensory system and improve self-regulation.

stem: A client had a stroke and presents with anosognosia. What does this mean?
options: The client is unaware of or denies their neurological deficits
The client has lost the ability to read
rationale: Anosognosia is unawareness or denial of one's own neurological deficit, common in right hemisphere stroke. The client may deny hemiplegia or neglect, posing significant safety risks.

stem: An OTA is working in an acute care setting. A client's oxygen saturation drops to 88% during a self-care activity. What should the OTA do?
options: Continue the activity to build endurance
Stop the activity, position the client in upright sitting, and notify nursing immediately
Increase the activity intensity to improve cardiovascular function
Document the reading and continue at a slower pace
rationale: Oxygen saturation below 90% is a critical threshold. The OTA must stop activity immediately, position for optimal breathing, and notify nursing for medical intervention.

stem: A client with chronic pain is catastrophizing about their condition. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Agree that the situation is hopeless
Use cognitive-behavioral strategies to identify and reframe maladaptive thoughts about pain
Discontinue therapy as pain management is not within OT scope
rationale: CBT-based approaches help clients identify catastrophizing thoughts and develop more balanced, realistic beliefs about their pain, improving coping and functional engagement.

stem: An OTA is assessing a client's upper extremity function using the Nine-Hole Peg Test. What does this test measure?
options: Fine motor dexterity and finger coordination speed
rationale: The Nine-Hole Peg Test is a timed measure of fine motor dexterity that assesses finger coordination speed. It is commonly used to track hand function in neurological conditions.

stem: A client with spinal cord injury develops a pressure ulcer on the ischial tuberosity. What modification should the OTA recommend?
options: Continue using the current wheelchair cushion
Assess and modify the wheelchair cushion system and increase pressure relief frequency
Have the client sit on a donut cushion
rationale: Pressure ulcer management requires reassessing the seating system, potentially upgrading to a pressure-redistributing cushion, and educating on more frequent weight shifts.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has Down syndrome and demonstrates hypotonia. Which positioning strategy supports feeding?
options: Upright seated position with head slightly flexed, hips at 90°, and feet supported
rationale: Children with hypotonia need stable positioning for feeding: upright with slight chin tuck (prevents aspiration), hips at 90°, and feet supported to provide a stable base.

stem: A client with an anterior cerebral artery (ACA) stroke presents with which deficit pattern?
options: Upper extremity weakness greater than lower extremity
Lower extremity weakness greater than upper extremity with personality changes
rationale: ACA stroke affects the medial cortical surface, causing contralateral lower extremity weakness greater than upper extremity, along with possible personality changes and urinary incontinence.

stem: An OTA is conducting a discharge planning session. The client lives alone and has moderate cognitive deficits. What is the PRIMARY consideration?
options: Whether the client can perform all IADLs independently
Safety in the home environment and the level of supervision or support services needed
The client's financial ability to afford equipment
The distance from the hospital to the client's home
rationale: For clients with moderate cognitive deficits living alone, safety is the primary discharge consideration, including supervision needs, emergency management, and environmental hazards.

stem: A client with a recent below-elbow amputation reports phantom limb pain. Which intervention may help?
options: Ignore the pain as it is not real
Mirror therapy, desensitization of the residual limb, and TENS application
Aggressive massage of the residual limb immediately post-surgery
Complete immobilization of the residual limb
rationale: Phantom limb pain is a real neurological phenomenon. Mirror therapy provides visual feedback to the brain, desensitization normalizes neural input, and TENS modulates pain signals.

stem: An OTA is training a client with hemiplegia in one-handed shoe tying. Which approach is MOST effective?
options: Teach a one-handed shoe tying method or recommend elastic shoelaces
Have the client only wear slip-on shoes forever
Wait until bilateral hand function returns before addressing footwear
rationale: One-handed shoe tying techniques or adaptive closures (elastic laces, Velcro straps) promote independence. Teaching compensation while still working toward recovery is best practice.

stem: A client with generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) is being seen in outpatient OT. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Exposure to maximum stress to build tolerance
Relaxation training, mindfulness, and graded exposure to anxiety-provoking occupations
Avoiding all activities that cause any anxiety
Only physical exercise without cognitive components
rationale: GAD intervention includes relaxation techniques (progressive muscle relaxation, deep breathing), mindfulness, and gradual exposure to anxiety-provoking activities to build coping capacity.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has a Zone II extensor tendon injury of the hand. Where is Zone II located?
rationale: Zone II of the extensor tendon system is located over the PIP joint. Injuries here can result in boutonnière deformity if the central slip is disrupted.

stem: A client with cognitive deficits post-TBI is working on meal preparation. The OTA observes the client leaving the stove burner on after cooking. What is the BEST intervention?
options: Remove cooking activities from the treatment plan
Implement compensatory strategies such as a timer, checklist, and stove auto-shutoff device
Allow the client to continue without modification to build awareness
Only practice cold meal preparation with no stove use
rationale: Compensatory strategies (checklists, timers, auto-shutoff devices) address safety while maintaining the client's participation in meaningful cooking activities.

stem: An OTA is providing seating evaluation for a client with scoliosis who uses a wheelchair. Which cushion feature is MOST important?
options: Contoured cushion with lateral trunk supports to accommodate and support the spinal curvature
No cushion — the seat sling alone is sufficient
rationale: Scoliosis requires contoured seating with lateral trunk supports to accommodate the spinal curvature, provide postural support, distribute pressure evenly, and prevent progression.

stem: A client with a metacarpal fracture is wearing a hand-based splint. At the follow-up visit, the OTA notices increased swelling and the client reports numbness in the fingers. What should the OTA do?
options: Reassure the client that swelling is normal
Remove the splint immediately, check circulation, and contact the supervising OT or physician
Tighten the straps for better immobilization
rationale: Increased swelling with numbness suggests the splint is too tight, possibly compromising circulation. Immediate removal, circulation check, and medical notification are essential.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has difficulty crossing midline. Which activity addresses this skill?
options: Drawing within a small space directly in front of the child
Painting on a large easel with sweeping strokes that cross from one side to the other
Writing on the right side of the paper only
Stacking blocks in a single column
rationale: Crossing midline activities require the child to reach across the body's center. Painting with sweeping horizontal strokes across a large easel naturally promotes midline crossing.

stem: A client with Parkinson's disease has micrographia. Which strategy helps improve handwriting?
options: Use a very thin pen to reduce effort
Use visual cues (lined paper with wide spacing) and practice large-amplitude writing
Switch to writing with the non-dominant hand
rationale: Micrographia in Parkinson's responds to external visual cues (wide-lined paper, target letters) and conscious large-amplitude writing practice to counteract progressive letter size reduction.

stem: An OTA is treating a burn patient who has neck and anterior chest burns. What position should be maintained to prevent contracture?
options: Neck flexion with chin to chest
Neck extension with no pillow, avoiding neck flexion contracture
Neck lateral flexion to the burned side
rationale: Anterior neck and chest burns contract into flexion. Anti-deformity positioning requires neck extension (no pillow) to counteract the flexion contracture force of healing scar tissue.

stem: A client receiving home health OT asks the OTA to perform a task unrelated to the treatment plan (e.g., cleaning the house). What should the OTA do?
options: Perform the task to maintain a good therapeutic relationship
Politely decline, explain that OTA services must relate to the treatment plan, and redirect to appropriate resources
Clean the house but document it as a therapeutic activity
rationale: OTA services must be related to the established treatment plan and be skilled interventions. The OTA should decline non-treatment tasks and help the client find appropriate resources.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has left unilateral neglect post-stroke. During mealtime, the client only eats food on the right side of the plate. Which intervention is appropriate?
options: Serve food only on the right side to accommodate the neglect
Use visual anchoring cues on the left side and train the client to scan left before eating
Remove all food from the left side of the plate
Do not address eating as it is not within OT scope
rationale: Visual anchoring (a colored stripe or marker on the left) cues the client to scan toward the neglected side. Systematic scanning training promotes awareness of the left visual field.

stem: A client with PTSD becomes triggered during an OT session and begins experiencing a flashback. What should the OTA do FIRST?
options: Continue the session to work through the trigger
Use grounding techniques (e.g., deep breathing, sensory awareness) to help orient the client to the present
Leave the client alone until they calm down
Physically restrain the client for safety
rationale: Grounding techniques help orient the client to the present moment during a flashback. Deep breathing, naming objects in the room, and tactile grounding provide sensory anchoring.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with swan neck deformity of the finger. Which splint is indicated?
options: Silver ring splint or figure-of-eight splint at the PIP joint to prevent hyperextension
rationale: Swan neck deformity involves PIP hyperextension and DIP flexion. A silver ring or figure-of-eight splint at the PIP prevents hyperextension while allowing functional flexion.

stem: A client with intellectual disability is learning to use public transportation. Which teaching method is MOST effective?
options: Use systematic instruction with task analysis, repeated practice on actual routes, and visual supports
Have the client memorize a map before attempting
Only allow accompanied travel with no independent practice
rationale: Systematic instruction breaks complex tasks into steps, uses repeated real-world practice, and incorporates visual supports to build independence in community mobility.

stem: An OTA is treating a client 2 days post total knee replacement. What is the primary OT goal at this stage?
options: Independent transfers, safe mobility with assistive device, and basic self-care
rationale: Early post-TKR OT goals focus on safe transfers, basic mobility with appropriate assistive device, and independent basic self-care activities to facilitate safe discharge.

stem: A child with cerebral palsy demonstrates a persistent asymmetric tonic neck reflex (ATNR) at age 3. What does this indicate?
options: Normal development — ATNR persists throughout life
Abnormal reflex retention that interferes with bilateral activities, rolling, and midline hand use
The child is developing ahead of schedule
This reflex is unrelated to motor function
rationale: ATNR should integrate by 6 months. Persistence at age 3 is abnormal and interferes with bilateral hand use, rolling, and midline crossing — all critical for development.

stem: An OTA notices a client with diabetes has a small wound on their foot during a self-care session. What should the OTA do?
options: Ignore it as foot care is not within OT scope
Report the wound to nursing or the supervising OT immediately, as diabetic wounds require prompt medical attention
Apply a bandage and continue the session
Tell the client to check it at their next doctor visit
rationale: Diabetic foot wounds carry high risk for infection, poor healing, and potential amputation. The OTA must report immediately for prompt medical evaluation and wound care.

stem: A client with radial nerve palsy presents with wrist drop. Which splint is indicated?
options: Resting hand splint in full flexion
Dynamic wrist extension splint to support the wrist in functional position
rationale: Radial nerve palsy causes wrist drop due to extensor paralysis. A dynamic wrist extension splint supports the wrist in a functional position while allowing finger use and tendon gliding.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has apraxia of speech following a stroke. How can the OTA support communication during OT sessions?
options: Use gestures, written choices, picture boards, and allow extra response time
Ignore communication difficulties and proceed normally
Only work on speech production exercises
rationale: Supporting communication in apraxia of speech includes multimodal strategies: gestures, written choices, picture boards, and providing adequate response time without pressuring the client.

stem: A client with shoulder adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) is in the frozen stage. Which intervention is appropriate?
options: Aggressive manipulation under anesthesia by the OTA
Gentle ROM within pain tolerance, joint mobilization within protocol, and modalities for pain management
No intervention until the thawing stage
rationale: In the frozen stage, intervention focuses on maintaining available ROM with gentle stretching, pain management with modalities, and joint mobilization within the established protocol.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has hemianopsia and wants to return to reading. Which adaptation is MOST helpful?
options: Use a typoscope or line guide and teach systematic scanning toward the affected visual field
Read only with one eye closed
rationale: A typoscope isolates individual lines of text, while systematic scanning training teaches the client to compensate for the visual field loss, enabling functional reading.

stem: A client with spastic cerebral palsy has an adducted, internally rotated thumb (thumb-in-palm deformity). Which splint addresses this?
options: Thumb abduction splint positioning the thumb out of the palm
Resting hand splint with all fingers in flexion
rationale: Thumb-in-palm deformity requires a splint that positions the thumb in abduction and extension, opening the web space to enable functional grasp and prevent contracture.

stem: An OTA is providing caregiver training for a family member of a client with advanced dementia. What is the MOST important content?
options: Complex rehabilitation exercises for the caregiver to administer
Safe handling techniques, simple communication strategies, and environmental safety modifications
How to perform a formal cognitive assessment
rationale: Caregiver training for advanced dementia focuses on practical skills: safe transfers, simplified communication, behavioral management, and home safety to reduce caregiver burden.

stem: A client with a peripheral nerve injury of the hand reports hypersensitivity to touch. Which desensitization protocol is appropriate?
options: Avoid all touch to the area indefinitely
Graded texture exposure starting with soft textures and progressing to rougher textures
Apply maximum pressure immediately to the area
Use only ice immersion for desensitization
rationale: Desensitization uses graded exposure to textures, starting with tolerable soft textures and progressing to rougher ones as tolerance improves, normalizing sensory processing.

stem: An OTA is working with a client on meal planning as an IADL. The client has executive function deficits. Which strategy is MOST helpful?
options: Have the client spontaneously plan meals without support
Provide a structured template, visual recipe cards, and step-by-step checklists
Plan all meals for the client without their involvement
Only work on basic ADLs as IADLs are too complex
rationale: Executive function deficits impair planning and organization. Structured templates, visual supports, and step-by-step checklists externalize the organizational process.

stem: A client with a mallet finger deformity (DIP extensor tendon rupture) needs which splint?
options: Continuous DIP extension splint worn 24 hours for 6-8 weeks
rationale: Mallet finger requires continuous DIP extension splinting for 6-8 weeks to allow the extensor tendon to heal. Even momentary DIP flexion can restart the healing timeline.

stem: An OTA is using the Allen Cognitive Level Screen (ACLS) with a client. The client completes the running stitch and the whip stitch but cannot do the single cordovan stitch. What is the approximate ACL?
rationale: Completing running and whip stitches but not single cordovan corresponds to approximately ACL 3.8-4.0, indicating the client can perform familiar goal-directed activities with visual cues.

stem: A client with a recent hip fracture repair is anxious about falling during transfers. Which approach is MOST therapeutic?
options: Push the client to transfer independently immediately
Grade the transfer activity, provide verbal reassurance, use a gait belt, and gradually reduce assist level
Allow the client to avoid all transfers
Have family perform all transfers permanently
rationale: Grading transfers from maximum assist to minimum assist with safety equipment (gait belt) builds confidence. Verbal reassurance and gradual progression address fear of falling effectively.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has difficulty with visual perception, specifically figure-ground discrimination. Which activity addresses this?
options: Finding hidden objects in a busy visual scene or completing hidden picture puzzles
Listening to stories with eyes closed
Free play without any structured activity
rationale: Figure-ground discrimination involves distinguishing an object from a busy background. Hidden picture puzzles and finding objects in cluttered scenes directly train this visual perceptual skill.

stem: A client with cervical spinal stenosis is experiencing bilateral hand numbness and clumsiness. Which sign suggests myelopathy?
options: Hyperreflexia, positive Hoffman's sign, and gait disturbance
rationale: Cervical myelopathy presents with upper motor neuron signs: hyperreflexia, positive Hoffman's sign, gait disturbance, and bilateral hand dysfunction requiring urgent medical evaluation.

stem: An OTA is treating a client with lateral trunk flexion in a wheelchair. Which postural support should be added?
options: Lateral trunk supports (pads) positioned at the rib cage to maintain upright trunk alignment
Larger rear wheels for the wheelchair
rationale: Lateral trunk supports at rib cage level provide external support to correct lateral trunk flexion, maintaining midline positioning and improving upper extremity function.

stem: A client with an eating disorder is participating in a meal preparation group. The client becomes visibly anxious when handling food. What should the OTA do?
options: Force the client to continue the activity
Acknowledge the anxiety, offer a modified role in the group, and use coping strategies
Remove the client from the group permanently
Ignore the anxiety and proceed with the activity
rationale: Acknowledging anxiety validates the client's experience. Offering a modified role maintains participation while using coping strategies builds tolerance and self-regulation skills.

stem: An OTA is providing tendon gliding exercises for a client post carpal tunnel release surgery. Which sequence is correct?
options: Straight → hook fist → full fist → tabletop → straight fist
Passive wrist flexion and extension only
rationale: Tendon gliding exercises follow a specific sequence (straight, hook, full fist, tabletop, straight fist) to maximize differential gliding of the flexor tendons and prevent adhesions.

stem: A client with a brain tumor is experiencing progressive cognitive decline. How should the OTA adjust the intervention approach?
options: Increase complexity to challenge the client
Shift to compensatory and environmental modification strategies as restorative gains become limited
Discharge the client as they cannot benefit from OT
Continue the same treatment plan regardless of decline
rationale: Progressive conditions require shifting from restorative to compensatory approaches. Environmental modifications, caregiver training, and adaptive strategies maintain safety and quality of life.

stem: An OTA is working with a client who has boutonnière deformity. What is the characteristic position of the finger?
options: MCP flexion with PIP and DIP extension
rationale: Boutonnière deformity involves PIP flexion and DIP hyperextension caused by disruption of the central slip of the extensor tendon, allowing lateral bands to migrate volar to the PIP.

stem: A client is recovering from a total shoulder replacement (anterior approach). Which movement must be AVOIDED during the initial recovery phase?
options: Shoulder extension combined with external rotation
rationale: Anterior approach total shoulder replacement requires avoiding combined shoulder extension and external rotation, which stresses the anterior capsule and subscapularis repair.

stem: An OTA is facilitating a life skills group for adolescents with behavioral health conditions. Which activity is MOST developmentally appropriate?
options: Coloring books designed for young children
Budgeting simulation, job interview practice, and social skills role-play
rationale: Adolescent life skills groups should include age-appropriate activities like budgeting, job readiness, and social skills training that prepare for transition to independent adult roles.

stem: A client with cubital tunnel syndrome has numbness in the ring and small fingers. Which nerve is compressed?
rationale: Cubital tunnel syndrome involves ulnar nerve compression at the elbow, causing numbness and tingling in the ring and small fingers, and weakness of intrinsic hand muscles.

stem: An OTA is discharging a client from outpatient hand therapy. Which documentation element is MOST critical?
options: The client's favorite activities during therapy
Objective comparison of initial and discharge measurements demonstrating functional outcomes
The number of sessions attended only
The OTA's personal opinion about the client's motivation
rationale: Discharge documentation must include objective comparison of initial and final measurements, demonstrating measurable functional outcomes that justify the skilled services provided.

stem: A 72-year-old patient recovering from a left CVA demonstrates difficulty sequencing steps when donning a button-down shirt. The OTA observes the patient attempting to button before placing arms in sleeves. Which cognitive deficit is MOST likely contributing to this difficulty?
rationale: Apraxia is a motor planning disorder that impairs the ability to sequence and execute purposeful movements despite intact motor function. The patient's difficulty with sequencing dressing steps (attempting to button before inserting arms) is characteristic of ideational apraxia. Agnosia involves recognition deficits, hemianopsia is a visual field cut, and ataxia involves coordination problems.

stem: An OTA is working with a patient who had a right hip replacement using a posterolateral approach. During dressing training, which technique should the OTA instruct the patient to use when donning pants?
options: Use a reacher to bring pants over the operated leg first while maintaining hip precautions
Stand and step into pants with the non-operated leg first
Sit on a low surface and cross the operated leg over the non-operated leg
Bend forward past 90 degrees to reach the feet
rationale: Following posterolateral hip replacement, patients must observe hip precautions: no flexion beyond 90 degrees, no internal rotation, and no adduction past midline. Using a reacher allows the patient to bring the pants over the operated leg first while seated, without violating hip precautions. Crossing legs, bending past 90 degrees, and standing to step in all risk dislocation.

stem: A patient with C6 tetraplegia is learning to feed independently. The OTA should recommend which of the following adaptive equipment setups?
options: Universal cuff with built-up handle utensils and a plate guard
Standard utensils with a non-slip mat only
Weighted utensils with a regular plate
Long-handled utensils with a swivel spoon
rationale: A patient with C6 tetraplegia has wrist extension but lacks finger flexion and grip strength. A universal cuff compensates for absent grasp by holding the utensil against the palm. Built-up handles increase surface area for tenodesis grasp, and a plate guard prevents food from sliding off the plate. Standard utensils require grip that C6 patients lack. Weighted utensils address tremor, not grip. Long-handled utensils address reach limitations.

stem: An OTA is performing a kitchen task assessment with a patient who has mild cognitive impairment. The patient successfully gathers ingredients but leaves the stove burner on after cooking. Which level of cognitive assistance does this patient MOST likely require?
options: Supervision for safety awareness during IADL tasks
Maximum assistance for all meal preparation tasks
Independent status with no follow-up needed
Total dependence requiring full caregiver meal preparation
rationale: The patient demonstrates ability to perform task components (gathering ingredients, cooking) but shows impaired safety awareness by leaving the burner on. This indicates a need for supervision level of assistance, where the caregiver provides cues for safety without physical help. Maximum assistance and total dependence overestimate the deficit. Independent status ignores the safety concern.

stem: A patient with bilateral upper extremity weakness from Guillain-Barré syndrome reports difficulty with morning hygiene tasks. The OTA should FIRST address which of the following?
options: Energy conservation techniques and task modification for grooming
Transfer training from bed to bathroom
rationale: In Guillain-Barré syndrome, fatigue management is paramount as the condition involves progressive weakness with potential for recovery. Energy conservation techniques and task modification (such as sitting during grooming, using lightweight tools) directly address the functional complaint. Resistive exercises may be premature during acute weakness. Transfer training is not the reported concern. Cognitive strategies are unnecessary as cognition is typically intact in GBS.

stem: An OTA is recommending assistive technology for a patient with ALS who has progressive upper extremity weakness but intact cognition. The patient wants to continue using a computer for work. Which device would be MOST appropriate at this stage?
options: Voice recognition software with adapted mouse alternatives
rationale: For a patient with ALS and progressive bilateral UE weakness, voice recognition software addresses the declining motor function while capitalizing on intact cognition and speech (which is typically preserved longer). Adapted mouse alternatives such as head tracking or eye gaze systems provide additional input methods as weakness progresses. Standard keyboard, handwriting tablets, and one-handed keyboards all require upper extremity motor function that will continue to decline.

stem: A patient with rheumatoid arthritis affecting the hands reports significant pain during meal preparation. Which adaptive equipment combination would BEST protect the joints while maintaining function?
options: Built-up grip utensils, jar opener, and ergonomic knife with rocker blade
Standard utensils with rubber grips added
Long-handled reacher and standard cutting board
rationale: Joint protection principles for RA include using the largest joint possible, distributing force over multiple joints, and reducing grip force. Built-up grip utensils reduce the force needed to grasp, a jar opener eliminates the twisting force on MCP and IP joints, and an ergonomic rocker knife allows cutting with a rocking motion instead of gripping and sawing. The combination addresses multiple joint protection principles during meal preparation.

stem: An OTA is evaluating wheelchair positioning for a patient with a T10 spinal cord injury. The patient reports increased pressure on the ischial tuberosities during prolonged sitting. Which seating modification should the OTA recommend FIRST?
options: Pressure-relieving cushion with gel or air flotation and pressure mapping assessment
Elevating leg rests to redistribute weight
Wider wheelchair frame to allow more movement
rationale: For a patient with T10 SCI, pressure injury prevention is critical due to absent sensation below the injury level. A pressure-relieving cushion (gel, air flotation, or hybrid) directly addresses the increased pressure on bony prominences. Pressure mapping provides objective data to optimize cushion selection. Reclining alone does not address seat surface pressure. Elevating leg rests may help but do not address the primary concern. A wider frame could worsen positioning and increase shearing forces.

stem: A patient with low vision secondary to macular degeneration needs to manage medications independently. Which assistive device should the OTA recommend?
options: Talking prescription reader and high-contrast pill organizer with large compartments
Standard weekly pill box with small print labels
Magnifying glass only for reading labels
Color-coded system using subtle pastel colors
rationale: Macular degeneration causes loss of central vision, making reading small print extremely difficult. A talking prescription reader uses audio output to bypass the visual deficit. A high-contrast pill organizer with large compartments leverages remaining peripheral vision and tactile discrimination. A standard pill box with small print would be unreadable. Magnification alone may be insufficient for advanced macular degeneration. Subtle pastel colors lack sufficient contrast for low vision patients.

stem: An OTA is training a patient with a unilateral below-elbow amputation to use a body-powered prosthesis. During training, the patient demonstrates difficulty operating the terminal device. Which movement pattern should the OTA emphasize?
options: Glenohumeral flexion with scapular abduction to operate the cable control system
Elbow flexion and extension on the residual limb
Wrist rotation movements at the prosthetic wrist unit
Finger opposition patterns using the terminal device manually
rationale: Body-powered prostheses use a cable control system activated primarily by glenohumeral flexion and scapular abduction (biscapular abduction). This movement creates tension on the cable that opens the terminal device. Elbow movements on the residual limb do not activate the cable system in a below-elbow prosthesis. Wrist rotation is a separate passive adjustment. Manual finger operation defeats the purpose of learning prosthetic control.

stem: An OTA is treating a patient with a traumatic brain injury who demonstrates difficulty with divided attention. Which activity would be MOST appropriate for addressing this deficit?
options: Preparing a simple recipe while carrying on a conversation
Sorting cards by color in a quiet room
Listening to a story and answering questions afterward
rationale: Divided attention requires simultaneously attending to two or more tasks. Preparing a recipe while conversing requires the patient to manage multiple streams of information (following steps, monitoring cooking, engaging in dialogue). Card sorting addresses selective attention or categorization. Copying designs targets visual-motor integration. Listening and answering questions assesses sustained attention and memory, not divided attention.

stem: A patient with early-stage Alzheimer's disease is having difficulty remembering to take medications and keep appointments. The OTA should implement which compensatory strategy?
options: External memory aids including a structured daily schedule, alarms, and visual checklists
Repetitive memory drills to strengthen internal recall
Complex mnemonic systems requiring multi-step learning
Cognitive exercises focused on improving working memory capacity
rationale: In Alzheimer's disease, progressive cognitive decline makes restorative approaches (memory drills, mnemonics, cognitive exercises) largely ineffective for long-term benefit. Compensatory strategies using external aids (written schedules, alarms, visual checklists) bypass the impaired memory system and provide environmental supports. These approaches capitalize on preserved procedural memory and habit formation while reducing demands on impaired declarative memory.

stem: An OTA is using the dynamic interactional approach with a patient who has executive function deficits following a stroke. The patient consistently fails to check work for errors. Which intervention strategy aligns with this approach?
options: Guided self-monitoring through structured self-questioning techniques during task performance
Having the therapist identify all errors and correct them for the patient
Removing all complex tasks and only providing simple activities
Providing only written instructions without additional processing strategies
rationale: The dynamic interactional approach, developed by Toglia, emphasizes metacognitive strategy training including self-monitoring and self-evaluation. Guided self-questioning teaches patients to pause and check their own work, promoting awareness of errors and building self-regulation skills. Having the therapist correct errors does not build self-monitoring. Avoiding complex tasks limits functional carryover. Written instructions alone do not address the metacognitive deficit.

stem: A patient recovering from a mild TBI reports difficulty concentrating in noisy environments such as the hospital cafeteria. Using a graded approach, what should the OTA do FIRST?
options: Practice attention tasks in a quiet environment and gradually introduce controlled distractions
Immediately practice tasks in the cafeteria to promote real-world adaptation
Recommend the patient avoid all noisy environments permanently
Focus exclusively on physical rehabilitation goals until cognition improves spontaneously
rationale: A graded approach to cognitive rehabilitation starts with tasks at the patient's current ability level and systematically increases demands. Beginning in a quiet environment establishes a baseline of success, then gradually introducing distractions builds tolerance and coping strategies. Immediate exposure to high-stimulation environments may overwhelm the patient. Permanent avoidance is not therapeutic. Waiting for spontaneous improvement ignores the benefit of structured intervention.

stem: An OTA observes that a patient with right hemisphere stroke consistently ignores food on the left side of the plate and bumps into objects on the left side. Which intervention approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Visual scanning training with anchoring techniques using a bright marker on the left side
Patching the right eye to force left eye use
Placing all items on the right side to compensate
Cognitive behavioral therapy for denial of deficit
rationale: Left unilateral neglect following right hemisphere stroke requires systematic visual scanning training. Anchoring techniques use a bright visual cue (red line, colored tape) on the neglected side to provide a starting reference point for scanning. The patient learns to find the anchor first, then scan from left to right. Patching is used for diplopia, not neglect. Placing items only on the right reinforces neglect. CBT does not address the neurological basis of neglect.

stem: An OTA is fabricating a resting hand splint for a patient with flexor tendon tightness following a distal radius fracture. The wrist should be positioned in which position?
options: Neutral to slight extension (10-20 degrees) with fingers in slight flexion at all joints
Full wrist flexion with fingers fully extended
Full wrist extension (45 degrees) with fingers tightly flexed
Ulnar deviation with thumb in full opposition
rationale: A resting hand splint positions the hand in a functional position to prevent deformity and maintain tissue length. Neutral to slight wrist extension (10-20 degrees) places the flexors on gentle stretch without overstressing healing tissues. Slight flexion at the MCP, PIP, and DIP joints maintains the collateral ligament length. Full flexion or extension positions could cause contracture. Ulnar deviation is not a functional resting position.

stem: A patient is 6 weeks post flexor tendon repair in zone II of the middle finger. The surgeon has cleared the patient for active motion. The OTA notices the patient has a 30-degree PIP joint extension lag. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Isolated FDS gliding exercises with the adjacent fingers held in extension
Aggressive passive extension stretching of the PIP joint
Immobilization of the PIP joint in full extension
rationale: At 6 weeks post flexor tendon repair, the tendon has sufficient healing for active motion but not aggressive resistance. Isolated FDS gliding exercises (blocking adjacent fingers in extension) promote differential tendon gliding to reduce adhesions that contribute to the extension lag. Aggressive passive extension could rupture the repair. Resistive strengthening is premature at 6 weeks. Immobilization would worsen adhesion formation.

stem: An OTA is treating a patient with carpal tunnel syndrome who reports numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers that worsens at night. Which splinting intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Wrist cock-up splint positioning the wrist in neutral to be worn at night
Dynamic MCP flexion splint worn during the day
Ulnar gutter splint immobilizing the ring and small fingers
Thumb spica splint with wrist in full flexion
rationale: Carpal tunnel syndrome involves compression of the median nerve in the carpal tunnel. A wrist cock-up splint in neutral position minimizes pressure within the carpal tunnel by preventing wrist flexion and extension, which both increase carpal tunnel pressure. Night splinting is particularly effective because many people sleep with wrists flexed, exacerbating symptoms. The affected fingers (thumb, index, middle) correspond to median nerve distribution. Other splints address different conditions.

stem: A patient with a boutonniere deformity of the ring finger presents with PIP flexion and DIP hyperextension. The OTA should fabricate which type of splint?
options: PIP extension splint maintaining the PIP in full extension while allowing DIP flexion
DIP extension splint with PIP free to move
Full finger immobilization splint with all joints in flexion
Dynamic MCP flexion splint with PIP and DIP free
rationale: Boutonniere deformity results from disruption of the central slip of the extensor mechanism, causing PIP flexion and compensatory DIP hyperextension. Treatment requires splinting the PIP joint in full extension to allow the central slip to heal while permitting active DIP flexion exercises to prevent lateral band tightness. A DIP splint does not address the primary PIP deformity. Full immobilization prevents needed DIP motion. An MCP splint does not address the involved joints.

stem: During a hand therapy session, an OTA notices that a patient's custom thermoplastic splint has a pressure area causing skin redness over the ulnar styloid. What should the OTA do FIRST?
options: Flare out the splint material over the bony prominence to relieve pressure
Add extra padding over the reddened area and continue use
Discontinue the splint permanently and notify the OTR
Tell the patient redness is normal and will resolve on its own
rationale: Pressure areas over bony prominences require immediate splint modification. Flaring out (bubbling) the thermoplastic material over the ulnar styloid creates space and eliminates direct pressure on the bony landmark. This is a standard splint adjustment within the OTA's scope. Adding padding can actually increase pressure by reducing space. Permanent discontinuation is premature when modification can solve the problem. Ignoring skin redness risks pressure injury development.

stem: An OTA is working with a 4-year-old child with developmental coordination disorder who has difficulty with handwriting and using scissors. According to developmental principles, which foundational skill should the OTA address FIRST?
options: Proximal stability and shoulder strength through weight-bearing and climbing activities
Letter formation practice with pencil and paper
Cutting complex shapes with adapted scissors
Keyboarding skills as an alternative to handwriting
