rationale: Motor development follows a proximal-to-distal pattern. Before refined distal skills (handwriting, cutting) can develop, the child needs adequate proximal stability at the shoulder girdle and trunk. Weight-bearing activities (wheelbarrow walking, climbing, push-ups) build the proximal stability foundation necessary for controlled distal movements. Addressing handwriting or cutting directly without proximal stability would be working at a level the child is not developmentally ready for.

stem: A 7-year-old child with autism spectrum disorder becomes extremely distressed during transitions between classroom activities. The OTA should recommend which strategy to the teacher?
options: Visual schedule with countdown timer and advance warnings before transitions
Removing the child from the classroom during all transitions
Allowing the child to skip any activities that cause distress
Providing no preparation and expecting the child to adapt independently
rationale: Children with ASD often struggle with transitions due to difficulty with flexibility, prediction, and changes in routine. A visual schedule provides predictability by showing the sequence of activities. A countdown timer and advance warnings give the child time to prepare mentally for the change. This approach uses visual supports (a strength for many children with ASD) and structured predictability. Removing the child or avoiding activities limits participation. Expecting unaided adaptation ignores the core deficit.

stem: An OTA is treating a 3-year-old with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy. During a feeding session, the child demonstrates a strong tonic bite reflex when the spoon touches the teeth. What is the SAFEST approach?
options: Present the spoon from the front, apply gentle downward pressure on the tongue, and wait for the reflex to release
Force the spoon out by pulling quickly to avoid prolonging the reflex
Use a metal spoon for durability during bite episodes
Discontinue oral feeding and recommend tube feeding immediately
rationale: The tonic bite reflex causes involuntary sustained jaw closure when the teeth or gums are stimulated. Safe management includes presenting the spoon from the front (avoiding lateral gum stimulation), applying gentle downward pressure on the tongue to inhibit the reflex, and waiting calmly for the jaw to release. Pulling the spoon forcefully can injure the oral structures or break teeth. Metal spoons risk dental damage. Discontinuing oral feeding is premature without exploring management strategies.

stem: An OTA is evaluating a 5-year-old's pencil grasp and observes the child using a fisted grasp with the forearm in pronation. According to typical development, this grasp pattern is characteristic of which age range?
options: 1-2 years old, indicating a significant delay for a 5-year-old
3-4 years old, indicating a mild delay
rationale: A palmar-supinate (fisted) grasp with full forearm pronation is typical of children around 1-2 years of age. By age 5, children should have developed through digital pronate grasp (2-3 years), static tripod grasp (3.5-4 years), and approaching a dynamic tripod grasp. A fisted grasp at age 5 represents a significant developmental delay in fine motor skills and warrants further assessment and intervention.

stem: An OTA working in early intervention is treating an 18-month-old with Down syndrome. The child is beginning to explore self-feeding but has difficulty bringing a spoon to the mouth. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Hand-over-hand guidance during spoon use with gradual fading of support
Spoon-feeding the child exclusively until age 3
Providing only finger foods and eliminating spoon use
Waiting until the child independently demonstrates spoon interest
rationale: Hand-over-hand guidance provides the proprioceptive and kinesthetic input needed to learn the motor pattern of spoon-to-mouth while allowing the child to participate actively. Gradual fading (moving from hand-over-hand to hand-under-hand to wrist support to independence) follows motor learning principles. Exclusive caregiver feeding delays skill development. Eliminating spoons avoids the developmental challenge. Waiting for spontaneous skill emergence ignores the developmental delay.

stem: An OTA is leading a group session in an acute psychiatric unit. A patient with schizophrenia becomes increasingly agitated, stands up, and begins pacing. The OTA should FIRST take which action?
options: Calmly acknowledge the patient's distress and offer a choice to step out briefly or take a quiet role
Firmly instruct the patient to sit down immediately
Ignore the behavior and continue with the group activity
Physically guide the patient back to the seat
rationale: In acute psychiatric settings, de-escalation techniques are essential for safety. Calmly acknowledging the patient's emotional state validates their experience without reinforcing the behavior. Offering choices (stepping out briefly or taking a quieter role) provides the patient with a sense of control and an acceptable outlet. Demanding compliance can escalate agitation. Ignoring the behavior may increase it and affect group safety. Physical contact with an agitated psychiatric patient can trigger a combative response.

stem: A patient with major depressive disorder in an inpatient setting has been refusing to participate in all therapy sessions for three days. The OTA should take which approach?
options: Offer a brief, low-demand task at bedside to establish therapeutic rapport and build engagement gradually
Document non-compliance and discharge the patient from OT services
Wait until the patient voluntarily requests therapy
Schedule the patient for the most challenging group sessions to motivate participation
rationale: Depression is characterized by low motivation, anhedonia, and withdrawal. A therapeutic approach involves meeting the patient where they are by offering low-demand, accessible tasks at bedside. This reduces barriers to participation, begins building therapeutic rapport, and may initiate engagement through small successes. Documenting non-compliance without adapting the approach is not client-centered. Waiting passively does not fulfill the therapeutic role. High-demand activities would be overwhelming and counterproductive.

stem: An OTA is co-leading a social skills group for adolescents with anxiety disorders. One participant experiences a panic attack during role-playing. The MOST appropriate immediate response is to:
options: Guide the adolescent through grounding techniques such as deep breathing and sensory awareness
Continue the role-play and address the panic attack after the session
Send the adolescent to the emergency department immediately
Tell the adolescent their fear is irrational and they should try again
rationale: Panic attacks are acute episodes of intense fear with physical symptoms (rapid heart rate, shortness of breath, dizziness). Grounding techniques including deep breathing (4-7-8 technique or box breathing) and sensory awareness (5-4-3-2-1 technique) help activate the parasympathetic nervous system and redirect attention from anxiety symptoms. Continuing the activity ignores the acute distress. Emergency department referral is not warranted for a panic attack without medical emergency. Dismissing the fear is invalidating and could worsen anxiety.

stem: An OTA is developing a community reintegration program for a patient with bipolar disorder who is preparing for discharge. Which activity is MOST appropriate to include?
options: Practice managing a daily routine including medication management, budgeting, and using public transportation
Advanced academic coursework to keep the patient intellectually stimulated
rationale: Community reintegration for bipolar disorder requires practical skill-building in areas commonly disrupted during mood episodes. Managing a daily routine addresses structure and predictability, medication management supports adherence (a common challenge in bipolar disorder), budgeting addresses potential impulsive spending, and public transportation supports community access. These are functional, occupation-based activities directly related to successful community living. The other options do not address the core functional deficits.

stem: During a craft group in a behavioral health setting, a patient with borderline personality disorder becomes angry and threatens to leave after receiving feedback from another group member. The OTA should:
options: Validate the patient's emotional response while maintaining group boundaries and encouraging the patient to express feelings appropriately
Allow the patient to leave without any intervention
Confront the patient about overreacting in front of the group
End the group session immediately to prevent conflict
rationale: Patients with borderline personality disorder characteristically struggle with emotional regulation, interpersonal sensitivity, and abandonment fears. Validation acknowledges the emotional experience without reinforcing maladaptive behavior. Maintaining boundaries provides the therapeutic structure these patients need. Encouraging appropriate expression models healthy coping. Allowing unaddressed departure may reinforce avoidance. Public confrontation is damaging. Ending the group punishes all members and disrupts therapeutic process.

stem: An OTA is treating a 6-year-old child with sensory processing disorder who is hypersensitive to tactile input. The child refuses to touch finger paint, play dough, or textured food. Using a sensory integration approach, the OTA should:
options: Provide a sequence of graded tactile activities starting with the child's preferred textures and gradually introducing new ones
Force the child to touch all avoided textures immediately to desensitize
Avoid all tactile activities entirely and focus only on visual tasks
Use only deep pressure input without any direct tactile exploration
rationale: Sensory integration therapy uses a graded approach, beginning with textures the child tolerates and systematically introducing new sensory experiences within a supportive context. This allows the nervous system to adapt gradually while maintaining the child's sense of safety and control. Forced exposure can trigger fight-or-flight responses and increase aversion. Avoiding tactile activities limits development. Deep pressure alone addresses modulation but does not build tactile discrimination or tolerance.

stem: A patient with sensory processing difficulties following a stroke demonstrates gravitational insecurity, refusing to tilt backward in the wheelchair for pressure relief. The OTA should implement which strategy?
options: Supported slow tilting with predictable movement and verbal preparation, progressing gradually
Rapidly tilting the wheelchair backward without warning to overcome the fear
Eliminating all tilting and using only lateral weight shifts for pressure relief
Referring to psychology for phobia treatment before continuing OT
rationale: Gravitational insecurity is an overresponse to vestibular input, particularly position changes. Treatment involves providing predictable, controlled movement with adequate support. Verbal preparation reduces anxiety by making the movement expected. Gradual progression allows the vestibular system to adapt. Rapid, unexpected tilting would intensify the fear response. Eliminating tilting avoids a necessary functional skill. Psychology referral is not the first-line approach for a sensory-based vestibular response.

stem: An OTA is creating a sensory diet for a child who is a sensory seeker, constantly crashing into furniture, seeking rough play, and chewing on non-food items. Which activities should be included in the sensory diet?
options: Heavy work activities such as carrying weighted items, wall push-ups, and chew tubes for oral sensory input
Light touch activities such as feather brushing and tickling
Passive sensory activities such as watching calming videos
Eliminating all physical activity to reduce seeking behaviors
rationale: Sensory seekers have high sensory thresholds and need intense proprioceptive and deep pressure input to achieve optimal arousal. Heavy work activities (carrying, pushing, pulling, climbing) provide organizing proprioceptive input. Chew tubes offer a safe oral motor outlet, replacing non-food chewing. Light touch is alerting and may increase dysregulation. Passive activities do not meet the child's sensory needs. Eliminating physical activity would increase seeking behaviors as the sensory need goes unmet.

stem: An OTA working in a neonatal ICU is positioning a premature infant born at 28 weeks gestational age. The infant shows signs of sensory overstimulation including finger splaying, hiccupping, and color changes. The OTA should IMMEDIATELY:
options: Reduce environmental stimulation, dim lights, decrease noise, and provide gentle containment hold
Continue the intervention and document the stress signs afterward
Provide vestibular stimulation by rocking to calm the infant
Begin range of motion exercises to redirect the infant's attention
rationale: Premature infants have immature nervous systems that are easily overstimulated. Finger splaying, hiccupping, and color changes are autonomic stress signals indicating sensory overload. The immediate response must reduce all sensory input: dim lights (reduce visual), decrease noise (reduce auditory), and provide gentle containment (swaddling or hands-on containment provides organizing deep pressure). Continuing intervention ignores distress signals. Additional vestibular or tactile input would worsen overstimulation.

stem: An OTA is assessing an adult patient with sensory modulation disorder who reports extreme difficulty tolerating clothing tags, certain fabric textures, and light touch. This pattern is MOST consistent with:
options: Tactile defensiveness characterized by overresponsiveness to non-noxious tactile stimuli
Tactile hyposensitivity requiring increased sensory input
Somatosensory agnosia affecting object recognition by touch
rationale: Tactile defensiveness (tactile overresponsiveness) is characterized by aversive or exaggerated behavioral responses to non-harmful tactile stimuli such as clothing tags, fabric textures, and light touch. The nervous system interprets these benign stimuli as threatening. Hyposensitivity would present as reduced awareness of touch. Somatosensory agnosia involves inability to identify objects by touch. Peripheral neuropathy typically causes numbness or tingling rather than aversion to touch.

stem: An OTA has been treating a patient for two weeks and notices significant improvement. The supervising OTR asks the OTA to complete a reevaluation and update the treatment goals. According to AOTA guidelines, the OTA should:
options: Contribute to the reevaluation by providing data and observations, but ensure the OTR completes the formal reevaluation and modifies the plan
Independently complete the reevaluation and modify all treatment goals
Refuse to participate in any reevaluation activities
Complete the reevaluation but use the previous evaluation as a template without changes
rationale: According to AOTA guidelines, the OTA contributes to the reevaluation process by collecting data, sharing clinical observations, and providing input on the patient's progress. However, the formal reevaluation, interpretation of findings, and modification of the treatment plan are the responsibility of the OTR. The OTA should collaborate with the OTR but not independently complete the formal reevaluation. Refusing to participate or copying previous evaluations without updates are both inappropriate.

stem: An OTA discovers that a colleague has been billing for therapy sessions that were not actually provided. The OTA should FIRST take which action?
options: Report the suspected fraud to the supervisor or facility compliance officer
Confront the colleague and demand they stop immediately
Ignore the situation as it does not involve the OTA's patients
Begin documenting the colleague's sessions to gather evidence over several months
rationale: Billing for services not rendered constitutes healthcare fraud, which is both illegal and a violation of professional ethics. The OTA has an ethical obligation to report suspected fraud through proper channels, starting with the supervisor or facility compliance officer. Direct confrontation may be ineffective or create a hostile situation. Ignoring fraud violates the ethical duty to report. Gathering evidence over months delays reporting of an ongoing illegal activity that harms patients and payers.

stem: An OTA is documenting a treatment session using SOAP note format. The patient performed a dressing task with moderate assistance due to left neglect. Which statement BEST belongs in the Objective section?
options: Patient required moderate assistance for upper body dressing with 3 verbal cues for left visual scanning, completing task in 12 minutes
Patient seems frustrated with dressing and is probably not motivated today
Patient will dress upper body with minimal assistance within 2 weeks
Patient's dressing difficulties are due to left neglect from the right CVA
rationale: The Objective section of a SOAP note contains measurable, observable data about the patient's performance. The correct answer includes the assistance level (moderate), specific cueing provided (3 verbal cues for left visual scanning), and quantifiable performance data (12 minutes). The second option is subjective interpretation. The third option is a goal (belongs in Assessment or Plan). The fourth option is clinical interpretation (belongs in Assessment).

stem: A patient's family member requests to see the patient's occupational therapy records. The patient is an adult with decision-making capacity and has not authorized release of information to family. The OTA should:
options: Politely decline the request and explain that a signed authorization from the patient is required
Show the family member the records since they are a close relative
Read selected portions of the notes aloud without showing the documents
Provide copies of the records without informing the patient
rationale: HIPAA regulations protect patient health information. Without a signed authorization from a competent adult patient, health records cannot be disclosed to family members, regardless of the relationship. The OTA must politely explain the privacy requirements and direct the family to obtain the patient's written consent. Sharing records verbally or in writing without authorization violates HIPAA. The patient's autonomy and privacy rights must be respected.

stem: An OTA receives a referral for a patient whose treatment needs fall outside the OTA's area of competence. According to ethical principles, the OTA should:
options: Discuss the referral with the supervising OTR and recommend appropriate clinician or seek additional training
Accept the referral and attempt to treat the patient without additional preparation
Refuse the referral without communicating with anyone
Accept the referral but only document goals within the OTA's comfort zone
rationale: The AOTA Code of Ethics includes the principle of competence, which requires practitioners to only provide services within their competence level. When a referral exceeds the OTA's competence, the ethical response is to communicate with the supervising OTR to arrange appropriate care (either transferring to a qualified clinician or obtaining supervision and training). Treating without competence risks patient harm. Refusing without communication disrupts continuity. Limiting documentation while treating is deceptive.

stem: An OTA is working with a 78-year-old patient in a skilled nursing facility who has a fear of falling after a recent hip fracture. The patient refuses to stand during therapy. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Begin with seated balance activities and supported standing at the parallel bars, gradually progressing as confidence increases
Respect the refusal and only perform bedside activities indefinitely
Have the patient attempt unsupported ambulation immediately to overcome the fear
Discharge the patient from OT due to non-participation
rationale: Fear of falling is common after hip fracture and can significantly limit recovery. A graded approach starting with activities the patient feels safe performing (seated balance) and progressing to supported standing (parallel bars provide stability and security) addresses both the physical and psychological barriers. This builds confidence incrementally. Avoiding standing indefinitely prevents functional progress. Immediate unsupported ambulation ignores the fear and risks repeat injury. Discharge fails the patient.

stem: An OTA is conducting a home safety assessment for an elderly patient being discharged after a stroke. Which finding represents the HIGHEST fall risk that should be addressed FIRST?
options: Throw rugs on hardwood floors throughout the hallway and bathroom
Low lighting in the bedroom closet
Kitchen cabinets that require reaching above shoulder height
A doorknob that is difficult to turn
rationale: Throw rugs on hard flooring are one of the most common environmental fall hazards, particularly for patients with post-stroke gait impairments, hemiparesis, or balance deficits. The combination of loose footing and slippery surfaces in the hallway and bathroom (where falls are most common) represents the highest immediate fall risk. Low lighting, high shelving, and difficult doorknobs are valid concerns but present lower immediate fall risk than unstable floor surfaces in high-traffic areas.

stem: An OTA is working with a patient who has moderate dementia in a memory care unit. The patient becomes combative during morning care routines. Which strategy should the OTA implement?
options: Follow a consistent routine, use calm simple verbal cues, and approach from the front to reduce startle response
Change the routine daily to provide novel stimulation
Have two staff members restrain the patient during care
Complete all care tasks as quickly as possible to minimize the duration of combativeness
rationale: Combative behavior in dementia often results from confusion, overstimulation, or feeling threatened. A consistent routine reduces confusion by leveraging procedural memory. Calm, simple verbal cues reduce cognitive demands. Approaching from the front prevents startle responses (approaching from behind is threatening). Changing routines increases confusion. Physical restraint escalates agitation and violates patient rights. Rushing increases overstimulation and does not address the cause of combativeness.

stem: An OTA is treating a 82-year-old patient with osteoarthritis of the hands who reports difficulty opening containers and turning faucets. Which joint protection principle should the OTA emphasize FIRST?
options: Use the largest joint available and distribute force across multiple joints to reduce stress on small hand joints
Increase grip strength through intensive resistive exercises
Encourage maximum effort during all hand activities to maintain strength
Recommend surgery to replace affected joints before trying conservative measures
rationale: Joint protection for osteoarthritis focuses on reducing stress on affected joints. Using the largest joint available (e.g., using palms instead of fingers, leaning on forearms instead of pushing with hands) and distributing force reduces concentrated pressure on arthritic joints. Lever-style faucet handles and jar openers apply this principle. Intensive resistive exercise can exacerbate joint inflammation. Maximum effort increases joint stress. Surgery is reserved for cases where conservative management has failed.

stem: An OTA working in a skilled nursing facility notices that a patient with dementia has lost 8 pounds over the past month. The patient frequently does not finish meals and appears confused about utensil use. The OTA should:
options: Assess mealtime performance to identify barriers and implement environmental and task modifications to support eating
Recommend a feeding tube insertion immediately
Increase meal portions to compensate for incomplete eating
Assume the weight loss is a normal part of aging and take no action
rationale: Significant weight loss in a dementia patient requires investigation of the functional barriers to eating. The OTA should assess the mealtime environment (distractions, seating, lighting), utensil use (may need simplified setup, finger foods, or adapted utensils), and cognitive demands of the meal. Modifications may include reducing distractions, providing one course at a time, or using high-contrast plates. A feeding tube bypasses functional assessment. Larger portions do not address the barriers. Weight loss in dementia is not normal and should not be ignored.

stem: An OTA is planning a therapeutic group for patients with chronic mental illness in a community day program. According to Mosey's taxonomy of groups, which group level would be MOST appropriate for patients who can interact with others but have difficulty sustaining cooperative behavior?
options: Cooperative group where members work together toward a shared goal with the OTA as advisor
Parallel group where members work side by side with minimal interaction
Project group where members share a short-term task with the OTA as leader
Mature group where members take full responsibility for group function
rationale: Mosey's taxonomy of groups progresses from parallel (minimal interaction) to project (shared short-term task) to egocentric-cooperative (beginning cooperation) to cooperative (sustained cooperation with shared goals) to mature (full independence). Patients who can interact but struggle to sustain cooperative behavior are at the cooperative level, where group goals require collaboration and the OTA serves as an advisor rather than director. Parallel is too basic. Project involves limited shared responsibility. Mature requires more independence than described.

stem: During a group cooking activity at a rehabilitation center, one member dominates the conversation and tasks while others become withdrawn. The OTA should:
options: Assign specific roles to each group member and use facilitation techniques to encourage quieter members to participate
Allow the dominant member to lead since they are showing initiative
Remove the dominant member from the group
End the cooking activity and switch to individual tasks
rationale: Effective group facilitation requires balancing participation among all members. Assigning specific roles (measuring ingredients, stirring, reading the recipe, setting the table) ensures each person has a meaningful contribution. Facilitation techniques such as directly inviting quieter members to share, using round-robin formats, and positively reinforcing contributions from all members support balanced participation. Allowing dominance excludes others. Removing a member is punitive. Ending the activity avoids addressing the group dynamic, which is itself a therapeutic opportunity.

stem: An OTA is leading a stress management group for outpatients with anxiety disorders. Two group members begin arguing about the effectiveness of relaxation techniques. The MOST therapeutic response is to:
options: Acknowledge both perspectives, normalize individual differences in treatment response, and redirect to experiential practice
Take sides with the member who supports relaxation techniques
Tell both members they are wrong and present research data
Ignore the conflict and move to the next agenda item
rationale: Therapeutic group leadership involves managing conflict constructively. Acknowledging both perspectives validates each member's experience. Normalizing individual differences teaches that not all techniques work equally for everyone, which is clinically accurate and reduces judgment. Redirecting to experiential practice (trying a technique together) moves from debate to experience, allowing members to form their own conclusions. Taking sides, dismissing both, or ignoring conflict all fail to utilize the therapeutic potential of the interaction.

stem: An OTA is designing a life skills group for young adults with intellectual disabilities transitioning to community living. Which group structure would be MOST effective?
options: Structured sessions with visual supports, role-playing real-life scenarios, and repetition of key skills across multiple sessions
Lecture-based format with lengthy verbal instructions and written handouts
Unstructured open discussion about community living challenges
One-time workshop covering all community skills in a single session
rationale: Individuals with intellectual disabilities benefit from structured learning environments with concrete, visual supports that reduce reliance on verbal processing. Role-playing real-life scenarios provides experiential learning opportunities. Repetition across multiple sessions supports skill consolidation through practice. Lecture-based formats rely heavily on verbal comprehension and sustained attention. Unstructured discussions may be too abstract. A single session does not allow sufficient practice and repetition for skill acquisition.

stem: An OTA is co-leading a caregiver support group for families of patients with traumatic brain injury. A caregiver becomes tearful and shares feelings of guilt about placing their spouse in a long-term care facility. The OTA should:
options: Provide empathetic listening, validate the difficulty of the decision, and invite other group members to share similar experiences
Offer clinical advice about whether the placement decision was correct
Redirect the conversation to a different topic to avoid emotional discomfort
Suggest the caregiver seek individual psychotherapy before returning to the group
rationale: Caregiver support groups provide a safe space for emotional processing and mutual support. Empathetic listening and validation acknowledge the caregiver's pain without judgment. Inviting others to share similar experiences leverages the group's therapeutic power by reducing isolation and normalizing difficult emotions. Giving clinical advice about the placement is outside the OTA's scope and dismisses the emotional need. Redirecting avoids the therapeutic moment. Requiring psychotherapy before group participation creates a barrier.

stem: A patient with multiple sclerosis reports increased fatigue that interferes with morning self-care. The OTA should recommend which energy conservation strategy?
options: Organize supplies within reach the night before, sit during grooming, and alternate rest periods between tasks
Complete all self-care tasks as quickly as possible in the morning
Eliminate bathing from the daily routine to conserve energy
Perform all activities standing to maintain muscle strength
rationale: Energy conservation for MS involves planning ahead (organizing supplies), reducing physical demands (sitting during grooming reduces energy expenditure by 25%), and pacing (alternating activity with rest). Rushing increases fatigue and symptom exacerbation. Eliminating essential self-care activities is not therapeutic. Standing for all activities increases energy expenditure and fatigue, which can trigger MS symptom flares due to Uhthoff's phenomenon with overheating.

stem: An OTA is training a patient with a dominant hand flexor tendon injury to perform one-handed techniques for meal preparation. Which task should be practiced FIRST?
options: Stabilizing bread with a non-slip mat and spreading with the non-dominant hand
Cutting raw vegetables with a sharp knife using the non-dominant hand
Lifting a heavy pot of boiling water with the non-dominant hand
rationale: One-handed training should progress from simple, low-risk tasks to complex, higher-risk tasks. Spreading butter on bread stabilized with a non-slip mat is a simple, safe task that builds confidence and basic non-dominant hand coordination. Cutting with a sharp knife presents a safety risk with an untrained hand. Can opener operation requires significant coordination. Lifting boiling water with the non-dominant hand presents a burn risk and requires strength and control.

stem: An OTA is selecting a toilet seat modification for a patient with bilateral knee replacements. The patient has difficulty lowering to and rising from a standard-height toilet. Which adaptation is MOST appropriate?
options: Elevated toilet seat with armrests to reduce knee flexion and provide support during transfers
Standard toilet seat with a grab bar on one side only
Low-profile toilet seat to increase knee flexion for strengthening
Commode chair placed over the bed
rationale: Following bilateral knee replacements, patients must limit knee flexion beyond prescribed ranges initially. An elevated toilet seat raises the sitting surface, reducing the degree of knee flexion required. Armrests on both sides provide support for lowering and rising, reducing strain on the surgical knees. A single grab bar provides unilateral support only. A low-profile seat would increase knee flexion beyond safe limits. A bedside commode is unnecessary if the patient can access the bathroom.

stem: An OTA is implementing errorless learning techniques with a patient who has severe memory impairment following anoxic brain injury. Which approach BEST exemplifies errorless learning?
options: Providing step-by-step guidance that prevents the patient from making mistakes during task learning
Allowing the patient to make errors and then providing corrective feedback
Testing the patient's recall before providing any instruction
Asking the patient to guess the correct answer before showing the procedure
rationale: Errorless learning prevents errors during the acquisition phase of learning, which is critical for patients with severe memory impairment who cannot benefit from error correction (as they may remember the error rather than the correction). Step-by-step guidance ensures correct performance from the start. Trial-and-error approaches, testing before instruction, and guessing all allow errors that may be encoded into implicit memory, interfering with correct learning.

stem: An OTA is treating a patient with de Quervain's tenosynovitis. The patient reports pain with thumb extension and radial wrist deviation. A positive Finkelstein's test is noted. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Thumb spica splint immobilizing the wrist and thumb CMC/MCP joints, combined with activity modification
Aggressive resistive thumb exercises to strengthen the tendons
Hot pack application directly over the inflamed tendons followed by vigorous massage
Full wrist and hand immobilization in a long arm cast
rationale: De Quervain's tenosynovitis involves inflammation of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons in the first dorsal compartment. A thumb spica splint immobilizes the wrist and thumb to rest the inflamed tendons. Activity modification reduces repetitive movements that aggravate the condition. Resistive exercises would worsen inflammation. Hot packs with vigorous massage over acutely inflamed tendons increase inflammation. Full immobilization is excessive and leads to unnecessary stiffness.

stem: An OTA is treating a 2-year-old child with cerebral palsy who demonstrates persistent asymmetric tonic neck reflex (ATNR). This reflex is interfering with bilateral hand use at midline. Which therapeutic activity is MOST appropriate?
options: Positioning the child in prone or side-lying while engaging in bilateral midline play activities
Encouraging the child to turn the head repeatedly to strengthen the reflex pattern
Having the child practice only unilateral reaching activities
Placing toys only on one side to avoid triggering the reflex
rationale: The ATNR causes extension of the arm on the face side and flexion on the skull side when the head is turned. Prone and side-lying positions reduce the influence of ATNR because the head is in a more neutral position relative to gravity. Bilateral midline play in these positions promotes hand-to-hand transfer and symmetrical use without triggering the reflex. Encouraging head turning strengthens the pathological pattern. Unilateral activities and one-sided toy placement accommodate rather than address the reflex interference.

stem: An OTA is developing a relapse prevention plan with a patient who has substance use disorder preparing for community discharge. Which component is MOST essential to include?
options: Identification of personal triggers, coping strategies for high-risk situations, and a sober support network plan
A commitment to never experience any negative emotions after discharge
A plan to avoid all social interactions to prevent exposure to substances
Reliance solely on medication management without behavioral strategies
rationale: Effective relapse prevention requires self-awareness of personal triggers (people, places, emotions, situations), concrete coping strategies for managing cravings and high-risk scenarios, and a support network (sponsor, support groups, sober friends). Eliminating negative emotions is unrealistic and sets up for failure. Social isolation removes protective factors and increases relapse risk. Medication alone without behavioral strategies has lower success rates than combined approaches.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who is hyposensitive to proprioceptive input and appears clumsy, uses excessive force when writing, and frequently breaks pencil tips. Which activity would BEST address this deficit?
options: Activities involving graded force control such as using a mechanical pencil or carrying fragile objects
Only fine motor activities using standard pencils with no force feedback
Removing all writing tasks to avoid frustration
Using only computer-based activities to eliminate the need for pencil grip
rationale: Proprioceptive hyposensitivity results in poor force modulation. Activities requiring graded force control provide feedback about how much pressure to apply. A mechanical pencil breaks with too much force, providing immediate feedback for self-regulation. Carrying fragile objects (eggs, cups of water) similarly teaches force grading. Standard pencils with no specific force feedback do not address the deficit. Removing or avoiding writing does not build the skill. Computer activities bypass rather than remediate the underlying deficit.

stem: An OTA is working under the supervision of an OTR in a home health setting. The OTR has been unavailable for the past two weeks due to a family emergency, and no substitute supervisor has been arranged. The OTA should:
options: Contact the employer to request an alternate OTR supervisor before continuing patient care
Continue treating all patients independently without supervision
Discontinue all patient care without notifying anyone
Ask the physical therapist to supervise OTA services
rationale: OTAs are legally required to practice under OTR supervision. Without supervision, the OTA cannot continue providing services as it falls below legal and ethical practice standards. The appropriate action is to proactively contact the employer to arrange for a substitute OTR supervisor. Practicing without supervision violates licensure requirements. Abruptly discontinuing care without notification abandons patients. A PT is not qualified to supervise OT services as it is a different profession with different scope.

stem: An OTA is treating an elderly patient with Parkinson's disease who is experiencing freezing of gait when approaching doorways. Which compensatory strategy should the OTA recommend?
options: Use visual cues such as stepping over an imaginary line or using laser pointer lines on the floor to overcome freezing
Instruct the patient to push harder through the doorway when freezing occurs
Recommend the patient use a wheelchair exclusively to avoid freezing episodes
Have the patient close their eyes when approaching doorways to reduce visual input
rationale: Freezing of gait in Parkinson's disease occurs when automatic motor programs fail, particularly at doorways, turns, or when initiating walking. Visual cues (laser pointer on the floor, stepping over lines, counting steps) engage cortical motor pathways that bypass the impaired basal ganglia circuitry. External cues substitute for the missing internal rhythmic cues. Pushing through can cause falls. Wheelchair use eliminates walking practice. Closing eyes removes visual feedback needed for safe mobility.

stem: A patient with left hemiplegia following a stroke is learning to transfer from wheelchair to bed. The OTA should position the wheelchair at which angle relative to the bed?
options: At a 45-degree angle with the patient's stronger right side closest to the bed
Perpendicular (90 degrees) to the bed with the left side closest
Parallel to the bed facing the foot of the bed
Directly behind the bed facing away from it
rationale: For a patient with left hemiplegia, transfers should lead with the stronger (right) side. Positioning the wheelchair at a 45-degree angle to the bed with the right side closest allows the patient to push up with the right arm, pivot on the right leg, and lower onto the bed surface. This maximizes use of the stronger side for weight-bearing and movement control. Perpendicular positioning with the affected side closest requires the patient to lead with the weaker side, increasing fall risk.

stem: An OTA is working with a patient who has C7 tetraplegia and wants to perform independent bowel and bladder management. Which adaptive technique should the OTA emphasize?
options: Use of a digital stimulator with adapted grip and mirror for visual feedback during the procedure
Complete dependence on nursing staff for all bowel management
Use of standard equipment without any adaptations
Delaying bowel management until full hand function returns
rationale: Patients with C7 tetraplegia have wrist extension and some finger control, making independence in bowel management achievable with adaptations. A digital stimulator with adapted grip compensates for reduced hand dexterity. A mirror provides visual feedback for techniques performed in positions where direct visualization is limited. Complete dependence is unnecessary given the functional capacity at C7. Standard equipment without adaptation may be difficult to use. Full hand function recovery is unlikely in complete SCI.

stem: An OTA is recommending a writing adaptation for a patient with essential tremor who needs to sign documents. Which adaptation would be MOST effective?
options: Weighted pen with a built-up grip to dampen tremor and increase stability
Lighter pen to reduce the effort required
Standard ballpoint pen with smooth ink flow
Pencil that can be erased if the writing is shaky
rationale: Essential tremor is an action tremor that worsens with voluntary movement. A weighted pen adds mass to the writing instrument, which dampens the oscillation frequency and amplitude of the tremor. A built-up grip reduces the fine motor demands of holding a standard-diameter pen. A lighter pen would have less dampening effect. Standard pens and erasable pencils do not address the tremor mechanism. Weight is one of the most effective simple adaptations for tremor management.

stem: An OTA is using a multicontextual treatment approach with a patient who has difficulty with problem-solving after a frontal lobe injury. The patient has learned to solve problems during meal preparation. According to this approach, what should the OTA do NEXT?
options: Transfer the problem-solving strategy to a different context such as managing a simulated shopping task
Continue practicing the same meal preparation task until mastery
Move to a completely unrelated cognitive exercise such as sudoku puzzles
Discharge the patient since they have demonstrated problem-solving ability in one context
rationale: The multicontextual approach (Toglia) emphasizes transfer of learning across situations by practicing strategies in multiple contexts with varying levels of similarity. After success in meal preparation, the next step is a related but different context (shopping) to promote generalization. Continuing the same task does not promote transfer. Unrelated exercises (sudoku) do not facilitate functional generalization. Discharge after success in one context ignores the common failure to transfer skills that characterizes frontal lobe injury.

stem: An OTA is evaluating a patient with Dupuytren's contracture of the ring finger. The patient has a 45-degree flexion contracture at the MCP joint. Following surgical fasciectomy, which post-operative intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Extension splinting of the affected finger combined with gentle active flexion exercises and edema management
Aggressive passive extension stretching beginning on post-operative day 1
Complete immobilization in flexion for 6 weeks
Resistive grip strengthening exercises starting immediately after surgery
rationale: Following fasciectomy for Dupuytren's contracture, the treatment goal is to maintain the extension gained surgically. An extension splint prevents the flexion contracture from recurring. Gentle active flexion exercises maintain tendon gliding without stressing the surgical site. Edema management prevents stiffness and complications. Aggressive stretching risks wound dehiscence. Immobilization in flexion would allow contracture recurrence. Resistive exercise is premature in the early post-operative phase.

stem: An OTA is working with a preschool-aged child with developmental delay who is unable to cut with scissors. The OTA notes the child cannot maintain an open web space. Which preparatory activity should be addressed FIRST?
options: Activities to develop hand arching and web space opening such as squeezing small balls and tearing paper
Tracing straight lines with a marker
Typing on a keyboard as an alternative to cutting
rationale: Scissor use requires an open web space (the arch between thumb and index finger) to separate the cutting (index/middle fingers) and stabilizing (ring/small fingers) sides of the hand. If the web space cannot be maintained, scissors cannot be controlled. Preparatory activities that develop hand arching and web space (squeezing balls, tearing paper) build the foundational motor pattern. Loop scissors may assist but do not address the underlying deficit. Tracing and typing do not develop the needed hand skills.

stem: An OTA is using the Model of Human Occupation (MOHO) to treat a patient with depression who has lost interest in previously enjoyed hobbies. According to MOHO, which subsystem is MOST affected?
options: Volition, specifically the values and interests component that drives occupational engagement
Habituation, specifically the role internalization component
Performance capacity, specifically the musculoskeletal component
Environmental context, specifically the social support component
rationale: In MOHO, volition refers to the motivation for occupation, including personal causation (sense of competence), values (what is important), and interests (what is enjoyable). Depression characteristically diminishes interest in previously enjoyed activities (anhedonia), directly impacting the volition subsystem. While habituation (roles and routines) and environment may also be affected, the primary symptom of lost interest maps directly to the volitional component, particularly interests and personal causation.

stem: An OTA is using the Wilbarger Deep Pressure and Proprioceptive Technique (DPPT) with a child who has sensory defensiveness. The parent asks if they can perform the protocol at home. The OTA should:
options: Train the parent in the specific protocol after confirming competency, as the technique requires strict adherence to the prescribed schedule and method
Refuse to teach the parent and state it can only be done by therapists
Tell the parent to brush the child whenever they seem agitated without specific training
Provide written instructions only without demonstrating the technique
rationale: The Wilbarger DPPT is designed to be implemented as a home program performed every 90-120 minutes during waking hours for optimal effectiveness. Training caregivers is essential for adherence. However, the technique requires specific pressure, direction, and timing—incorrect application can increase defensiveness. The OTA should demonstrate the technique, observe the parent performing it, and confirm competency before independent use. Refusing limits efficacy. Unsupervised use risks harm. Written instructions alone are insufficient for a tactile technique.

stem: An OTA is documenting functional progress for a patient whose insurance company requires evidence of skilled OT services to continue coverage. Which documentation approach BEST demonstrates medical necessity?
options: Specific measurable functional outcomes such as dressing time reduced from 25 to 15 minutes with decreased assistance level from moderate to minimal
General statements such as 'patient is improving' and 'continue current plan'
Only listing the activities performed during each session without outcomes
Copying the same progress note for each session with updated dates
rationale: Insurance companies require evidence that skilled OT services result in measurable functional improvement. Specific, quantifiable data (time measurements, assistance levels, cueing frequency) demonstrates objective progress. Changes in function (dressing time, independence level) show that skilled intervention is producing results that justify continued coverage. Vague statements lack objective evidence. Activity lists without outcomes do not show progress. Duplicated notes suggest lack of progress and invite coverage denial.

stem: An OTA is treating an 80-year-old patient with age-related macular degeneration who wants to continue reading. Which low vision strategy should the OTA recommend?
options: High-contrast large print materials with a stand magnifier and task lighting positioned to reduce glare
Standard print books with overhead fluorescent lighting
Audio books exclusively with no attempt to use remaining vision
Reading in dim lighting to reduce eye strain
rationale: Macular degeneration affects central vision while preserving peripheral vision. High-contrast large print maximizes the remaining visual function. A stand magnifier enlarges text while keeping the page at a consistent focal distance. Task lighting (positioned to illuminate the page without creating glare) enhances contrast sensitivity. Standard print is too small. Audio books abandon visual reading entirely when adaptation is possible. Dim lighting worsens visual function rather than reducing strain.

stem: An OTA is evaluating group member readiness to transition from a structured task group to a less structured community integration group. Which behavior BEST indicates readiness for the transition?
options: The member consistently initiates problem-solving, offers support to peers, and demonstrates appropriate social interactions without prompting
The member attends all sessions but remains silent and avoids eye contact
The member follows specific step-by-step instructions but cannot adapt when plans change
The member demonstrates frequent conflicts with other group members and staff
rationale: Readiness for less structured community-based groups requires demonstrated self-directed behavior including independent problem-solving (cognitive readiness), prosocial behavior such as offering peer support (social readiness), and appropriate unprompted social interactions (behavioral readiness). Silent attendance shows physical presence but not active engagement. Rigid adherence to instructions without flexibility indicates unreadiness for less structured settings. Frequent conflicts indicate social skills requiring further development in structured settings.

stem: An OTA is treating a patient with bilateral below-knee amputations who uses prosthetic legs. The patient reports difficulty with balance during shower transfers. Which safety modification is MOST important?
options: Install a shower bench, grab bars, and non-slip mat to eliminate standing balance demands during bathing
Recommend showering while wearing prosthetic legs
Suggest bathing only with a caregiver present
Advise against showering and recommend sponge baths only
rationale: Bilateral below-knee amputees face significant balance challenges on wet surfaces, especially without prostheses (which should not be worn in water). A shower bench eliminates the need for standing balance during bathing. Grab bars provide stability during transfers. A non-slip mat reduces slip risk on wet surfaces. This combination addresses the safety concern while promoting maximum independence. Wearing prostheses in water damages them. Requiring a caregiver reduces independence. Eliminating showers is unnecessarily restrictive.

stem: An OTA is working with a patient who has myasthenia gravis and notices worsening weakness after 15 minutes of upper extremity activity. The OTA should:
options: Schedule therapy sessions in the morning when the patient has the most strength and incorporate frequent rest breaks
Push through the fatigue to build endurance
Schedule sessions in the late afternoon when muscles are warmed up
rationale: Myasthenia gravis causes fatigable weakness that worsens with sustained activity and improves with rest. Therapy should be scheduled when the patient has the most strength (typically mornings and after medication peak). Frequent rest breaks prevent excessive fatigue that could compromise respiratory and swallowing muscles. Pushing through fatigue is dangerous as it can precipitate myasthenic crisis. Afternoon sessions occur when weakness is typically worse. Discontinuing activities eliminates therapeutic benefit.

stem: An OTA is using constraint-induced movement therapy (CIMT) with a patient who had a stroke 6 months ago. The patient has at least 20 degrees of active wrist extension and 10 degrees of finger extension on the affected side. Which component is ESSENTIAL to the CIMT protocol?
options: Restraining the unaffected hand with a mitt for 90% of waking hours while practicing intensive task-oriented training with the affected hand
Alternating use of both hands equally throughout the day
Using the affected hand only during therapy sessions and the unaffected hand at all other times
Wearing the restraint on the affected hand to prevent use
rationale: CIMT involves three key components: restraint of the unaffected extremity (mitt worn 90% of waking hours), intensive task-oriented practice with the affected hand (6+ hours daily), and transfer package techniques (structured problem-solving to apply skills in daily life). The patient meets the motor criteria (20° wrist extension, 10° finger extension). Alternating hands does not force affected hand use. Using the affected hand only during therapy is insufficient intensity. Restraining the affected hand is the opposite of the protocol.

stem: An OTA is working with a 10-year-old child with ADHD in a school setting. The child has difficulty remaining seated and focused during classroom instruction. Which sensory-based accommodation should the OTA recommend?
options: Provide a wobble cushion or therapy ball chair and allow movement breaks every 20 minutes
Require the child to sit perfectly still in a standard chair for the entire class period
Remove the child from the classroom whenever restlessness occurs
Provide a standing desk only with no seating option
rationale: Children with ADHD often have high sensory needs for proprioceptive and vestibular input. A wobble cushion or therapy ball allows micro-movements that provide sensory input while the child remains at the desk, improving attention and reducing disruptive seeking behaviors. Scheduled movement breaks provide structured opportunities for larger movement. Demanding stillness contradicts the child's neurological needs. Removal from class limits learning. A standing-only desk without options does not accommodate the child's variable needs throughout the day.

stem: An OTA is treating a burn patient with a circumferential burn to the right forearm. The patient is 10 days post-burn and scar formation is beginning. The primary goal of splinting at this stage is to:
options: Position the forearm, wrist, and hand in an anti-deformity position to prevent scar contracture
Allow the scar to contract naturally before intervening
Splint in the position of comfort to reduce pain
Avoid splinting to prevent pressure on healing tissue
rationale: Burn scar contracture is a major complication that can severely limit function. Anti-deformity positioning during scar formation (wrist extension, MCP flexion, IP extension, thumb abduction) counteracts the natural tendency of scars to contract. The position of comfort is typically the position of deformity (flexion, adduction). Allowing natural contraction leads to contractures requiring surgical release. Avoiding splinting during active scar formation misses the critical window for contracture prevention.

stem: An OTA is providing home modifications recommendations for a patient with paraplegia who uses a manual wheelchair. For the patient to access the home independently, which modification is MOST critical?
options: A ramp with a 1:12 slope ratio at the entrance and doorway widths of at least 32 inches
Installing an elevator inside the two-story home
Replacing all flooring with deep pile carpet for comfort
Lowering all light switches to 24 inches from the floor
rationale: Home access is the most critical modification for wheelchair users. A ramp with a 1:12 slope (1 inch of rise per 12 inches of length) meets ADA standards for wheelchair navigation. Doorways must be at least 32 inches wide (36 inches preferred) for wheelchair clearance. Without access into the home, no other modifications matter. An elevator is expensive and may not be necessary if living areas can be arranged on one floor. Deep pile carpet increases wheelchair rolling resistance. Light switches at 24 inches are too low; 48 inches is the standard wheelchair-accessible height.

stem: A patient with an acquired brain injury demonstrates perseveration during a toothbrushing task, continuously brushing the same teeth. The OTA should:
options: Provide a gentle verbal redirect and physical cue to move to the next area, using a structured sequence chart
Allow the patient to continue brushing the same area until they stop spontaneously
Take over the task and brush the patient's teeth to ensure completion
Eliminate toothbrushing from the treatment plan
rationale: Perseveration is the repetition of a behavior or response despite it no longer being appropriate. A gentle verbal redirect ('Now brush the bottom teeth') paired with a physical cue (guiding the hand to the next area) interrupts the perseverative loop. A structured sequence chart (visual cue card showing top right, top left, bottom right, bottom left) provides external structure to guide the complete task. Waiting for spontaneous stopping may never occur. Performing the task for the patient eliminates therapeutic benefit. Removing the task avoids functional treatment.

stem: An OTA is treating a premature infant in the NICU who demonstrates trunk and extremity extension posturing. Which positioning strategy is MOST appropriate?
options: Supported flexion positioning using nesting rolls to promote physiological flexion similar to the uterine environment
Allowing the infant to remain in extension to build extensor muscle strength
Prone positioning without any support or containment
Supine positioning with arms and legs fully extended
rationale: Premature infants lack the intrauterine constraints that promote physiological flexion. Extension posturing is common and can lead to abnormal movement patterns. Nesting rolls provide boundaries that simulate the uterine environment, supporting the infant in a flexed, midline position. This promotes normal neurodevelopmental organization, self-regulation, and hand-to-mouth behavior. Allowing extension reinforces abnormal posture. Unsupported prone positioning can be dangerous. Extended supine encourages the extension pattern the intervention aims to correct.

stem: An OTA is working with a patient who has obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) and spends 3 hours daily on hand-washing rituals, preventing participation in work and social activities. Using an occupational performance approach, the OTA should PRIMARILY focus on:
options: Establishing structured daily routines that schedule meaningful occupations and gradually reduce time allocated to rituals
Reinforcing the hand-washing ritual as an occupation to improve performance quality
Avoiding any discussion of the rituals during therapy
Replacing hand-washing with a different repetitive activity
rationale: From an occupational performance perspective, the OCD rituals are displacing meaningful occupations (work, social participation). Structured daily routines with scheduled meaningful activities create a framework that competes with ritual time and rebuilds occupational balance. Gradual reduction of ritual time (in collaboration with the treatment team, often alongside ERP) prevents overwhelming anxiety. Reinforcing rituals increases dysfunction. Ignoring rituals fails to address the primary barrier. Replacing with another repetitive activity does not restore occupational balance.

stem: An OTA is assessing a patient's home for ergonomic modifications to a home office setup. The patient reports neck pain and bilateral wrist numbness after prolonged computer use. Which modification should be prioritized?
options: Adjust monitor height to eye level, position keyboard so elbows are at 90 degrees, and add a wrist rest with neutral wrist alignment
Raise the chair height so feet dangle freely
Position the monitor below desk level to look down
Use a standard mouse without any ergonomic modifications
rationale: Neck pain from prolonged computer use typically results from poor monitor positioning, and wrist numbness suggests carpal tunnel compression from sustained wrist flexion or extension. Monitor at eye level reduces cervical flexion/extension. Keyboard positioning with 90-degree elbow flexion places wrists in neutral. A wrist rest prevents wrist extension during typing. Dangling feet increase postural strain. Below-desk monitor increases cervical flexion. Unmodified mouse use does not address the contributing factors.

stem: An OTA is treating a patient who had a total shoulder replacement with an approach through the subscapularis. During the first 6 weeks of rehabilitation, which movement should be AVOIDED?
options: Active external rotation and combined abduction with external rotation to protect the repaired subscapularis
Gentle pendulum exercises in a forward-lean position
Passive forward flexion within the surgeon's prescribed range
Isometric internal rotation with the arm at the side
rationale: When the subscapularis is detached and repaired during a total shoulder replacement, active external rotation and combined abduction with external rotation stress the repair and risk failure. The subscapularis is an internal rotator, and its opposing motion (external rotation) places the repair under tension. Pendulum exercises use gravity-assisted motion without stressing the repair. Passive forward flexion within limits is safe. Gentle isometric internal rotation may be permitted as it activates the repaired muscle without excessive strain.

stem: An OTA is working with a child who has fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD) in a school setting. The child has difficulty following multi-step classroom instructions. The OTA should recommend:
options: Breaking instructions into single steps with visual supports and providing immediate feedback after each step
Providing all instructions at once and expecting the child to remember them
Allowing the child to figure out instructions independently without support
Having the child write all instructions in a journal before starting tasks
rationale: Children with FASD typically have executive function deficits affecting working memory, sequencing, and processing. Breaking multi-step instructions into single steps reduces working memory demands. Visual supports (picture schedules, step cards) provide external structure that compensates for internal processing deficits. Immediate feedback after each step reinforces correct performance before moving on. Multi-step instructions overwhelm working memory. Independent problem-solving exceeds cognitive capacity. Writing requires simultaneous processing that may also be impaired.

stem: An OTA notices that a colleague is treating a patient while appearing to be under the influence of alcohol, as evidenced by slurred speech, unsteady gait, and alcohol odor. The OTA's FIRST ethical obligation is to:
options: Ensure immediate patient safety by calmly intervening and then report the concern to the supervisor
Confront the colleague aggressively in front of the patient
Ignore the situation as it is not the OTA's responsibility
Document the observation in the patient's medical record
rationale: Patient safety is the highest priority in all healthcare settings. An impaired practitioner poses an immediate risk to patient safety. The OTA should first ensure the patient is safe (calmly offering to take over or redirect the session), then promptly report the concern to the supervisor or appropriate authority. Aggressive confrontation could escalate the situation and alarm the patient. Ignoring the situation violates ethical obligations. Documenting in the patient's chart is not appropriate for concerns about a colleague's behavior.

stem: An OTA is working with a 65-year-old patient recovering from a hip fracture in a skilled nursing facility. The patient becomes tearful and states, 'I don't see the point of therapy. I'm just going to end up back here again.' The MOST appropriate therapeutic response is:
options: Acknowledge the patient's feelings, explore concerns about the future, and collaboratively identify meaningful goals that give therapy purpose
Tell the patient that crying is not productive and they should focus on exercises
Agree that readmission is likely and there may not be much point
Ignore the emotional expression and continue with the planned exercises
rationale: The patient's expression of hopelessness indicates emotional distress that directly impacts therapy motivation and outcomes. Acknowledging feelings validates the patient's experience. Exploring concerns allows identification of underlying fears (loss of independence, burden on family). Collaborative goal-setting reconnects therapy to personally meaningful outcomes, addressing both emotional and functional needs. Dismissing emotions damages rapport. Agreeing with hopelessness reinforces defeatism. Ignoring the emotional state fails to address a significant barrier to participation.

stem: During a community outing with a group of patients with developmental disabilities, one patient begins to have a seizure. The OTA should FIRST:
options: Ensure the patient's safety by clearing the area of hard objects, positioning the patient on their side, and timing the seizure
Place an object between the patient's teeth to prevent tongue biting
Restrain the patient's arms and legs to prevent injury during convulsions
Leave the patient to attend to the other group members first
rationale: Seizure first aid priorities include safety (clearing hard objects to prevent injury), positioning (side-lying to maintain airway and prevent aspiration), and monitoring (timing the seizure as duration over 5 minutes constitutes status epilepticus requiring emergency medical services). Placing objects in the mouth can cause tooth damage, aspiration, or bite injury to the helper. Restraining limbs can cause musculoskeletal injury. Leaving the seizing patient unattended is unsafe and constitutes abandonment.

stem: An OTA is providing discharge planning for a patient with a T4 spinal cord injury. The patient lives alone in a second-floor apartment with no elevator. Which recommendation is MOST critical for safe discharge?
options: Relocate to an accessible ground-floor or elevator-equipped residence before discharge
Practice stair climbing with the wheelchair
Install a stair lift that accommodates a wheelchair
Discharge to the current apartment with a home health aide for stair transfers
rationale: A T4 SCI results in paraplegia requiring full-time wheelchair use. A second-floor apartment without elevator access is a fundamental barrier to safe independence. No amount of training or adaptation makes wheelchair use on stairs safe for daily community access. Relocation to an accessible dwelling is the most critical recommendation. Stair climbing in a wheelchair is extremely dangerous. Standard stair lifts do not accommodate wheelchairs. A home health aide for stair transfers is unsustainable and unsafe for daily use.

stem: An OTA is treating a patient with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) who works as a data entry clerk. Which workplace modification and treatment combination is MOST appropriate?
options: Counterforce brace just distal to the elbow, ergonomic keyboard with wrist rests, and eccentric wrist extensor strengthening
Immobilization of the entire arm in a sling during work
Aggressive active range of motion exercises focusing on wrist flexion and extension at maximum speed
Heat application for 30 minutes before work with no other interventions
rationale: Lateral epicondylitis involves the common extensor tendon origin. A counterforce brace redistributes forces away from the damaged tendon. An ergonomic keyboard and wrist rests reduce sustained wrist extension during typing, decreasing tendon strain. Eccentric strengthening (controlled lengthening of the wrist extensors) promotes tendon remodeling and is evidence-based treatment for tendinopathy. Full immobilization is excessive. Aggressive ROM can worsen the condition. Heat alone does not address the mechanical causes.

stem: An OTA is facilitating a social skills group for adults with autism spectrum disorder. One member repeatedly interrupts others and monopolizes conversation time. Which group technique should the OTA use?
options: Implement a visual turn-taking system such as a talking stick and provide concrete rules about conversation duration
Allow the member to speak freely to avoid triggering anxiety
Exclude the member from the group until they can control their behavior
Ignore the interruptions and let the group self-regulate
rationale: Adults with ASD often struggle with implicit social rules such as turn-taking and reading conversational cues. Concrete, visual systems (talking stick, timer, token system) make abstract social rules tangible and predictable. Explicit rules about conversation duration ('each person speaks for 2 minutes') reduce ambiguity. This approach teaches social skills within the group context rather than avoiding them. Unrestricted speaking does not build skills. Exclusion is punitive. Self-regulation may be precisely what these members cannot do without support.

stem: An OTA is evaluating the functional impact of peripheral neuropathy in a patient with diabetes. The patient reports dropping objects and difficulty feeling water temperature. Which safety concern should the OTA address FIRST?
options: Burns from inability to sense water temperature; recommend using a thermometer before bathing and lowering water heater temperature
Dropping objects; recommend using heavier objects to increase awareness
Difficulty buttoning; provide a button hook immediately
Pain management through gentle massage of the hands
rationale: Loss of temperature sensation in diabetic neuropathy creates an immediate burn risk during bathing and cooking. Burns in diabetic patients heal poorly due to impaired circulation and can lead to serious complications including infection and amputation. Using a thermometer to check water temperature and lowering the water heater to 120°F (49°C) prevents scalding. While dropping objects and button difficulty are functional concerns, burn prevention is a higher safety priority requiring immediate intervention.

stem: An OTA is assessing a patient with early-stage Parkinson's disease for handwriting difficulty (micrographia). The patient's writing progressively gets smaller across the page. Which intervention is MOST effective?
options: Use lined paper with widely spaced bold lines and practice large-amplitude writing exercises with visual cues
Switch exclusively to typing and abandon handwriting
Practice writing smaller and faster to improve speed
Use a fine-point pen for more precise control
rationale: Micrographia in Parkinson's disease results from bradykinesia and hypokinesia affecting the writing muscles. Widely spaced bold lines provide visual targets that cue larger letter size. Large-amplitude movement training (LSVT BIG principles) retrains the motor system to produce bigger movements, directly countering the tendency toward smallness. Abandoning handwriting is premature in early-stage disease. Practicing smaller writing reinforces the pathological pattern. Fine-point pens do not address the amplitude deficit.

stem: An OTA is working with a patient who has experienced domestic violence and is now in a safe shelter. The patient has difficulty making simple daily decisions and appears fearful. Using a trauma-informed approach, the OTA should:
options: Offer structured choices in therapy to rebuild the patient's sense of agency and control over daily decisions
Make all decisions for the patient to reduce their stress
Require detailed disclosure of the traumatic experiences before beginning therapy
Set rigid therapy schedules with no flexibility for the patient's preferences
rationale: Trauma-informed care principles include safety, trustworthiness, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. Domestic violence survivors often have had autonomy stripped away. Offering structured choices (e.g., 'Would you like to work on cooking or organizing today?') begins rebuilding the sense of control and decision-making capacity. Making all decisions perpetuates the power imbalance. Requiring trauma disclosure can be retraumatizing. Rigid schedules without patient input contradict the principles of choice and collaboration.

stem: An OTA is using retrograde massage on a patient's hand to reduce post-surgical edema. The massage strokes should be directed:
options: From distal to proximal (fingertips toward the wrist and forearm) to move fluid toward the heart
From proximal to distal (forearm toward the fingertips) to push fluid out
In circular motions only over the surgical site
In random directions with firm pressure over edematous areas
rationale: Retrograde massage for edema reduction follows the venous and lymphatic return pathways from distal to proximal. Gentle strokes from fingertips toward the wrist and forearm move excess fluid toward the venous return system and lymph nodes in the axillary region. Proximal-to-distal strokes would push fluid further into the already congested distal tissues. Circular motions over the surgical site risk disrupting healing tissue. Random firm pressure could damage tissues and does not facilitate organized fluid drainage.

stem: An 8-year-old child with spina bifida at the L3-L4 level is being assessed for self-catheterization readiness. Which factors should the OTA evaluate to determine readiness?
options: Fine motor skills for catheter manipulation, cognitive understanding of the procedure, and emotional readiness for self-management
Only chronological age to determine if the child is old enough
Whether the child can walk independently
Academic performance as an indicator of cognitive readiness
rationale: Self-catheterization readiness requires assessment of multiple domains: fine motor skills (ability to manipulate the catheter and supplies), cognitive skills (understanding the procedure sequence, hygiene principles, and when to catheterize), and emotional readiness (motivation, comfort with the procedure, and self-management responsibility). Chronological age alone does not determine readiness as development varies. Ambulation status is separate from catheterization ability. Academic performance does not directly assess procedural readiness.

stem: An OTA is working with a patient who uses a power wheelchair and has severely limited upper extremity function due to muscular dystrophy. The patient wants to participate in painting as a leisure activity. Which adaptation would BEST enable participation?
options: Mouth-held painting tool with an easel positioned at face height and adapted canvas holder on the wheelchair tray
Standard paint brushes with built-up handles
Finger painting with adapted hand splints
Advising the patient to choose a different hobby due to physical limitations
rationale: When upper extremity function is severely limited, alternative control sites must be utilized. Mouth-held painting tools allow individuals with minimal arm movement to paint using oral motor control, which may be preserved in muscular dystrophy. Positioning the easel at face height and securing the canvas on the wheelchair tray optimizes the setup for mouth painting. Built-up handles still require hand grip. Finger painting requires hand function. Discouraging participation violates client-centered practice and the OT philosophy of enabling occupation.

stem: An OTA is treating a patient with anosognosia following a right hemisphere stroke. During therapy, the patient insists there is nothing wrong and refuses to use compensatory strategies for left neglect. The OTA should:
options: Use concrete task-based feedback to demonstrate functional limitations without confrontation, and gradually build awareness
Argue with the patient about the severity of their deficits until they agree
Document non-compliance and discharge from therapy
Agree with the patient that there are no deficits and eliminate all compensatory strategies
rationale: Anosognosia is a neurological inability to recognize one's own deficits, not willful denial. Concrete task-based feedback (pointing out a missed item during a visual scanning task, noting untouched food on the left side of the plate) provides objective evidence without confrontation. Gradual awareness building through structured activities that naturally reveal the deficit is more effective than argument. Arguing is counterproductive with a neurological condition. Discharge fails the patient. Eliminating compensatory strategies puts the patient at risk.

stem: An OTA is working with a nursing home resident who hoards items in their room, creating a tripping hazard. The resident becomes very distressed when staff attempt to remove items. The OTA should:
options: Collaborate with the resident to organize items safely, using labeled bins and designated safe areas, while respecting the resident's attachment to belongings
Remove all hoarded items from the room while the resident is at therapy
Ignore the hoarding behavior as a personal choice
Restrict the resident's access to all personal items
rationale: Hoarding in nursing home residents may be related to cognitive decline, loss of control, anxiety, or attachment issues. A therapeutic approach balances safety with respect for the resident's dignity and belongings. Collaborative organization (labeled bins, designated areas) reduces the tripping hazard while giving the resident control and maintaining their sense of security. Removing items without consent causes distress and may violate resident rights. Ignoring the safety hazard is negligent. Restricting all items is excessive and damaging.

stem: An OTA is planning a therapeutic gardening group for patients in a geriatric psychiatric unit. Several group members have difficulty bending, limited hand strength, and cognitive impairments. Which modification BEST supports inclusive participation?
options: Use raised garden beds at wheelchair height, ergonomic tools with built-up handles, and simple step-by-step visual instructions for each task
Use standard ground-level garden beds and standard tools
Have cognitively impaired patients watch while others do the gardening
Eliminate gardening and substitute a less physically demanding activity
rationale: Inclusive group design modifies the activity and environment to accommodate diverse abilities. Raised beds eliminate the need for bending. Ergonomic tools with built-up handles reduce grip strength demands. Visual step-by-step instructions compensate for cognitive processing deficits. This combination allows all members to participate meaningfully. Standard equipment excludes members with physical limitations. Having some members watch is not participation. Eliminating the activity fails to adapt to meet the members where they are, which is a core OT principle.

stem: An OTA is performing a kitchen safety assessment with a patient who has left-sided homonymous hemianopsia following a stroke. The patient is preparing to make toast. Which safety concern is the HIGHEST priority?
options: Risk of burns from not seeing the toaster or hot surfaces on the left side of the counter
Difficulty reading the recipe for toast preparation
Inability to spread butter evenly on toast
Choosing the wrong type of bread from the pantry
rationale: Homonymous hemianopsia is loss of the same visual field (left) in both eyes. During kitchen tasks, the patient may not see hot surfaces, sharp objects, or appliances in the left visual field, creating a significant burn and injury risk. This is a safety-critical concern that requires immediate environmental adaptation (placing hot items on the right, using visual scanning strategies). Difficulty reading recipes, spreading butter, and selecting bread are functional concerns but do not carry the same level of injury risk.

stem: An OTA is treating a patient who has been placed on suicide precautions in an inpatient psychiatric unit. Which safety measure is MOST critical during an occupational therapy session?
options: Ensure all potentially dangerous materials (scissors, sharp tools, cords) are accounted for before, during, and after the session
Leave all art supplies accessible for the patient to encourage self-expression
Allow the patient to work independently in a private room during craft activities
Focus only on discharge planning since the patient will leave soon
rationale: Patients on suicide precautions require constant monitoring and restricted access to potential means of self-harm. In OT sessions, sharps (scissors, craft knives, needles), cords (macramé, leather lacing), and toxic substances (certain adhesives, paints) must be counted before and after sessions and kept under the OTA's direct control during use. Accessible supplies create opportunities for self-harm. Private rooms eliminate observation. Focusing only on discharge ignores the acute safety needs of the current situation.
